name: The Branching History
description: History is not a sequence of fixed eras. It's a tree. Each branch is a genuinely different world with its own name, tensions, factions, and trajectory. Two players who diverge at Era 1 should be playing in worlds that are unrecognizable to each other by Era 3.
design_principle: The era structure itself branches. Not just outcomes within fixed eras — the ERAS are different. Different names, different tensions, different lengths, different available factions and gods. The player doesn't walk through the same corridor choosing left or right. They walk into different buildings entirely.

root.name: The Foundation
root.tension: The world is small enough to know. Forge-people in the mountain. Scholars by the sea. River nomads who sing their maps. Volcano-tenders who talk to the earth. Delta traders who owe nobody loyalty. Five concept gods who have existed since before humans gave them names. And something growing in the Ashfield — a newborn consciousness made of accumulated fear, breathing plague, trying to understand itself through the only lens it has. The player-god wakes in this world without memory. Their first vessel is born. Every choice they make plants a seed they won't see bloom for centuries. The Foundation is the only era where everything is still possible. By the time it ends, the first choices will have eliminated most of those possibilities forever.

factions: the_forge_council (master smiths in Thornwall — isolationist, secretive, proud of their craft)
the_solmari_senate (southern scholars — knowledge as authority, expanding influence through trade offers)
the_rootwalker_network (river nomads — no permanent settlements, oral tradition, the River Tongue carries information)
cultures: iron_holds (northern — craft as identity, the mountain as home)
rootwalkers (everywhere and nowhere — movement as philosophy)
ash_tenders (the Ashfield margin — people who live near fear and learned to manage it)
the_brackish (Senn Delta — traders, survivors, loyal to nobody)
gods: the_deep (ocean, knowledge, patience — Tier 5 concept god)
the_burn (fire, craft, transformation — Tier 5 concept god)
the_drift (movement, change, rivers — Tier 5 concept god)
the_silence (quiet, secrets, absence — Tier 5 concept god)
the_weave (connection, trade, relationships — Tier 5 concept god)
the_ashfield_god (fear, plague, confusion — forming, Stage 1-2, not yet conscious)
historical_figures: harren (master smith — embodies the choice between tradition and opening)
senator_lyris (political survivor — embodies the choice between knowledge sharing and knowledge control)
vaya (rootwalker composer — wrote the Song of the Cracked Stone, embodies movement as answer)
ossa (ash tender elder — talks to the Ashfield, understands the fear-god is confused not malicious)
threats: the_ashfield_god (growing — the plague will get worse if unaddressed)
the_southern_offer (trade that could open or close Thornwall forever)
the_ash_cough (fear-plague spreading from the Ashfield — kills through the mind, not the body)

thread_beginnings.worship_economy: The five concept gods exist without needing worship. The Ashfield god is the first worship-dependent entity — born from collective fear. How mortals relate to this newborn god will set the pattern for every divine relationship that follows.
thread_beginnings.faction_power_balance: Three factions with three philosophies: craft (Iron Doctrine), knowledge (Consul's Proposition), movement (Song of the Cracked Stone). Which one the player empowers determines the shape of the second era. But the real power might be the ones nobody's watching: the Brackish in the delta, who owe nobody loyalty and understand the value of information.
thread_beginnings.player_god_identity: The player-god remembers nothing. Their first vessel is born into a world that doesn't know they exist. What the player chooses to care about — which vessel, which faction, which crisis — begins to define what kind of god they are. Domain crystallization starts here, in the first three lives, in the smallest choices.

states.name: The Age of Exchange
states.condition: Trade opened + plague managed + cultures blending
states.tension: Prosperity. But prosperity creates new predators. The merchant class rises. Old identities dissolve in the solvent of commerce. Who benefits? Who gets left behind?

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (reformed, trade-friendly)
the_solmari_senate (ascendant)
merchant_guilds (new)
active_gods: the_weave (strengthened by connection)
a commerce god (forming)
threats: wealth_concentration
cultural_erosion
ashfield_god (managed but growing)
missing: the_true_forge (exiled)
rootwalker traditions (fading)

states.feels_like: Renaissance Florence. Trade makes everything possible. Patronage funds art and war equally. Everyone is getting richer and nobody is getting safer.

key_figures.maven_sol: Consortium founder's daughter. Thirty-two. She grew up watching her mother build the trade network from a single delta warehouse to the continent's nervous system. Maven inherited the network and the question it carries: when does connecting people become controlling them? She runs the Consortium's fairness board — the body that sets prices and resolves disputes. She's good at it. She's also aware that 'setting prices' is two words away from 'setting the world.' The Scale (commerce god) responds to her judgments with a warmth that should be comforting and isn't. A god approving of your decisions means your decisions feed the god. She can't tell if she's serving fairness or feeding a divine appetite.
key_figures.old_harren_legacy: Harren is dead. But his forge remains, and his last apprentice — a woman named Deel — runs it as a Consortium-licensed workshop. She produces blades on contract. Excellent blades. The contracts specify dimensions, materials, delivery dates. The contracts don't specify why. Deel forges because the contract says forge. She's never made a blade for a person who asked for it. She's never seen the face of someone who carries her work. The Exchange turned craft from a relationship into a transaction, and Deel is too young to know the difference.
key_figures.the_first_bridge_child: A ten-year-old in the trade quarter who speaks both northern and southern fluently, who eats northern bread and southern fruit for the same breakfast, who has a forge-quarter grandfather and a Senate-quarter grandmother and finds the old rivalries genuinely confusing. She's the Compromise's proof of concept — a person who simply doesn't understand what the fighting was about. She's also the person both sides are most afraid of: living proof that the old identities can dissolve.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Consortium rising as a neutral power that's becoming the only power)
worship_economy (the Scale forming — a commerce god being born from accumulated belief in fair exchange)
thread_connections.sub_thread: maven_and_the_scale — a woman setting prices and a god approving. Resolution: the Scale grows honestly (→Golden Compromise) or the Scale is captured by the system (→Syndicate Age, Merchant Wars, Crash)
sub_sub_threads: deels_contract_forge (craft without relationship — the Exchange's cost)
the_bridge_child (the old identities dissolving — proof of concept or proof of loss?)

states.name: The Age of Iron
states.condition: Trade rejected + Iron Doctrine dominant + quarantine maintained
states.tension: Fortress mentality. Thornwall is impregnable and suffocating. The best weapons in the world guard borders nobody tries to cross anymore. Strength without purpose becomes its own prison.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (militarized)
the_true_forge (respected exiles)
the_wall_keepers (new)
active_gods: the_burn (fed by forge-worship)
the_silence (growing in isolation)
threats: internal_fracture
ashfield_god (unaddressed, Stage 3)
cultural_stagnation
missing: southern knowledge
rootwalker trade routes
mixed cultures

states.feels_like: Cold War-era fortress state. Sparta. The walls are high and the people inside them have forgotten what the outside looks like. Strength as identity. Isolation as ideology.

key_figures.forge_warden_sev: Thirty-eight. Commands the wall-keepers — the soldiers who maintain Thornwall's perimeter. He's never fought anyone. The walls haven't been tested in his lifetime. He maintains readiness for a threat that exists in doctrine but not in memory. Every morning he walks the wall and looks out at the empty passes and the trade road he's never walked and wonders if the wall is protecting or containing. He won't say this aloud because the Iron Doctrine doesn't have a word for the feeling of guarding something from nothing.
key_figures.the_daughter_of_harren: Meren. Harren's granddaughter. She inherited his forge but not his certainty. She forges beautifully — the bloodline shows. But she's read things. A southern text made it through The Foot, a metallurgy paper that describes heat transfer in terms that make the sacred seventh fold sound like physics. She keeps the paper hidden under her anvil. She hasn't changed her technique. She's just... aware now. Awareness, in Thornwall, is the first crack.
key_figures.the_foot_dweller: Unnamed — the people of The Foot don't use formal names with outsiders. A trader at the unofficial town below the mountain who sells information upward and goods downward. She's the membrane between Thornwall and the world. Neither side trusts her. Both sides need her. She considers herself the freest person on the continent: answerable to nobody, needed by everyone, standing at the exact point where isolation meets the world.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (total internal control, zero external influence — the fortress as identity)
worship_economy (the Burn sustained by a closed system — the most focused, most confined divine force)
thread_connections.sub_thread: merens_paper — Harren's granddaughter with a southern text under her anvil. Resolution: she stays silent (→Long Siege), she shares it (→Silent Revolution), she leaves (→Walls Crack)
sub_sub_threads: sevs_empty_passes (guarding against nothing)
the_foot_membrane (the unofficial border between isolation and the world)

states.name: The Age of Questions
states.condition: Unworship discovered + Ashfield investigated + philosophy thriving
states.tension: The most dangerous discovery in history: worship can be redirected. Gods can be made and unmade by belief. Every faction wants to control this. The gods themselves are afraid. Reality is negotiable.

dominant_factions: the_unworshippers (radical)
the_solmari_senate (studying it)
divine_conservators (new — protecting gods)
active_gods: the_ashfield_god (understood but not resolved)
all five concept gods (threatened)
the_player_god (suddenly very interesting to everyone)
threats: weaponized_belief
divine_panic
theological_civil_war
missing: certainty
stable worship
any god who trusts mortals

states.feels_like: The Reformation meets the Manhattan Project. Someone discovered how to split the atom of faith. The genie is out of the bottle. Every faction is racing to control what can't be controlled.

key_figures.ossa_vindicated: Ossa, the Ash Tender elder, is now the most important person on the continent. She was right — the Ashfield god is a newborn consciousness, confused not malicious, shapeable through belief. She spent decades being called an eccentric. Now the Senate, the Unworshippers, and the divine conservators all want her knowledge. She's eighty and she's exhausted and she's watching her life's work be simultaneously vindicated and co-opted. The Unworshippers want to use her understanding to diminish gods. The Senate wants to use it to control them. Ossa just wanted to talk to the Ashfield. Now everyone wants to weaponize the conversation.
key_figures.the_first_unworshipper: Kavan. Young. Brilliant. Arrogant. He was the first to successfully apply unworship — redirecting belief away from a minor harvest spirit that was demanding too many offerings. The spirit faded. The offerings stopped. The fields still grew. Kavan published the results and the world changed. He considers himself a liberator. He's also, without knowing it, the person who will eventually make the Silence Falls possible. Every technique has a first successful user. Kavan is the Oppenheimer of faith.
key_figures.the_frightened_god: Not a person — the Weave, experiencing fear for the first time. The concept god of connection has always existed because connection exists. But now mortals know they can redirect belief, and the Weave — for the first time in its ancient existence — understands that it could be diminished. A concept god afraid of an idea. The irony is not lost on it. The fear makes the Weave cling to its worshippers, which makes it needier, which makes it more vulnerable to the exact technique it fears.

main_threads: worship_economy (the discovery that belief shapes gods — the most dangerous knowledge in the world)
faction_power_balance (every faction racing to control unworship/directed worship)
thread_connections.sub_thread: kavans_technique — the first successful unworship. Resolution: used carefully (→New Covenant), weaponized (→God War), overused (→Silence Falls), or the gods respond (→Divine Reckoning)
sub_sub_threads: ossas_co-option (a lifetime of understanding turned into a weapon by people who don't understand what they're wielding)
the_weaves_fear (a concept god discovering vulnerability — clinging makes it weaker)

states.name: The Age of Wandering
states.condition: Song of the Cracked Stone dominant + Rootwalker network + plague treated through movement
states.tension: A generation left the settled world. The Song spread. Young people walk the rivers instead of inheriting forges. The network is everywhere and nowhere. Nobody rules because nobody stays.

dominant_factions: the_river_tongue (reluctant power)
settled_rootwalkers (new)
the_walking_generation (movement)
active_gods: the_drift (massively empowered)
the_deep (weakened — fewer people near the coast)
threats: infrastructure_decay (nobody maintains roads)
rootwalker_identity_crisis
settled_resentment
missing: stable institutions
standing armies
anyone who stays long enough to govern

states.feels_like: The Mongol trade routes without the Mongols. Everything moves. Information flows. Culture blends. But nothing stays long enough to become an institution, and institutions are what survive crises.

key_figures.vaya_remembered: Vaya is dead but her Song lives in a hundred mouths. The Song of the Cracked Stone has become the Walking Generation's anthem. The problem: Vaya never wrote it down. Twelve students learned it. Each taught it differently. The Song that was supposed to unite is already beginning to diverge. In the Age of Wandering, the Song is one song. By the next era, it might be a hundred.
key_figures.young_walker_denn: Seventeen. Left his father's farm to walk the rivers because the Song told him the river teaches everything. He's been walking for eight months. He's learned: how to read weather, how to find clean water, how to sleep in rain. He hasn't learned: how to treat a wound, how to negotiate with a stranger, how to be still when still is what the moment needs. The Walking Generation is full of Denns — young, passionate, underprepared. The river teaches everything, but it teaches slowly, and some lessons arrive as injuries.
key_figures.the_drift_awake: The Drift has never been more awake. A concept god of movement, fed by an entire generation choosing to move. The rivers run clearer. The paths feel easier. The weather cooperates with travelers in ways that aren't coincidence. The Drift is happy — and a happy god is a powerful god, and a powerful god reshapes the world in its image, and a world reshaped for movement is a world where staying becomes harder. The fields dry up slightly. The houses feel less comfortable. The Drift isn't malicious. It's enthusiastic. Enthusiasm in a god is terrifying.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (nobody rules because nobody stays — governance impossible without permanence)
worship_economy (the Drift massively empowered — a movement god reshaping the world for movement)
thread_connections.sub_thread: the_songs_divergence — Vaya's Song already splitting. Twelve students, twelve versions. The unity seed carrying the fragmentation seed. Resolution: the Song holds (→Great Meeting), the Song fractures (→Song Breaks), or movement itself becomes contested (→Road Wars)
sub_sub_threads: denns_education (the young walker learning that the river teaches slowly)
the_drifts_enthusiasm (a happy god that makes staying harder — divine reshaping through joy, not wrath)

states.name: The Slow Rot
states.condition: Default outcomes across most axes + low player engagement
states.tension: Nothing was solved. Everything compounds. The plague is endemic. Trade is resentful. Philosophy is tribal. Not apocalypse — mundanity. The world that forgot it could be different.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (diminished)
the_solmari_senate (bureaucratic)
local_strongmen (new)
active_gods: the_ashfield_god (Stage 3-4, background radiation)
the_silence (thriving in apathy)
threats: chronic_decline
institutional_decay
the_ashfield_god (now too large to ignore)
missing: hope
anyone who remembers what ambition felt like

states.feels_like: Late Roman Empire. The structures still stand but nobody believes in them. The roads exist but nobody repairs them. The Senate meets but nobody listens. The most dangerous thing in the world is the shrug.

key_figures.the_last_senator: Prenn. Sixty-one. Attends Senate sessions alone some weeks. The Senate was built for forty members. Twelve seats are filled. Eight of those attend rarely. Prenn comes every session because the alternative is admitting the institution is dead. She keeps minutes for meetings that make no decisions. She writes proposals nobody reads. She is the Rot embodied: a person performing the motions of a functioning system because the performance is the last thread connecting the present to a past that worked.
key_figures.ash_cough_child: A boy in the delta district who coughs ash every morning. He's nine. He's never known a morning without the cough. His parents treat it with herbs that don't work because the healer's guild can't fund the real treatment because the trade network that funded the guild collapsed. The boy doesn't know his cough is a symptom of a god. He doesn't know the Ashfield god exists. He knows: he coughs, the herbs taste bad, his mother looks worried. The Rot reduced a divine crisis to a child's bad morning.
key_figures.the_shrug_itself: Not a person — a cultural posture. The Shrug is the Slow Rot's defining gesture. When the trade road needs repair: shrug. When the Ash Cough worsens: shrug. When the Senate proposes a measure: shrug. The Shrug isn't cruelty or ignorance. It's the accumulated weight of a hundred problems that each required more energy than anyone had left. The Shrug is rational. The Shrug is also the thing that will kill the world, one unraised shoulder at a time.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (all factions diminished — nobody powerful enough to lead, nobody invested enough to try)
worship_economy (the Silence thriving in apathy — the Ashfield god now just weather)
thread_connections.sub_thread: prenns_minutes — a senator writing minutes for an empty room. Resolution: someone reads them (→Awakening or Desperate Gambit), nobody does (→Final Decline), or something from outside makes them irrelevant (→Outside Arrives, Wake-Up Call)
sub_sub_threads: the_cough_child (a divine crisis reduced to a bad morning)
the_shrug (the gesture that kills the world — rational, understandable, fatal)

states.name: The Merchant Wars
states.condition: Wealth concentration unchecked + merchant guilds vs old factions
states.tension: Dex adjusted the price of southern medicine by twenty percent during a negotiation dispute with the forge quarter. The forge quarter couldn't afford the new price. They held out for three weeks. Fourteen people died — not from violence, from delayed treatment. Dex considers it a successful negotiation. The price was adjusted. The trade resumed. In Dex's world, the fourteen deaths are market friction. In the forge quarter, they're murder. Brennan, the forge quarter's trade negotiator, retaliated by cutting ore shipments to the southern cities. Two construction workers died when a scaffold collapsed from inferior materials. Brennan considers this proportional. The weapons are identical. Neither side thinks they're the aggressor. That's the Merchant Wars — mutual escalation through commerce. Nobody swings a sword. Everybody dies. The proxy armies come later, when physical force is needed to protect trade routes or enforce blockades. But the war was economic before it was military, and economic violence is harder to condemn because it doesn't look like violence until you count the bodies.
states.feels_like: The East India Company era meets the Opium Wars. Corporate warfare where the weapon is a price and the battlefield is a ledger. The moral question: if you change a tariff and people starve, is that war? The answer is yes and nobody admits it.

dominant_factions: merchant_coalitions (new — wealthy traders who've outgrown the Consortium framework)
the_forge_council (militarizing — craft becoming defense)
the_solmari_senate (buying influence — wealth as political tool)
active_gods: the_scale (in crisis — trying to enforce fairness when both sides weaponize the concept of fairness)
the_burn (growing — commercial conflict feeds the fire as surely as military conflict)
the_weave (straining — connections being used as weapons)
threats: economic_violence_normalized (people dying from price changes, and nobody calling it killing)
proxy_armies (when commercial pressure isn't enough, buy soldiers)
the_scale_breaks (a fairness god in a world where fairness is a weapon)

key_figures.dex: Consortium broker. Orchestrated the Southern Blockade — cut off medicine shipments during a price dispute. Fourteen died. Dex's defense: 'I made an offer. They rejected it. I didn't force anyone. Their leaders chose pride over their people's health. Blame the people who refused to negotiate, not the person who gave them the option.' The logic is traceable. The conclusion is monstrous. Dex doesn't see a contradiction because in Dex's framework, commerce isn't violence — it's choice.
key_figures.brennan: Forge quarter trade negotiator. Retaliated against Dex's blockade by cutting ore shipments. Two southern workers died from a scaffold collapse. Brennan's defense mirrors Dex's exactly: proportional response, their choice, their consequences. The Merchant Wars' horror: both sides use identical logic to justify identical violence and both are right by their own framework.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (wealth replacing military power)
worship_economy (the Scale struggling to define fairness in a trade war)
thread_connections.sub_thread: the_blockade_cycle — Dex and Brennan's mutual escalation. Resolution: either regulated (→Regulated Peace), absorbed into corporate power (→Corporate Dominion or Syndicate Age), or overthrown (→People's Revolt)
sub_sub_threads: the_scale_crisis (a fairness god in an unfair war)
proxy_armies (when commerce isn't enough — the transition from economic to military violence)

states.name: The Golden Compromise
states.condition: Trade benefits shared + cultural bridges held + threats managed collectively
states.tension: Torren negotiated the Compromise for the Forge Council. He agreed to open the forges to limited southern observation. Three non-sacred techniques shared. The trade road through the mountain pass. In exchange: medicine for the Ash Cough, access to southern scholarship, guaranteed ore prices. The deal was fair. Torren knows it was fair. But fair doesn't mean equal sacrifice. The Senate gave things they could replace — money, medicine, knowledge that could be recreated. The Forge gave things they can never get back — exclusivity, secrecy, the identity that came from being the only people in the world who knew how to work the deep ore. Renn, the Senate's lead negotiator, sees the Compromise as a triumph. She facilitates the cultural exchanges, works the Joint Council, believes in the future they're building. She genuinely doesn't understand Torren's resentment because she can't see non-material losses as equivalent to material ones. Medicine can be weighed. What does identity weigh? The Golden Compromise works. The trade flows. The cultures blend. The Bridge Folk children speak both languages. Torren attends the joint festivals. He smiles at the Bridge Folk children. He keeps score.
states.feels_like: The EU at its best — genuine cooperation built on genuine concession. One side gave things that can be counted and replaced. The other side gave things that can't. The cracks are there. They look like smiles that don't reach the eyes and festival toasts that taste like ash.

dominant_factions: the_joint_council (newly formed — Forge + Senate + Bridge Folk mediators)
the_forge_council (cooperating but keeping score)
the_solmari_senate (cooperating and genuinely pleased)
new_cultures: bridge_folk (mainstream now — the Compromise's children, bilingual, politically essential)
the_holdouts (northern craftsmen who consider the open forges a betrayal — small, declining, angry)
active_gods: the_weave (strongest it's been — genuine connection feeding it)
the_scale (stable — enforcing fair trade terms willingly agreed upon)
the_burn (uneasy — the forge identity is changing, and the Burn changes with it)
threats: asymmetric_sacrifice (one side gave more and both sides know it but only one side admits it)
cultural_erosion_disguised_as_blending (the northern traditions dissolving into the exchange culture)
the_holdouts (small now — dangerous if they find a cause)

key_figures.torren: Forge Council elder. Negotiated the Compromise. Fair deal, unequal sacrifice. The Senate gave medicine and money — replaceable. The Forge gave techniques and access — irreversible. Torren attends every Joint Council meeting. He votes for cooperation. He means it. He also keeps a mental ledger of what was given up: the three techniques (now taught in southern universities), the mountain pass (now a trade highway), the forge quarter (now half-populated by southern scholars). He doesn't talk about the ledger. He doesn't need to. His silence IS the score. The question the Golden Compromise can't answer: how do you compensate for an identity that was traded for prosperity?
key_figures.renn: Senate negotiator. Lyris's protégé. Genuinely believes the Compromise is the world's greatest achievement — and she's not wrong. The trade flows, the plague is managed, the cultures are blending. She works the Joint Council with skill and real warmth. Her blind spot: she measures sacrifice in material terms. Medicine given. Gold spent. Techniques shared. She can't see Torren's loss because it has no weight, no volume, no price tag. 'What did you lose?' she'd ask, and mean it. Torren would point at everything and say 'this' and she'd think he meant 'nothing.'

main_threads: faction_power_balance (cooperation maintained by asymmetric sacrifice — stable but scored)
worship_economy (the Weave thriving on real connection — the Burn uneasy about the changing forge identity)
thread_connections.sub_thread: torrens_score — the elder who cooperates and keeps count. Resolution: if the score stays silent → Long Peace or Early Peace. If the score becomes a grievance → Old Wounds. If someone exploits the score → The Betrayal.
sub_sub_threads: the_holdouts (northern purists — small now, dangerous if activated)
bridge_folk_identity (the Compromise's children — do they belong to both sides or neither?)

states.name: The Iron Crusade
states.condition: Fortress mentality + external threat forces expansion
states.tension: Ivar is the first Forge Commander to march Thornwall's army past the walls. He doesn't want to. He's a smith, not a general — but the Ashfield god's expansion is threatening the eastern passes, and the fortress that was built to endure can't endure something that seeps through stone. The Council's vote was unanimous: if the walls can't hold, take the fight to the source. Ivar marches with the finest-equipped army in the world. Every blade forged in Thornwall. Every shield stamped with the mountain sigil. They genuinely believe they're saving the world. Sera, a southern scholar conscripted as a translator, watches them march through the first town they 'liberate' — a trading post that didn't ask to be liberated. The townspeople look at Thornwall's army the way you look at a storm: something enormous and impersonal that will change everything and doesn't care what you think about it. Sera translates Ivar's speech: 'We bring safety.' The townspeople hear: 'We bring ourselves.' The Crusade's tragedy is that Ivar means every word. He genuinely wants to protect. But protection imposed is occupation in a different uniform, and the army that was built to defend Thornwall doesn't know how to defend a people it doesn't understand.
states.feels_like: The First Crusade. A fortress culture that turns its defensive power outward with genuine conviction and no self-awareness. The soldiers believe they're saving the world. The world they're saving doesn't agree. The destruction isn't intentional — it's the natural consequence of applying fortress logic to an unfamiliar landscape.

dominant_factions: the_forge_army (Thornwall's military — finest equipment in the world, zero cultural awareness)
occupied_towns (trading posts, border communities — didn't ask for liberation)
the_true_forge (deep-mountain exiles watching their culture march past them)
active_gods: the_burn (war-fed — the forge-fire becoming a war-fire, transforming with the army's purpose)
the_ashfield_god (the reason for the Crusade — but it doesn't fight armies, it seeps through cracks)
the_silence (growing in the occupied territories — people going quiet under foreign soldiers)
threats: mission_creep (the Crusade was supposed to stop the Ashfield god — it's now conquering towns that have nothing to do with the Ashfield)
the_burn_transforming (the forge-fire becoming something the smiths don't recognize — war changes gods)
cultural_incomprehension (Thornwall doesn't understand the people it's occupying — and doesn't think it needs to)

key_figures.ivar: Forge Commander. A smith who became a general because the Council needed someone who understood how the army's equipment worked. He's methodical, careful, principled. He treats every town his army occupies the way he'd treat a forge project: identify the problem, apply the solution, move to the next. He can't see that people aren't metal. You can't solve a culture by hammering it. His soldiers look at him with devotion. The occupied people look at him with the specific fear reserved for someone who's trying to help you in a way you didn't ask for.
key_figures.sera: Southern scholar. Conscripted as a translator because she speaks the border dialects. She translates Ivar's speeches and watches the townspeople's faces and knows that 'we bring safety' is being heard as 'we bring ourselves.' She tells Ivar this. He listens, nods, and doesn't change the speech because from his perspective it's accurate. Sera is the Crusade's conscience — the person who sees the gap between intention and impact and can't close it because the army doesn't think the gap exists.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge becomes an imperial force — defense transformed into conquest)
worship_economy (the Burn transforming from craft-fire to war-fire — the god changes when its people change)
thread_connections.sub_thread: ivar_and_sera — the commander who means well and the translator who sees the gap. Resolution: either Ivar learns to listen (→Converted Enemy), or the Crusade overextends (→Crusade Breaks), or the conquest succeeds and Ivar becomes something Thornwall never intended (→Iron Empire)
sub_sub_threads: the_burn_transformation (a craft-god becoming a war-god — what does that mean for the forge identity?)
occupied_towns (people who didn't ask for liberation — their silence is its own thread)

states.name: The Walls Crack
states.condition: Internal fracture + isolation unsustainable
states.tension: Nol didn't break the wall. She found a crack — a section of the quarantine barrier between the forge quarter and the lower town where the mortar had gone soft from decades of neglect, because the plague the wall was built for ended thirty years ago and nobody bothered to repair something nobody remembered the purpose of. Nol was seventeen. She was carrying her tools — an apprentice set, nothing sacred, the kind they give you before you've earned the real ones. She squeezed through the gap at dawn. She walked down the mountain for the first time in her life. She saw the southern trade road. She saw the sky without walls around it. She didn't come back. The Forge Council sent someone after her. The person they sent also didn't come back. The crack in the wall was physical. The crack in Thornwall was generational — the young had been raised behind walls built for a threat that no longer existed, taught the Iron Doctrine by masters who believed it and couldn't explain it to students who needed reasons, not tradition. The walls kept the plague out. Then the walls kept the southern ideas out. Then the walls kept the young people in. Now the walls are cracking because nobody maintains a prison they don't admit is a prison.
states.feels_like: The fall of the Berlin Wall. Not a dramatic breach — a slow leak. One person at a time, squeezing through the gap that nobody repairs because nobody will admit the wall has become a cage. The inside pressure exceeds the outside threat. When the walls crack, everything compressed behind them rushes out.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (losing control — can't seal the cracks without admitting the walls are prisons)
the_leavers (young smiths going through the gap, one at a time, carrying techniques with them)
the_keepers (old masters who genuinely believe isolation protects the craft — and they're not entirely wrong)
active_gods: the_burn (confused — the forge-faith is splitting between those who stay and those who leave)
the_silence (filling the empty workshops as young smiths leave)
the_drift (felt for the first time inside Thornwall — the leavers carry the sense of movement)
threats: brain_drain (the best young smiths leave first because the best can survive anywhere)
identity_collapse (if anyone can learn the techniques, what makes Thornwall special?)
the_masters_grief (watching your apprentice walk away carrying everything you taught them to a place that doesn't understand what they have)
missing: a reason to stay (the plague is over, the trade offers are better outside, the world is wider than the mountain)
anyone under thirty who believes the walls serve a purpose
the_repairs (nobody maintains the wall because nobody believes in it — the decay is the statement)

key_figures.nol: Seventeen. Apprentice smith. The first person through the crack — not because she was rebellious, but because she was curious. She heard about the southern trade road from a merchant who shouted up from the base of the mountain. She wanted to see it. She squeezed through a gap in a wall built for a plague that ended before she was born. She didn't intend to leave forever. She intended to look. The looking was enough. The sky without walls around it was enough. She sent a message back to her workshop-mate: 'There are trees down here.' Three more apprentices left that week.
key_figures.master_hael: Sixty-one. Forge master. Nol's teacher. He taught her everything — the grip, the heat-reading, the way to fold iron seven times for a blade that sings. He taught her because that's what masters do. He didn't teach her to leave. When Nol went through the wall, Hael went to the crack and stood there for an hour. He didn't follow. He didn't seal it. He stood in the gap between the world he built and the world his student chose and he couldn't move in either direction. He's still there, metaphorically. He attends Council meetings where they debate sealing the cracks. He votes no. He can't explain why. The explanation is: you can't seal a crack your student walked through without saying your student was wrong to walk, and he taught her to be curious, and curiosity is what walked.
key_figures.the_council_hardliner: Name: Forge Elder Darren. He wants the cracks sealed. Not because he doesn't understand why the young are leaving — he understands exactly. He left once, in his twenties, spent a year in the south, came back because the forge called him home. He knows the pull. He knows some people don't come back. He wants to seal the cracks because every smith who leaves carries techniques that took centuries to develop, and once they're out, they're gone. Not lost — dispersed. Diluted. Applied to things the forge never intended. He's not wrong. The techniques are irreplaceable. The question is whether you can keep knowledge alive by imprisoning the people who carry it.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge fracturing from within — the isolationist identity cracking)
worship_economy (the Burn splitting between the staying forge and the leaving smiths — a god dividing)
thread_connections.sub_thread: nols_departure — a seventeen-year-old walking through a gap and not coming back. Resolution: either the leavers scatter and the techniques disperse (→Diaspora/Northern Diaspora), or the staying masters reform to make staying worth it (→Reformation), or the fracture becomes violent (→Forge War/Civil War)
sub_sub_threads: haels_gap (a teacher who can't condemn what he taught)
darrens_dilemma (keeping knowledge alive by imprisoning its carriers)
the_unrepaired_wall (the physical decay as political statement)

states.name: The Divine Reckoning
states.condition: Gods unable to ignore the unworship threat
states.tension: The gods chose champions. Not to fight — to argue. Each champion IS their god's case for existing. The Burn chose Oreth, the greatest living smith, and told him: stop forging. Put down the hammer. Let the world see what it loses when the fire goes out. Oreth's strike isn't a weapon — it's a demonstration. Blades dull and nobody makes new ones. Kilns cool and clay sits unformed. The Burn's argument is simple: try living without me. The Weave chose Ellam, a Bridge Folk mediator, and gave her a different task: negotiate. Not fight, not strike, not demonstrate — talk. Find the words that let gods and mortals share a world without one side kneeling. Ellam's task is impossible because negotiation requires both sides to have something to offer, and the mortals have unworship (the power to kill gods) while the gods have services (weather, craft, agriculture, connection) that nobody realizes are services until they're withdrawn. The Reckoning isn't Ragnarok — it's a labor dispute between gods and mortals where both sides hold weapons they don't want to use. The gods can withdraw their services. The mortals can withdraw their belief. Both threats are devastating. Neither side can afford to go first.
states.feels_like: A divine labor strike meets the Cuban Missile Crisis. Ragnarok as a political crisis. Every god choosing a mortal who embodies their argument — not their power, their ARGUMENT. The question isn't who's stronger. It's who's more necessary. And the answer might be: both. Or neither.

dominant_factions: divine_champions (mortals chosen by gods to represent them — not warriors, advocates)
the_unworshippers (holding the ultimate threat — the ability to kill gods through disbelief)
the_divine_conservators (mortals who chose to protect the gods — not from devotion, from pragmatism)
active_gods: the_burn (on strike — withdrawing its fire to prove its value. Forges cooling. The argument: try living without me.)
the_weave (choosing negotiation over demonstration — the only god that thinks talking might work)
the_deep (retreating — pulling its knowledge inward. Scholars finding their insights drying up. The deep ocean going quiet.)
the_silence (growing — as always, thriving in the gaps between the louder gods)
the_player_god (uniquely positioned — a god rebuilt by a mortal, the only one who knows what it's like to be forgotten and to return)
threats: the_mutual_threat (both sides can destroy the other — the mortals through unworship, the gods through withdrawal. The standoff is stable only as long as nobody blinks)
the_burns_strike (every day without fire is a day closer to collapse — the Burn is proving its point and the point is killing people)
champion_pressure (the mortals chosen as champions didn't ask for this — they carry their god's survival on their shoulders)

key_figures.oreth: The Burn's champion. Greatest living smith. Fifty-six. His hands have made ten thousand blades. The Burn chose him because he IS the argument — his craft is the Burn's gift to humanity, and his refusal to work is the Burn's threat. Oreth put down his hammer on a Tuesday morning. He hasn't picked it up since. His apprentices are confused. His clients are furious. His forge is cold and the cold is spreading — not supernaturally, just the practical reality of the world's best smith refusing to work. Oreth doesn't enjoy the strike. He loves forging. The hammer is the thing that makes him himself. But the Burn asked him, and the Burn has been in his fire since he was twelve, and when your god says 'show them what they'll lose,' you show them.
key_figures.ellam: The Weave's champion. Bridge Folk mediator. Thirty-eight. Has spent her life between cultures — translating, connecting, finding the words that let people hear each other. The Weave chose her because she already embodies what it represents: connection. Her task is to negotiate a new relationship between gods and mortals. She's the only champion whose job isn't to threaten but to talk. She's also the one most likely to fail, because negotiation requires good faith from both sides and the Reckoning has eroded good faith to its foundations. Ellam sits in rooms with divine champions who are demonstrating their gods' necessity through withdrawal, and she tries to find a framework where withdrawal becomes partnership. It's the hardest diplomatic challenge in history. She might succeed. That's the thread.

main_threads: worship_economy (the divine labor dispute — gods and mortals negotiating the terms of their relationship for the first time)
faction_power_balance (the champions as faction representatives — whoever's champion succeeds shapes the new world)
thread_connections.sub_thread: ellam_negotiation — the Bridge Folk mediator trying to find words for a new divine-mortal relationship. Resolution: she succeeds (→New Pantheon, negotiated relationship), or the withdrawal goes too far (→Godless Age, the mortals decide they'd rather go without), or both sides settle for less (→Divine Compromise, the unhappy marriage)
sub_sub_threads: oreths_strike (the smith who put down the hammer — proving the fire's value by its absence)
the_player_god_bridge (the player-god uniquely positioned — a god rebuilt by a mortal, the bridge between the two sides)

states.name: The Great Meeting
states.condition: Network reaches critical mass + need for collective decision
states.tension: Brin called the Meeting. She didn't have the authority — nobody does, that's the problem. She sent runners to every camp on the northern network with the same message: 'The Senate is building toll stations on the rivers. If we don't decide together how to respond, they'll pick us off camp by camp. Senn Ford. First thaw. Be there.' Forty-three camps came. Some sent elders. Some sent everyone. Some sent nobody but a runner with a message: 'We don't do meetings.' They're right. Rootwalkers have never decided anything collectively. Decisions are made by walking — you go where you agree and leave where you don't. There's no mechanism for a vote because voting requires staying in one place long enough to count. There's no mechanism for a chair because chairing requires authority and authority requires hierarchy and hierarchy is what the Song of the Cracked Stone was written to reject. Brin is standing in front of forty-three camps at Senn Ford trying to invent governance in real time. She's a route-keeper, not a politician. She knows rivers, not rhetoric. She proposed the only structure she understands: 'We walk together. When we reach a fork, we talk. If we can't agree, the camps that disagree walk different paths. The paths that work, we follow. The paths that don't, we learn from.' The river-fork model. Governance as navigation. It's brilliant or insane and nobody can tell which because nobody's ever tried.
states.feels_like: The Constitutional Convention meets the Haudenosaunee Great Law of Peace. People who defined themselves by rejecting structure are building one from scratch, using the only metaphor they have: the river. Every rule they write threatens what they are. Every rule they don't write leaves them vulnerable. The Meeting is the moment the Rootwalkers decide whether they're a people or just people who happen to walk the same rivers.

dominant_factions: the_meeting_camps (forty-three camps who showed up — the beginning of collective identity)
the_refusers (camps that sent 'we don't do meetings' — principle or cowardice, depending who you ask)
the_settled_observers (Senate, Forge remnants — watching to see if the walkers can organize, hoping they can't)
active_gods: the_drift (intensely present — a god of movement watching its people try to be still long enough to decide. The river at Senn Ford is unusually calm, as if the Drift is holding its breath.)
the_weave (drawn to the Meeting — the first genuine attempt at connection across the whole network)
threats: the_meeting_failing (if forty-three camps can't agree, the network fragments — and the Senate is waiting)
governance_paradox (every structure the Meeting builds contradicts the Rootwalkers' identity as structureless people)
the_song_question (which version of the Song opens the Meeting? The question hasn't been asked yet. When it is, it might end the Meeting before it begins.)

key_figures.brin: Route-keeper who called the Meeting. Thirty-nine. She didn't call it because she wanted power — she called it because she saw the Senate toll stations and realized that individual camps can't resist a state. She's standing in front of forty-three camps inventing governance from river metaphors. The river-fork model: walk together, talk at forks, diverge when you must, learn from the paths that fail. She's not a natural leader. She stutters when she's nervous. She repeats herself. She once stopped mid-sentence to point out that the ford was shifting. But she's the only person who tried, and trying was the thing nobody else did.
key_figures.camp_elder_vas: Eighty. Oldest person at the Meeting. He remembers when Rootwalkers didn't need to meet because nobody threatened the rivers. He came not because he believes in meetings but because he wants to see what his people do when they're afraid. He sits at the edge and watches. When Brin falters, Vas doesn't speak. He waits. His waiting is a vote of confidence that looks like indifference but isn't. He knows that if he speaks, his age will turn the Meeting into an audience, and an audience isn't governance.
key_figures.the_refuser_voice: A runner from the Three Bridges camp, carrying their elder's message: 'The river doesn't vote. It flows. We flow with it. When the Senate puts soldiers at the ford, we find another ford. That's what Rootwalkers do. Meetings are for people who've forgotten how to walk.' The message is arrogant and it's also the purest expression of Rootwalker philosophy at the table. Brin has to answer it. She hasn't figured out how.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Rootwalkers deciding whether to become a faction — the existential question)
worship_economy (the Drift watching its people try to be still — will the god of movement support governance?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: brins_invention — a route-keeper inventing governance from river metaphors. Resolution: the river-fork model works (→River Republic), the Meeting fails (→Great Scattering), or the Song question breaks it (→Song War)
sub_sub_threads: vas_watching (the elder whose silence is its own kind of speech)
the_refusers_challenge (the purest Rootwalker argument against the thing the Rootwalkers need)
the_song_opening (which version? the question that could end it all)

states.name: The Awakening
states.condition: Player finally engages deeply + catalyst event (possibly Fen's gambit, possibly the player-god's own vessel)
states.tension: Essa repaired the quarantine ward wall. That's it. That's what started it. Three days of work with reclaimed stone, mortar mixed from ash and river clay. The wall had been broken for nineteen years. Nobody fixed it because fixing requires energy and energy requires hope and hope requires believing things can be different and nobody in the Rot believes that. Essa fixed it because a child in her village was sick and the nearest healer was on the other side of the wall. The fix worked. The child was treated. People saw Essa's wall and thought: 'oh. you can just... do things.' Then Essa cleared a section of the trade road. Then someone else repaired a bridge. Then a third person reopened a well. The Awakening isn't a revolution. It's a cascade of small repairs by people remembering that broken things can be fixed. But the cascade has costs Essa doesn't see. The quarantine ward wall was broken because a woman named Breca was using the gap to smuggle medicine from outside the official channels — medicine that the remnant healer's guild couldn't provide because the supply lines had collapsed. Essa's wall blocked the smuggling route. Breca's patients died. The first act of awakening killed the invisible system that was keeping the worst of the Rot survivable. Hope is volatile. The act of trying is necessary AND the act of trying has consequences that the trying person doesn't see because they're too busy trying.
states.feels_like: February 1990, not 1994. The moment people realize it can change — before anyone has figured out what to change it into. Not a revolution but a cascade of small acts that collectively amount to one. The most hopeful era because it follows the most hopeless. The most volatile because hope from despair has no guardrails.

dominant_factions: the_fixers (Essa's people — small groups repairing infrastructure, reopening trade routes, doing things)
the_old_guard (remnant faction leaders who failed before — not resistant, just exhausted and afraid of hoping)
the_smugglers (Breca's network — people who kept the Rot survivable through unofficial channels, now disrupted by the fixing)
active_gods: the_player_god (if present — this is the first deeply engaged vessel in generations, and the world feels it)
the_ashfield_god (if Fen's gambit preceded this — the resurgent Cough is the crisis that forced the Awakening)
the_weave (stirring — connection being rebuilt for the first time in decades)
the_silence (receding — reluctantly, angrily, the way darkness recedes from a candle)
threats: hope_is_fragile (the Awakening is a cascade of small acts — any one could fail and break the momentum)
invisible_systems_disrupted (the Rot had its own survival infrastructure — the fixing destroys it before replacements exist)
the_old_guard_paralysis (the people with experience are too traumatized to lead, and the people with energy have no experience)
missing: a plan (the Awakening is momentum without direction)
trust (a generation of apathy has no muscle memory for cooperation)
forgiveness (the people who survived the Rot by doing nothing now have to work with the people who survived by doing the wrong things)

key_figures.essa: Twenty-six. Born in the Rot. Grew up in a village where the adults had stopped maintaining anything because maintaining requires hope. She's not a visionary — she's a person with a functional anger that turns into action. She can see that problems have solutions and the solutions are often physical: repair the wall, clear the road, reopen the well. Her blind spot is systemic. She fixes what she can see. She can't see the networks that existed in the gaps — the smuggling routes, the unofficial trade, the shadow systems that kept the Rot livable. Every wall she repairs closes a gap someone was using. She doesn't know this, and when she finds out, it will be the moment that determines whether the Awakening matures or fractures.
key_figures.breca: The smuggler. Forty-seven. Has been running medicine through the quarantine ward gap for eleven years. Not a criminal — a pragmatist. The official supply lines collapsed. The healer's guild can't get reagents. Breca found a source outside the system and built a route through the ruins. Essa's wall killed the route. Three of Breca's patients died in the two weeks before she found an alternative path. Breca doesn't hate Essa. She hates the idea that fixing is simple. She knows the Rot better than anyone because she's been surviving it actively, not just enduring it. When Essa comes to her — and Essa will, because the next repair will require knowledge only the smugglers have — Breca will say: 'You want to fix the world? Start by learning what's already working.'
key_figures.the_old_guard_elder: Name varies by which remnant faction they represent. They've seen trying before — or they've inherited the memory of the trying that failed. They watch Essa's cascade and feel something they haven't felt in years: fear. Not of failure. Of hope. Because hope is the thing that makes failure hurt. In the Rot, nothing hurts because nothing matters. The Awakening means things matter again. The elder isn't sure they can survive caring.

internal_divisions.the_fixers: Essa's cascade. Practical people doing practical repairs. Young, energetic, impatient. They see the Rot as a maintenance problem, not a spiritual one. They're right about the maintenance. They're wrong about it being just maintenance.
internal_divisions.the_smugglers_network: Breca's people. The shadow infrastructure that kept the Rot survivable. Disrupted by the fixing, resentful of being disrupted, but also grudgingly impressed that someone is trying. They have knowledge the fixers need — about supply routes, about what's actually available, about where the dangers are. The question: do they share it or protect it?
internal_divisions.the_old_guard: The remnant faction leaders. Experienced, traumatized, cautious. They could lead the Awakening but leadership requires hope and hope requires vulnerability and the Rot taught them that vulnerability kills. Some of them will rejoin. Some won't. The ones who don't aren't cowards — they're people who've been broken by the world's indifference and aren't sure they can survive it mattering again.
internal_divisions.the_still_rotting: People who didn't wake up. The Rot goes deep enough that some communities can't respond to the cascade. They're not resistant — they're past the point where momentum reaches them. The Awakening's hardest question: what do you do about the people the Awakening can't reach?

states.origin_wound: The Awakening's origin wound is the Rot itself. Everything that's broken was maintained once. Every collapsed institution was funded once. Every abandoned road was traveled once. The Awakening isn't building something new — it's remembering that the old things existed and deciding they're worth rebuilding. The wound is the gap between what was and what is, and the fact that the gap was created not by a catastrophe but by a series of decisions not to maintain, not to fund, not to care, not to try. Essa's wall is the first decision to try. It is also, accidentally, the first disruption to the shadow systems that learned to survive in the gap. The wound and the healing are happening simultaneously.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the remnant factions must decide whether to rejoin — cooperation requires trust they don't have)
worship_economy (the Weave stirs — connection being rebuilt — but the Silence resists)
player_god_identity (this is the player's most engaged era — what they choose to fix reveals what kind of god they're becoming)
thread_connections.sub_thread: essa_and_breca — the fixer and the smuggler. Resolution: they either learn from each other (Awakening matures → New Foundation), or the fixers destroy the shadow systems before replacements exist (Awakening fractures → False Dawn or Second Rot)
sub_sub_threads: old_guard_return (can traumatized leaders hope again?)
the_still_rotting (people the Awakening can't reach)
wall_cascade (each repair has visible benefits and invisible costs)

states.name: The Final Decline
states.condition: Continued low engagement + no catalyst
states.tension: Tam found a book in a ruin. He can't read it. Nobody in his village can read. He knows the marks mean something because they're too regular to be decoration, but the skill that would tell him what they mean has been lost for two generations. He uses the book to prop up a table leg. It's a history of the First Era — a complete account of the Founding, the trade negotiations, the Three Answers, the Ashfield god's birth. Everything Tam's world was built from. It holds up a table now. The Final Decline isn't tragedy. Tragedy requires awareness of what's being lost. The Final Decline is entropy — the slow forgetting of things nobody knows they've forgotten. The Ashfield god is weather now. People say 'the ash wind is bad today' the way they'd say 'it's raining.' Nobody remembers it was once a god, once a crisis, once a thing that could be addressed. The Senate building is a ruin nobody visits. The trade road is a line of flat stones in the grass that people walk on without knowing someone built it. The world doesn't end. It thins. Layer by layer, like paint flaking off a wall, revealing the blank surface underneath.
states.feels_like: The Dark Ages without the eventual Renaissance. Not tragedy — entropy. A book holding up a table leg. The saddest state because nobody caused it and nobody notices it and nobody will remember there was anything to notice.

dominant_factions: none (there are no factions — just villages that don't know each other exist)
active_gods: the_silence (dominant — not through power but through the absence of everything else)
the_ashfield_god (background weather — people don't remember it was ever a god)
the_player_god (if present — the only consciousness that remembers what this world was)
threats: forgetting (the loss of knowledge so complete that nobody knows it's been lost)
thinning (the world becoming simpler, sparser, less connected every generation)
the_book_under_the_table (the most complete history of the world, holding up furniture)
missing: literacy (gone for two generations in most settlements)
trade (why trade when you don't know anyone to trade with?)
the_concept_of_history (nobody tells stories about the past because the past sounds implausible)

key_figures.tam: Fourteen. Found a book in a ruin. Uses it to prop up a table. He'll never know what it says. He's not stupid — he's a competent farmer, a decent hunter, he knows his river and his soil. He's missing a skill that his great-grandmother had and that nobody thought to teach because the school closed and the school closed because the healer's guild couldn't fund it and the guild couldn't fund it because the trade network collapsed and the trade network collapsed because — but nobody knows any of this. Tam knows: his village, his field, his river. The world is the size of what he can walk to in a day. It used to be bigger. He doesn't know that.
key_figures.grandmother_ula: Seventy-eight. The oldest person in Tam's village. She can read — the last person who can. She learned from her mother, who was the village's last teacher. Ula doesn't tell anyone she can read because nobody asks and nobody cares and the skill has no practical value in a world without books. Except there IS a book — the one under Tam's table. If Ula saw it, she could read it. She could read the history of the world her grandson lives in without knowing. But Ula's eyes are failing and she lives on the other side of the village and Tam has never mentioned the book because a book that holds up a table isn't worth mentioning. The most important knowledge gap in the world is a hundred yards of footpath between a woman who can read and a boy who found something worth reading.
key_figures.the_player_god: If the player is still watching — and in the Final Decline, the player's continued attention is itself the last divine act — they can see everything Tam can't. They remember the Senate building when it was full. They remember the trade road when it carried goods. They remember the Ashfield god when it was a crisis. They're watching a world they shaped dissolve into simplicity, and they can't intervene because there's nobody to embody who would understand what the god is trying to tell them. The loneliest moment in the game: a god with perfect memory in a world without history.

states.origin_wound: Every institution that dissolved was funded once. Every road that disappeared was maintained once. Every skill that was lost was taught once. The Final Decline's wound is the accumulation of small decisions not to maintain, not to teach, not to fund — each one rational in its moment, collectively fatal across generations. Nobody decided to lose literacy. Nobody decided to forget the gods. The decline just happened, one unmade choice at a time, while nobody was paying attention.

main_threads: player_god_identity (the god with perfect memory in a world without history — the loneliest position in the game)
worship_economy (the divine ecosystem collapsed to a monoculture of Silence and background weather)
thread_connections.sub_thread: tam_and_ula — the boy who found a book and the grandmother who can read, a hundred yards apart and never connecting. Resolution: they connect (→Ember in Ash, the last knowledge is passed), or they don't (→endgame_default or endgame_fragmentation, the knowledge dies with Ula)
sub_sub_threads: the_book_under_the_table (the complete history of the world holding up furniture)
the_player_watching (a god who remembers everything in a world that remembers nothing)

states.name: The Corporate Dominion
states.condition: Merchant guilds win the wars + no faction strong enough to regulate them
states.tension: Ira is the richest person in the world and she's bored. Not the performative boredom of the wealthy — genuine, structural boredom. She owns three trade routes, two ports, a share in every forge contract, and a seat on the Consortium's inner board. She can buy anything. She has bought everything. The problem: buying was the game, and the game is over, and winning feels like an empty room. Across the delta, in a settlement the Consortium calls a 'labor district,' Pol repairs fishing nets for a wage that hasn't changed in twelve years. He doesn't hate Ira — he doesn't know she exists. His world is the net, the dock, the pay window, the walk home. His citizenship is a subscription he can't cancel because canceling means losing the dock access, and the dock is the only place that hires netmakers. He's not oppressed in any way he can name. He's just... owned. The world works perfectly for anyone who can pay and adequately for anyone who can work and not at all for anyone who can do neither. The perfection is the horror: nothing is broken, everything functions, and the system has optimized away every quality that isn't productive. Art is investment. Friendship is networking. The forge quarter produces blades on contract — excellent blades, no wasted motion, no ritual, no mystery. The Burn has been replaced by the Scale as the dominant god, and the Scale doesn't care about beauty, only balance.
states.feels_like: Cyberpunk without the tech. East India Company at peak power. A world that works and is therefore impossible to argue against — every critique sounds like ingratitude because the food arrives on time and the nets get repaired. The horror is the efficiency.

dominant_factions: the_trade_consortium (absolute — every faction is a subsidiary, every citizen a subscriber)
bought_forge_council (produces on contract — no autonomy, excellent output)
senate_as_consultants (advisors whose advice is taken when profitable and ignored when not)
active_gods: the_scale (fully formed, dominant — the commerce god has replaced the Burn as the primary divine force. Enforces contracts, ensures transactions, optimizes flow.)
the_weave (commodified — connection as a service. Relationships have a subscription tier.)
the_silence (in the labor districts — where the workers are quiet because the system doesn't require their voices)
threats: permanent_underclass (Pol's world — not starving, not free, not aware there's a difference)
meaning_crisis (Ira's world — everything buyable, nothing worth buying)
nothing_left_to_buy (the logical endpoint: when the market has consumed everything, what's the next product? The answer, historically, is people)

key_figures.ira: The richest person in the world. Forty-four. Inherited her first trade route from her mother, built the rest through competence and timing. She's not a villain — she's a winner who's discovered that winning was the interesting part and winning is over. She sits in her delta mansion and looks at the water and feels nothing. She's started buying art — not for investment, for the feeling. The feeling doesn't come. She knows the system she sits atop is perfect and empty and she can't articulate what's missing because the system has no word for what's missing. The word is 'meaning' and it's not a commodity.
key_figures.pol: Netmaker. Twenty-eight. Born in the labor district. His father was a netmaker. His grandfather was a fisherman who became a netmaker when the Consortium bought the fishing rights. Pol repairs nets for a wage that covers his subscription and leaves enough for a drink on rest days. He doesn't dream of revolution — he doesn't know the system he's inside has a name. It's just how things are. The nets need mending. The pay window opens at dawn. He's the Dominion's proof of concept: a person who functions perfectly inside the system and has no inner life the system can't service. Except — he sings while he works. Old songs, from before the Consortium. He doesn't know where they come from. He just sings. The singing is the thing the system didn't account for.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (total corporate control — every faction absorbed)
worship_economy (the Scale dominant — commerce as the primary divine force, meaning-crisis at the top)
thread_connections.sub_thread: ira_and_pol — the richest person bored by perfection, the poorest person singing in the nets. Resolution: the Ledger Closes (endgame — the system perfects itself into permanence)
sub_sub_threads: pols_singing (old songs in the labor district — the thing the system didn't account for)
iras_emptiness (meaning as the one thing that can't be bought — the crack in the perfection)

states.name: The People's Revolt
states.condition: Wealth inequality triggers mass uprising + player sides with the dispossessed
states.tension: Avan led the revolt. She was a dock worker — one of Naya's generation, raised after the Crash, old enough to remember when the credit notes meant something and young enough to have spent her adult life in the rebuild that mostly rebuilt the wealthy. She stood on the Consortium's trade platform and said 'no' and didn't move. Three hundred people stood behind her. They didn't have weapons. They had a word. The Consortium sent soldiers. The soldiers hesitated — most of them were dock workers' children. Three hundred became three thousand. The Consortium fled the delta by boat. The revolution won before anyone realized it was happening. Now Avan is in charge of a world she only knows how to break. She's brilliant at resistance — she can organize a blockade, inspire a crowd, stare down a soldier. She has no idea how to run a grain distribution system. The People's Council meets in the Consortium's old headquarters, sitting in chairs that still smell like rich people's leather, and tries to decide what to do with power they never planned to hold. The morning after the revolution: the barricades are down, the Consortium is gone, and nobody knows how to make the trade routes work without the people who used to own them.
states.feels_like: The French Revolution the morning after the Bastille. Righteous anger that was right about the problem and has no solution for the morning. The cruelest question in politics: now what?

dominant_factions: the_peoples_council (Avan's coalition — dock workers, former debt-born, Naya's barter network, everyone who was underneath. United by what they hate. Divided on everything else.)
remnant_guilds (underground — former Consortium traders hiding, waiting, some offering to 'help' the new government with the supply chains they once controlled)
the_justice_seekers (want the old Consortium leaders punished — vengeance as policy)
active_gods: the_burn (empowered by revolutionary fire — the forge-god was never supposed to be a revolution-god but fire is fire)
a_justice_god (forming from collective anger — newborn, confused, demanding punishment before anyone has written a law)
the_scale (broken and reforming — the old commerce god trying to define 'fair' in a world where the old fairness was the problem)
threats: revolutionary_terror (the justice god demanding punishment before law — vengeance feels righteous until it consumes the innocent)
the_competence_gap (Avan can lead a revolt. She can't run a grain shipment. The only people who know how are the people she overthrew.)
the_old_guard_offering_help (remnant Consortium traders saying 'let us run the supply chains — we know how.' Their competence is real. Their agenda is unclear.)

key_figures.avan: Dock worker turned revolutionary leader. Thirty-three. She grew up watching the Consortium's ships pass her mother's house — ships full of goods her mother couldn't afford, going to people her mother would never meet. She's charismatic, fearless, principled. She led the revolt with nothing but a word and three hundred bodies. She won. And winning is the worst thing that's happened to her because resistance requires only courage and governance requires everything she doesn't have: patience, compromise, expertise in systems she spent her life refusing to learn. She sits in the Consortium's old chair and she can feel the leather and she hates it and she needs it because there's no other chair.
key_figures.crel: Former Consortium supply chain manager. Forty-one. Not wealthy — middle management. He ran the grain distribution network for the delta. When the revolt happened, he hid. When the People's Council couldn't figure out the grain routes, they found him. He's now advising Avan on logistics. He's useful. He's also the person who ran the system that starved dock workers to maximize profit margins. He didn't design the system. He operated it. The question Avan faces every time she looks at Crel: can you build a new world with the people who built the old one? And: can you build it without them?
key_figures.the_justice_god: Forming from collective anger. Newborn — younger than the Ashfield god was when it first breathed plague. It manifests as a pressure: an urge toward punishment, toward accountability, toward making the guilty pay. It doesn't distinguish between the guilty and the convenient. It doesn't understand mercy because mercy wasn't in the anger that created it. The People's Revolt's darkest thread: the revolution birthed a god of justice that has no concept of fairness, because justice and fairness are not the same thing, and the people who made this god were too angry to know the difference.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (power seized without a plan — the competence gap)
worship_economy (a justice god forming from anger — born without the concept of mercy)
thread_connections.sub_thread: avan_and_crel — the revolutionary who needs the old system's operator. Resolution: they find a way to work together (→endgame_genuine_peace, the new world inherits the old world's competence without its injustice), or the justice god's demands consume the compromise (→endgame_mutual_destruction, vengeance eats the revolution)
sub_sub_threads: the_justice_god_growing (a newborn god of justice without mercy — the revolution's divine child)
the_leather_chair (Avan sitting in the Consortium's seat — what does power feel like when you know its history?)

states.name: The Long Peace
states.condition: Compromise deepens into genuine trust across generations
states.tension: It held. The compromise became a culture. Children grow up not understanding why their grandparents fought. The peace is real but fragile — maintained by institutions nobody questions anymore, which means nobody notices when the institutions start to crack.
states.feels_like: Post-war Europe. Genuine peace built on genuine sacrifice, maintained by people who've forgotten the sacrifice. The danger isn't war — it's complacency.

dominant_factions: the_joint_council (stable)
bridge_folk (mainstream now)
cultural_preservationists (minority)
active_gods: the_weave (strongest it's ever been)
the_drift (fading — nobody moves anymore)
threats: institutional_rot
forgotten_history
a new generation that takes peace for granted

key_figures.venns_daughter: Mira. Thirty. Born into the peace that Venn's generation built. She runs the Joint Council's cultural exchange program. She's never known conflict. She finds the old war memorials baffling — stone monuments to something that feels as distant as mythology. She's efficient, competent, and utterly unprepared for the idea that the institutions she manages could fail because she has no framework for failure. She IS the Long Peace: functional, well-meaning, and unable to imagine what's underneath the floor she walks on.
key_figures.the_last_memory_keeper: An old forge-quarter woman who remembers the blockade. She tells stories at the memorial days. Fewer people come each year. The stories sound exaggerated to the young — 'people really starved? Over ore prices?' She keeps telling them because the alternative is the stories dying, and if the stories die, the peace loses the fear that created it, and fear is the peace's immune system.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (genuine peace maintained by institutions nobody questions anymore)
worship_economy (the Weave strongest it's ever been — the Drift fading because nobody moves)
thread_connections.sub_thread: the_memory_fading — the generation that takes peace for granted. Resolution: institutions evolve to survive forgetting (→endgame_genuine_peace) or they calcify (→endgame_complacent_fall)

states.name: The Old Wounds
states.condition: Compromise holds but resentment festers + a catalyst event cracks it
states.tension: Someone remembered. A descendent of the faction that sacrificed most finds the records, reads the original terms, and realizes what their grandparents gave up. The compromise was fair, once. It isn't anymore. The wounds were supposed to heal. They scarred instead.
states.feels_like: The Troubles. Inherited grievance. Nobody alive caused the original wrong but everyone alive lives with it. The question: can you heal a wound that's become an identity?

dominant_factions: the_joint_council (cracking)
the_rememberers (new)
the_reconcilers (new, desperate)
threats: inherited_grievance
selective_memory
the_peace_was_never_real

key_figures.the_descendant: Tavrin. Twenty-six. Found his grandmother's diary in a sealed chest. The diary describes the original Compromise terms — and how much the forge quarter gave up compared to what the Senate quarter received. The terms were public once; nobody reads them now. Tavrin reads them. He starts asking questions at Joint Council meetings. The questions are polite. The questions are devastating. 'Clause seven says equal access. The forge quarter's access is through a secondary gate. Is that equal?' He's not angry. He's precise. Precision is worse than anger because you can dismiss anger and you can't dismiss arithmetic.
key_figures.the_reconciler: Lenna. Joint Council mediator. She sees Tavrin's questions and knows he's right and knows that being right could destroy the peace. She tries to address the inequities quietly — reroute the access, adjust the terms. The old guard on both sides block her because adjusting the terms means admitting the original terms were unfair, and admitting unfairness means the peace was built on a lie. She's trapped between a legitimate grievance and a fragile structure that can't survive the grievance being spoken aloud.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (inherited grievance cracking the Compromise's foundation)
worship_economy (the Weave under stress — connections built on hidden inequity)
thread_connections.sub_thread: tavrins_arithmetic — a descendant with a diary and correct math. Resolution: the inequity is addressed (→endgame_alliance_with_cost) or it festers into fragmentation (→endgame_fragmentation)

states.name: The Iron Empire
states.condition: Crusade succeeds + Forge Council absorbs conquered territories
states.tension: They won. The finest army in the world conquered the finest cities in the world. Thornwall rules from mountain to coast. The Iron Doctrine is now the law everywhere. But empires don't know how to stop expanding, and the thing that made the forge great — isolation, focus, craft — dies the moment you rule people who don't share those values.
states.feels_like: The Roman Empire at Trajan. Conquest completed. The question isn't how to win more — it's how to hold what you have when what you have is bigger than your identity.

dominant_factions: the_forge_empire (overextended)
provincial_governors (new)
resistance_cells (everywhere)
active_gods: the_burn (war-god, fully formed)
the_silence (growing in conquered territories)
threats: imperial_overreach
cultural_resistance
the_empire_forgot_how_to_forge

key_figures.governor_ash: Born in a conquered southern town. Appointed provincial governor by the Forge Empire. He administers Thornwall's laws over people who look like him and sound like him and hate him for serving the conqueror. He's good at his job — the province is well-managed, the forges productive, the roads maintained. He hates the empire he serves and serves it competently because the alternative — incompetent rebellion — would get more of his people killed than competent administration does. He's the empire's most useful tool: a conquered person who makes conquest look like governance.
key_figures.the_forge_master_abroad: Callae. A Thornwall master-smith stationed in a coastal province. She was sent to 'teach the Iron Doctrine.' She arrived and found the coastal smiths already producing work that was different from Thornwall's — adapted to salt air, using alloys the mountain never needed. She's supposed to impose the Doctrine. Instead she's learning. She can't tell the Empire this because learning from the conquered is an admission that the conqueror doesn't already have all the answers.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (empire overextended — governing people who don't want to be governed)
