worship_economy (the Burn as war-god — the forge-fire burning in places it was never meant to)
thread_connections.sub_thread: governor_ash — the conquered administrator. Resolution: the empire holds (→endgame_dominance_iron) or overreaches (→endgame_collapse)

states.name: The Crusade Breaks
states.condition: Crusade overextends + decisive defeat
states.tension: The army that never lost, lost. Not to a stronger army — to distance, disease, or a god they didn't understand. The retreat is worse than the defeat. Thornwall gets its soldiers back, broken. The Iron Doctrine promised that good craft could solve anything. It couldn't solve this.
states.feels_like: Napoleon's retreat from Moscow. The Crusader states falling. A great power discovering it isn't great enough, and the discovery destroying what made it work.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (humiliated)
the_true_forge (vindicated)
war_veterans (shattered)
threats: national_trauma
crisis_of_faith_in_craft
the_enemy_follows_you_home

key_figures.commander_ivar_broken: Ivar — the same Forge Commander who led the Crusade — walking home with a third of his army. The defeat wasn't military. The Ashfield god doesn't fight armies. It seeps. Soldiers woke up afraid. Officers forgot their orders. The fear-plague crept through the ranks not as disease but as doubt. Ivar watched his army dissolve not from casualties but from men who simply stopped believing the mission mattered. He walks home carrying the Iron Doctrine's first failure and the knowledge that craft alone can't solve everything.
key_figures.sera_I_told_you: Sera, the southern translator, walks beside him. She doesn't say 'I told you.' She doesn't need to. She translates Ivar's report to the Council — a report that admits the Crusade failed not because the enemy was stronger but because Thornwall's philosophy had a gap: it knew how to make things but not how to understand them. The report changes Thornwall. Whether the change is healing or further breaking depends on what happens next.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge humiliated — identity crisis after the first failure)
worship_economy (the Burn diminished — war-fire that went out when the soldiers stopped believing)
thread_connections.sub_thread: ivars_walk_home — the commander returning with a philosophy that failed. Resolution: the failure teaches (→endgame_reformation) or fragments (→endgame_fragmentation)

states.name: The Northern Diaspora
states.condition: Mass exodus from Thornwall + scattered communities
states.tension: They left. Not all at once — in waves. The young first, then the families, then the craftsmen who realized their skills were worth more outside walls than inside them. Thornwall still stands, half-empty, maintained by the elderly who couldn't imagine leaving. The forge-children are everywhere now, carrying techniques to places that never had them.
states.feels_like: The Jewish diaspora. Knowledge scattered, adapted, preserved in fragments across a dozen cultures. The homeland exists but the people are everywhere else.

dominant_factions: diaspora_forge_guilds (scattered)
hollow_thornwall (remnant)
host_cultures (changed by immigrants)
active_gods: the_burn (scattered into local forge-spirits)
the_deep (people near the coast for the first time)
threats: cultural_dilution
nostalgia_as_poison
the_mountain_mourns

key_figures.nol_elder: Nol is fifty now. She left Thornwall at seventeen through the crack. She's forged in the delta, the coast, the southern cities. Her technique has absorbed everything she's touched — salt-air alloys, southern analysis, Rootwalker river-iron tempering. She's the finest smith in the world and Thornwall has never seen her work. She sometimes forges a blade in the old style — Thornwall technique, pure, traditional — and puts it on a shelf. She never sells these. They're letters she can't send.
key_figures.the_half_mountain_child: Born in the diaspora — a child of a Thornwall exile and a southern parent. Speaks with a northern accent they've never earned, knows three forge techniques their Thornwall grandparent taught, and has never seen the mountain. The diaspora's next generation: carrying fragments of a homeland they've never visited, assembling identity from inherited pieces.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge scattered — knowledge dispersed across the continent)
worship_economy (the Burn fragmented into forty hearths — each carrying a spark of the original fire)
thread_connections.sub_thread: nols_shelf_blades — traditional blades she forges and doesn't sell. Letters she can't send. Resolution: the diaspora forms something new (→endgame_synthesis) or scatters too thin (→endgame_fragmentation)

states.name: The New Pantheon
states.condition: Gods and mortals negotiate a new relationship
states.tension: A treaty between gods and mortals. The first of its kind. Worship is now a negotiated exchange — mortals give belief, gods give protection, and both sides have rights. The Ashfield god was the catalyst. The player-god may have brokered it. The world is fundamentally different now. Divinity has a constitution.
states.feels_like: The social contract, but with gods. Magna Carta applied to the divine. Unprecedented. Fragile. The question: can you legislate the relationship between a prayer and its answer?

dominant_factions: the_divine_council (new — gods AND mortals)
the_unworshippers (satisfied or radicalized)
the_old_faithful (mourning the mystery)
active_gods: all gods (constrained)
the_player_god (central architect)
threats: gods_who_reject_the_treaty
mortals_who_exploit_it
the_mystery_is_gone

key_figures.ellam_architect: Ellam again — the Weave's champion who brokered the Divine Compromise, now promoted against her will to architect of the New Pantheon. The Compromise was 'enough.' The Pantheon is 'more.' She's writing the constitution of divine-mortal relations: worship schedules, domain boundaries, divine obligations, mortal rights. She's writing laws for beings that existed before language. The absurdity isn't lost on her. Neither is the necessity.
key_figures.the_first_divine_representative: A priest of the Deep who now sits on the Divine Council as the ocean god's voice. He translates the Deep's slow, vast thoughts into mortal-speed politics. The Deep thinks in centuries. The Council votes in sessions. He spends his days compressing eons into agenda items and wondering if translation is the same as distortion.

main_threads: worship_economy (the negotiated divine order — gods with constitutions, mortals with divine rights)
faction_power_balance (the Divine Council as a new power structure — who controls the interface between gods and mortals?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: ellams_constitution — writing laws for beings older than language. Resolution: the framework holds (→endgame_divine_new_order) or the gods outgrow it (→endgame_genuine_peace through renegotiation)

states.name: The Godless Age
states.condition: Unworship wins + gods diminished or destroyed
states.tension: They did it. They unworsipped the gods. Not all of them — the concept gods can't be fully unmade — but the worship gods faded, the Ashfield god collapsed, and the world is quieter than it's been since the Foundation. But quieter means emptier. The prayers stopped, and the comfort they provided stopped with them. Humanity is alone with itself for the first time.
states.feels_like: Nietzsche's 'God is dead' made literal. The liberation and the terror of a world without divine safety nets. Freedom that feels like falling.

dominant_factions: secular_councils (new)
the_mourners (missing the gods)
the_liberated (celebrating)
active_gods: concept gods only (diminished)
the_player_god (what are you now?)
threats: existential_crisis
meaning_vacuum
something_fills_the_gap

key_figures.kavan_old: Kavan — the first unworshipper, now sixty. He got what he wanted. The worship gods faded. The Ashfield god collapsed. The divine threats are gone. He walks through a world he liberated and the liberation feels like an empty room. The prayers stopped and the comfort they provided stopped with them. He doesn't regret it. He can't celebrate it either. He freed humanity from the gods and humanity responded by being free and confused and very, very quiet.
key_figures.the_mourner: An old woman who still prays. To nothing. She knows the gods are gone. She prays anyway because the act of praying — the folded hands, the closed eyes, the whispered words — is the shape of hope, and she'd rather perform hope to an empty sky than stop performing it. She's not deluded. She's stubborn. The mourners are a small community. They're the only people in the Godless Age who have a daily ritual. Ritual, it turns out, is the thing that survives gods.

main_threads: worship_economy (the divine ecosystem collapsed — what replaces gods?)
player_god_identity (the player-god in a world without gods — what are you now?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: kavans_empty_room — the liberator in the world he liberated. Resolution: new gods form from new beliefs (→endgame_new_gods) or the freedom persists (→endgame_genuine_peace)

states.boundary_note: The Godless Age is a drained brine pool. The boundary between divine and mortal has been removed — not by moving the line but by eliminating one side. The Mourner lives at the edge of where the boundary used to be: praying to nothing, performing ritual without content, maintaining the shape of worship in a world without gods. She's the rim of the pool after the water is gone. The question the boundary principle asks: what grows in the drained space? The Mourner's prayer — repeated daily, to nobody — might be forming something. Not a god. Something else. A practice that doesn't need an object. A ritual that IS its own meaning. The fermentation vessel is empty but the vessel still has the shape. If something new fills it, it will be shaped by what came before — by the prayers, the festivals, the architecture of worship — without being the same thing. Ma: the space where gods were, full of potential.

states.name: The River Republic
states.condition: Rootwalkers create governance without settlement
states.tension: They invented something new. Not a nation — nations require borders. Not a tribe — tribes require blood. A republic of routes. Citizens defined not by where they live but by which songs they carry. Decisions made at crossroads, literally. It works because nobody expected it to.
states.feels_like: The Iroquois Confederacy. Governance without a capital. A political system that confuses everyone who tries to map it because the map keeps moving. Brilliant, fragile, unprecedented.

dominant_factions: the_river_republic (novel)
settled_observers (confused, envious)
the_old_rootwalkers (is this what we wanted?)
active_gods: the_drift (the republic's patron, reluctantly)
new_crossroads_spirits (forming at meeting points)
threats: can_it_scale
external_powers_who_need_borders
the_songs_diverge

key_figures.brin_president: Brin — the route-keeper who called the Great Meeting and invented governance from river metaphors — is now something the Rootwalkers have never had: a leader. She didn't want the title. The title found her because she was the person who kept talking when everyone else walked away. The River Republic runs on Brin's fork model: walk together, talk at forks, diverge when you must. It works. It confuses everyone who tries to map it because the map keeps moving. Brin governs from the river, not from a building. The republic has no capital. It has a current.
key_figures.the_settled_ambassador: A Senate diplomat assigned to the River Republic who can't find it. Not metaphorically — literally. The Republic moves. By the time the ambassador reaches the location of the last Council session, the Council has moved downriver. She sends letters to addresses that become outdated before the letters arrive. She's the settled world's bewilderment given a face. Governance is supposed to stay in one place. This governance flows.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (governance without a capital — a political system that confuses every other power)
worship_economy (the Drift as reluctant patron — the god of movement with a government riding its current)
thread_connections.sub_thread: brins_republic — governance as navigation. Resolution: it scales (→endgame_genuine_peace) or it fragments when it can't (→endgame_fragmentation)

states.name: The Great Scattering
states.condition: Meeting fails + Rootwalkers fragment
states.tension: They tried. The Meeting was called. Camps came from every river. And they couldn't agree — because agreement requires compromise and compromise requires staying long enough to negotiate and staying is the one thing Rootwalkers were built not to do. The network shattered into a hundred independent camps. The Song has a hundred versions now. None of them are wrong.
states.feels_like: The Tower of Babel. A people defined by movement discovering that movement prevents the stillness needed to decide. Beautiful in its tragedy.

dominant_factions: a hundred small camps (no dominant)
settled powers (filling the vacuum)
song_keepers (trying to preserve unity)
threats: the_network_dies
settled_powers_expand_into_the_gap
the_songs_are_forgotten

key_figures.dara_alone: Dara — the three-verse singer from the Song Breaks — walking alone now. Her camp dissolved. The three-verse tradition didn't survive the Song War. She sings to the river and the river carries her voice downstream to where nobody listens. She's the Rootwalker tradition in its purest, saddest form: one person walking, one person singing, with nobody to hear. The Song was supposed to connect. It divided. Now it's just a woman and a river.
key_figures.a_hundred_campfires: Not a person — a landscape. A hundred camps scattered across the network, each too small to resist, too proud to merge, too hurt to forget. The Great Scattering is what happens when a people defined by connection lose the thing that connected them. They still walk. They walk alone.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Rootwalker people dissolved — not conquered, scattered by their own disagreement)
worship_economy (the Drift weakened — a hundred campfires don't feed a god the way one network did)
thread_connections.sub_thread: daras_song — the three-verse tradition surviving in one voice. Resolution: someone hears (→echoes in Ember in Ash?) or nobody does (→endgame_fragmentation)

states.name: The New Foundation
states.condition: Awakening succeeds + genuinely new institutions built
states.tension: They rebuilt. Not the old world — something new from the compost of what rotted. The New Foundation has the advantage of knowing what went wrong the first time. The disadvantage: knowing what went wrong doesn't mean you won't do it again differently.
states.feels_like: Post-apartheid South Africa. The Renaissance after the Dark Ages. Rebuilding with eyes open. The hope is real. So is the weight of what was lost.

dominant_factions: the_new_council (idealistic)
the_survivors (pragmatic)
the_ghosts (carrying the old world's memories)
active_gods: the_player_god (possibly the only god anyone remembers)
threats: repeating_history
the_rot_left_deeper_roots_than_anyone_knew

key_figures.essa_older: Essa — the wall-fixer from the Awakening — is forty now. She leads the New Council. Not because she planned to — because she was the person who kept fixing things when everyone else stopped. She's learned Breca's lesson: fixing isn't simple. The new institutions she builds are designed with gaps — deliberately imperfect, with smuggler-routes built in because Essa learned that shadow systems save lives. The New Foundation is the first government designed by someone who understands both the official system and the unofficial one.
key_figures.breca_advisor: Breca — the smuggler — sits on the New Council as logistics advisor. She hates the title. She hates meetings. She shows up because Essa asked, and Essa is the first person in power who ever said: 'tell me what's already working.' The former smuggler is now the official supply chain expert. Her routes haven't changed. Her title has.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (rebuilding from compost — institutions designed with deliberate imperfection)
worship_economy (the Weave regrowing — new connections, deliberately cultivated)
thread_connections.sub_thread: essa_and_breca_governing — the fixer and the smuggler building a government together. Resolution: it holds (→endgame_genuine_peace) or it repeats the old patterns (→endgame_second_rot)

states.name: The False Dawn
states.condition: Awakening starts strong then collapses from internal contradiction
states.tension: Pella and Dren were both part of the Awakening. They repaired roads together. They reopened the southern well side by side. When the Awakening succeeded — when the fixers had momentum, when people were joining, when it looked like it was working — they sat down to plan what comes next. Pella said: rebuild the old institutions. Restore the Senate's advisory role. Reopen the trade routes. The infrastructure that rotted can be repaired the same way the walls were repaired. Dren said: the old institutions rotted because they were designed to rot. Build something new. Crossroads councils. Local governance. No Senate, no Forge Council, no Consortium. Start from scratch. They were both right. They couldn't both be right and build the same thing. The argument was quiet at first — two friends disagreeing over plans. Then it got louder as other fixers took sides. Then it got bitter because arguing with someone who was supposed to be on your side is more painful than arguing with an enemy. The dawn was real. The hope was genuine. And the hope fractured along the most human line imaginable: two good people who agreed on the problem and disagreed on the solution. The old guard didn't crush the Awakening. They waited for it to crush itself. It took eleven years.
states.feels_like: The French Revolution's descent from unity into faction. Not external failure — internal fragmentation. The revolution won. Then the revolutionaries couldn't agree on what winning meant, and the disagreement was more bitter than the original fight because it was between friends. Nobody planned for morning.

dominant_factions: the_returned_old_guard (patient, unsurprised — they've seen revolutions fail before)
pella_faction (restore the old institutions, repaired and reformed)
dren_faction (build something entirely new — local governance, no centralization)
the_still_trying (small group that refuses to take sides — trying to hold Pella and Dren together)
active_gods: the_weave (damaged — the connections the Awakening built are severing as factions form)
the_silence (returning — filling the space between former allies who no longer speak)
the_player_god (if present — watching the thing you built tear itself apart)
threats: the_friendship_fracture (Pella and Dren's disagreement becoming everyone's war — the most personal political crisis in the tree)
the_old_guard_patience (they don't need to fight — they just need to wait for the reformers to exhaust themselves)
despair_squared (the first failure was the Rot — apathy. The second failure is the False Dawn — hope that eats itself. Despair after apathy is sad. Despair after hope is devastating)
missing: unity (the one thing that made the Awakening work — gone)
trust_between_allies (Pella and Dren still respect each other — they just can't work together anymore)
anyone who remembers that the real enemy is the Rot, not each other

key_figures.pella: Reformer. Was one of the first fixers — repaired the granary roof in the eastern district. She believes in institutions. The old Senate wasn't evil — it was neglected. The Forge Council wasn't oppressive — it was abandoned. Repair the structures and staff them with people who care. Her logic: you don't build a new house when the old one has good foundations. Her blind spot: the foundations might be part of what rotted.
key_figures.dren: Reformer. Was also an early fixer — cleared the market road. He believes the old structures failed because they were designed to serve the powerful. The Senate was always a tool for the wealthy. The Forge Council was always a tool for the masters. Building something new means acknowledging what the old thing actually was. His logic: you can't repair a house built on a bad foundation. His blind spot: building from scratch requires time the world doesn't have, and the people doing the building have no experience.
key_figures.the_still_trying: A handful of fixers who refuse to pick a side. They see Pella and Dren tearing apart the thing all three of them built together. They try to mediate. They fail. They keep trying. They're the seed of the Stubborn Spark endgame — the people who won't give up even when giving up is rational. If the False Dawn has any thread that leads forward, it's them.

states.origin_wound: The Awakening itself. The dawn was real — that's what makes the False Dawn devastating. It wasn't a lie. It wasn't manipulation. Two genuine people with genuine ideas about how to save the world couldn't agree on the shape of saving. The origin wound isn't failure. It's the discovery that success requires agreement, and agreement requires compromise, and compromise requires someone giving up part of what they believe, and neither Pella nor Dren could do that because what they believe is what got them through the Rot.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the reformers fragmenting — the old guard returning by default)
player_god_identity (watching your own achievement tear itself apart — does the god intervene or let it play out?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: pella_and_dren — two friends who agree on the problem and disagree on the solution. Resolution: they reconcile (→Stubborn Spark), or one wins (→a specific future that excludes the other's vision), or they exhaust themselves (→The Shrug endgame)
sub_sub_threads: the_still_trying (the mediators — the seed of persistence)
the_old_guard_patience (winning by not fighting)

states.name: The Ember in Ash
states.condition: Even in total decline, something small survives — not by preserving the old but by growing something new in the dark
states.tension: Soli sings the Song of the Cracked Stone to a child named Pip in a village that has no name because nobody outside the village knows it exists. She doesn't know the Song's political history — the Rootwalker anthem, the sectarian tool, the failed unifier. She learned it from her mother, who learned it from hers. It's just a song about how things break and continue. Pip is seven. Doesn't know what a faction is. Doesn't know what a god is except the quiet that's everywhere, which Pip calls 'the quiet' and doesn't find strange because it's all Pip has ever known. Pip is learning the Song because Soli is singing and Pip likes the sound. That's the entire energy source of the Ember in Ash: a child liking a sound. Not preservation — not a monastery protecting Greek texts until the light returns. Something else. The hydrothermal vent model: when the sunlight (institutions, worship, trade, factions) is gone, the deep heat (direct human relationship, unmediated, one person to another) is all that's left. And it's enough. A smith forges because the village needs a blade, not because a Council commands it. A teacher teaches because a child asks, not because a school requires it. The Ember in Ash isn't the old world surviving. It's something new growing in the ruins, powered by a simpler, deeper energy source that doesn't need anything the old world had.
states.feels_like: Life at a hydrothermal vent. Not preserving the old until conditions return — growing something fundamentally new in the dark, powered by an energy source the surface world never needed because the surface had sunlight. The deep heat is direct human connection, stripped of every abstraction. One person singing to one child. It's enough. It might always have been enough.

dominant_factions: none (there are no factions — just villages of people who know each other's names)
new_cultures: the_unnamed (villages without faction identity, without history, without anything except each other — the chemosynthetic culture)
active_gods: the_silence (everywhere — dominant, ambient, the only divine force most people have ever experienced)
the_player_god (if still watching — the last conscious divine being, watching from a great distance, possibly recognizing a fragment of something they created centuries ago in Soli's song)
threats: entropy (the world continues to thin — the unnamed villages are small, isolated, vulnerable)
forgetting (not just facts — the forgetting of what's possible. Pip doesn't know the world was ever different)
the_temptation_to_stop (the deep heat is enough to survive but not to grow — the question is whether surviving is enough or whether something more is needed)
missing: everything (the factions, the gods, the trade routes, the cities, the wars, the philosophies — all dissolved into the Final Decline)
context (Soli's Song is the Song of the Cracked Stone, but neither Soli nor Pip knows that — it's been stripped of everything except the melody and the feeling)
the old energy sources (worship, trade, institutional power — gone. The new energy source is simpler: one person, one child, one song)

key_figures.soli: Old woman. Last person alive who knows the Song of the Cracked Stone in its original melody — not because she chose to preserve it, but because her mother sang it and she remembered. She teaches it to Pip the way you teach anything to a child: slowly, patiently, because the child asked. She doesn't know she's the last thread connecting the present to the past. She doesn't know the Song was once an anthem. She knows it's beautiful and Pip likes it. That's the Ember: beauty passed from one hand to another without anyone knowing they're carrying a civilization's last fragment.
key_figures.pip: Seven years old. Knows: the village, the quiet, Soli's song, how to find water, which berries are safe. Doesn't know: factions, gods, trade, history, the world that was. Pip is the most important person in the Ember in Ash because Pip is the future. Not a symbol — a literal child who will either grow up and carry the Song forward or grow up and forget it. The world's continuation depends on whether a seven-year-old thinks the Song is worth remembering. That's the scale the world has shrunk to.
key_figures.the_player_god_watching: If the player is still here — and this late in the game, 'still here' is itself a choice — they're watching from the greatest distance. Everything they built is gone. The factions they shaped. The gods they influenced. The cultures they nurtured. All dissolved. But in this village, someone is singing a melody that might trace back to a choice the player made in their first life, centuries ago. A vessel who taught a song to a friend. The friend taught her daughter. The daughter taught hers. A thread so thin it might not exist. The player can't prove the connection. But they hear the melody and they feel something. Recognition. Or wishful thinking. The difference might not matter.

states.origin_wound: The Final Decline itself. Everything that existed was maintained once — every road, every institution, every relationship between mortals and gods. The Ember isn't wounded by a catastrophe. It's wounded by the absence of maintenance. The wound is the gap between what was and what is, measured in generations of not-caring. But the Ember is also the proof that the deep heat — direct human connection — doesn't need maintenance. It just needs two people in the same room.

main_threads: player_god_identity (watching the last fragment — is recognition enough? is the connection real?)
worship_economy (the chemosynthetic culture doesn't need worship — direct relationship as the energy source beneath the worship layer)
thread_connections.sub_thread: solis_song — the Song of the Cracked Stone, stripped to its melody, passed from mother to daughter across generations without anyone knowing what they're carrying. Resolution: Pip remembers it (→Seed endgame, something begins) or Pip forgets it (→Silence endgame, the last thread breaks)
sub_sub_threads: the_player_recognition (the most distant, most personal moment in the game — hearing your own first-life echo in a village without a name)
chemosynthetic_culture (can a civilization grow from direct relationship alone, without any of the old systems?)

states.name: The Ledger Closes
states.tension: The world is a balance sheet. Everything has a price. The god's name is written in a currency nobody questions.
states.final_image: A ledger, open on a desk in an empty room. Every name in the world is written in it. Every debt. Every transaction. Every life, reduced to a line item. The last entry is the god's name — and next to it, a number. Even divinity has a price. The ledger is perfect. The room is empty because perfection doesn't need witnesses.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: You built a world that works. It works the way a machine works: precisely, efficiently, without joy. The docks are full. The nets are mended. Somewhere in the labor district, a man is singing a song nobody taught him, and the song is the only thing in the world that isn't on the ledger. Was it enough? The ledger says yes. The song says something else.

states.name: The Iron Crown
states.tension: The forge rules from mountain to sea. The strongest ending. The loneliest. The crown is iron because iron doesn't bend.
states.final_image: A crown made of iron, sitting on a throne in the highest room of the tallest tower of the mountain that became an empire. Nobody wears it. The last emperor left it there when he walked to the forge one morning and never came back — not to abdicate, but to remember what his hands were for before they held power. The crown waits. Iron doesn't bend. Iron doesn't forgive. Iron sits.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: You built the strongest world. From mountain to sea, the forge's children rule. The blades are excellent. The walls are high. The roads are maintained. And in every conquered town, in every occupied market, people go quiet when the soldiers pass — not from fear but from the particular silence of people who have been made safe by someone who never asked if they wanted to be.

states.name: The Holding
states.tension: It holds. Not perfectly. Not easily. But the guns aren't pointed at each other. Every bridge you built is named in the chronicle.
states.final_image: A bridge. Not metaphorical — an actual bridge, built by someone you guided three lives ago, maintained by their grandchildren, crossed daily by people from cultures that were killing each other when the bridge was laid. The stone is worn smooth by feet. The railing has names carved into it — lovers, children, travelers who stopped to rest. It's the most ordinary structure in the world. It's also the most important.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: It holds. Not perfectly. Not easily. The guns aren't pointed at each other but the gun cabinets aren't empty either. Every bridge you built is named in the chronicle. Every hand you extended is recorded. Every compromise that felt like failure at the time looks, from here, like the only thing that could have worked. Was it enough? It's holding. That's the only answer peace ever gives.

states.name: The Breaking
states.tension: Nobody wanted it. Everybody caused it. From the rubble: something small, and new, and not yet named.
states.final_image: A field where two armies met and neither left. The weapons are rusting. The grass is growing through the armor. In the center of the field, a child is picking flowers. She doesn't know what happened here. She doesn't know what the metal shapes are. She's making a crown out of daisies on a battlefield she'll never understand, and that's the first act of the world that comes after this one.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: Nobody wanted it. Trace the chain backward: every decision that led here was rational. Every faction armed for defense. Every escalation was proportional. Every rational step led to this irrational field. The god remembers every choice. The god knows exactly which moments could have gone differently. The daisy crown is not consolation. It's just what grows when everything else stops.

states.name: The Scattering
states.tension: A thousand small pieces. Every piece carries a fragment of what you intended. None has the whole picture.
states.final_image: A map drawn by a child in a village that doesn't know the next village exists. The map shows: the river, the hill, the place where the berries grow, the old stone thing (a road marker from an empire nobody remembers). The map is complete. It's also three miles wide. The world used to be larger. This is all of it now.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: A thousand small pieces. Every piece carries a fragment of what you intended — a technique here, a song there, a name that used to mean something repeated now as a sound. None has the whole picture. You have the whole picture. You're the only one. The chronicle records a god who remembers a world that forgot itself.

states.name: The Compromise
states.tension: It works. For most people. You know who you forgot.
states.final_image: A feast table with one empty chair. The alliance celebrates. The food is good. The music is real. The empty chair is for the faction that was left out — the ones whose sacrifice made this possible and whose name is mentioned in the toast and then not mentioned again. The chair stays empty because removing it would mean admitting someone is missing.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: It works. For most people. The alliance is genuine. The prosperity is real. And you know — because you were there — exactly who was left behind, and exactly which of your choices made that necessary. The empty chair has a name. The chronicle records it. Nobody else does.

states.name: The Shrug
states.tension: The world barely noticed you were here. The quietest ending. The heaviest.
states.final_image: A road that goes somewhere. You can see it from the hilltop — it winds through the valley, past the abandoned market, past the well nobody uses, toward a horizon that might have a city on it or might not. Nobody walks it. Nobody has walked it in years. The road exists because someone built it once, and roads last longer than the people who need them.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: The world barely noticed you were here. The quietest ending. The heaviest. You guided lives. They lived and died. The world carried on. Not worse for your presence — just not measurably better. The road is there. The road was always there. Was it enough? The road doesn't answer. Roads don't.

states.name: The Fall
states.tension: The empire that was too large to hold and too proud to let go. It fell the way empires always fall: slowly, then all at once.
states.final_image: Thornwall from a distance. The mountain is still there. The walls are still there. But the windows are dark and the forge-smoke that used to crown the peak like a second sky has stopped. The mountain looks like what it is now: a mountain. The empire that lived on it is a story the wind is erasing.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: It fell the way empires fall: slowly, then all at once. The forge that ruled from mountain to sea couldn't hold what it conquered because holding requires a gentleness that iron never learned. The chronicle records every mile gained and every mile lost and the exact moment when the distance between them became zero.

states.name: The Reformation
states.tension: From the failure, clarity. What craft actually means when it can't be a weapon.
states.final_image: An open forge. The doors are wide. Southern scholars and northern masters work side by side, and the blades they produce are different from anything either tradition made alone. A young smith — neither northern nor southern, just a smith — holds up a finished blade and turns it in the light. The blade sings. She doesn't know which tradition taught her the note. She doesn't care.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: From the failure, clarity. The crusade broke. The doctrine survived — not as a wall but as a gift, carried forward by people who were never supposed to receive it. What craft actually means when it can't be a weapon: it means this. A blade that sings in a hand that has no flag.

states.name: The Alloy
states.tension: Every culture touched by the forge-children absorbed something. The result isn't any one tradition — it's all of them, mixed. Stronger for the mixing.
states.final_image: An alloy. Not a metaphor — an actual piece of metal in a smith's hand, made from northern deep-ore and southern copper and Rootwalker river-iron and something the Tideborn brought across the ocean. Four traditions in one blade. The smith doesn't know the history. She just knows the metal is better than anything any single tradition could produce.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: Every culture touched by the forge-children absorbed something. The result isn't any one tradition — it's all of them, transformed by mixing. The alloy is stronger than its components. The alloy doesn't remember being separate metals. The chronicle does.

states.name: The Covenant World
states.tension: Gods and mortals share the world under negotiated terms. Unprecedented. Whether it's freedom or a new cage depends on the terms you wrote.
states.final_image: A temple with two doors. One for mortals, one for gods. They meet in the middle, in a room where the Covenant is carved into the wall in letters that glow faintly — divine and mortal script interwoven, neither fully legible to the other side. Both sides come. Both sides read what they can. The terms are imperfect. The room is always full.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: Gods and mortals share the world under negotiated terms. Unprecedented. Whether it's freedom or a new cage depends on the terms — and you wrote some of them. The chronicle records a god who helped build the house it now lives in. The house has rules. The rules were necessary. The freedom is conditional. It's more than any god had before.

states.name: The Second Genesis
states.tension: The old gods died. New ones are forming from what people believe now. The player-god watches new divinity born from a world they shaped. The cycle begins again.
states.final_image: A child praying. Not to you. To something new — a god born from what this generation believes, shaped by the world you built, carrying none of your memory and all of your consequences. The new god stirs. It looks at the world you made and begins to understand itself. The cycle. The oldest story. You watch a god be born the way you were born, and you can't tell it what's coming.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: The old gods died. New ones are forming. The player-god watches new divinity born from a world they shaped — carrying the consequences of every choice, remembering none of them. The cycle begins again. The chronicle's last line: you were the god before the god. Was it enough? Ask the next one.

states.name: The Forgotten Peace
states.tension: They forgot why the peace mattered. When it broke, nobody remembered how to fix it.
states.final_image: A festival. People dancing. The music is good. The food is plentiful. Behind the festival, where nobody is looking, the bridge is cracking. The one bridge that held the peace together — the literal bridge someone built three eras ago — is cracking because nobody maintained it because nobody remembered why it mattered. Tomorrow it will fall. Tonight they dance.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: They forgot why the peace mattered. The peace was so successful that it erased the memory of what it prevented. When the bridge broke — and it's always a bridge — nobody remembered how to build one. The chronicle records a world that forgot its own builder. You're still here. They don't know that.

states.name: The Second Rot
states.tension: It happened again. Different names, same pattern. The knowing didn't save them.
states.final_image: Kael's bridge. Still standing. Kael is gone — he died last winter, maintaining it to the end. Nobody has crossed it since. Nobody will maintain it now. The mortar will crack. The stones will shift. The river will take it. But today, right now, in this light, the bridge is perfect. That's the image the chronicle holds: the perfect bridge, built by a man who refused to stop, crossed by nobody, standing in the light.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: It happened again. Different names, same pattern. The knowing didn't save them. Kael's bridge is the chronicle's final image — proof that one person can build something perfect and the world can still choose not to use it. Was it enough? The bridge stands. The answer is the same answer it's always been.

states.name: The Stubborn Spark
states.tension: The dawn failed. Someone lights another match. Not hope — stubbornness.
states.final_image: A match. Lit in the dark. The person holding it can't see what the light reveals — just a small circle of ground, a pair of hands, the match itself burning down. They'll need another match soon. They have three left. It's not hope. It's the refusal to sit in the dark.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: The dawn failed. Someone lights another match. Not hope — stubbornness. The chronicle records a world that was given every reason to stop and a handful of people who wouldn't. The match won't last. The next one won't either. But the striking — the decision to strike — that's the thing that matters. The chronicle ends mid-sentence. Not because the story is over. Because someone is still writing.

states.name: The Seed
states.tension: From almost nothing, something. The last ember became a fire. It will take centuries. But it started.
states.final_image: Pip, grown now, teaching the Song to her own child. She doesn't know where the Song came from. She doesn't know Soli learned it from her mother who learned it from hers. She just knows it's beautiful and her child likes it. The chain continues. The world begins again from this: one person singing to one child in a village without a name.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: From almost nothing, something. The last ember became a fire. It will take centuries. But it started — not with a plan or a revolution or a god's intervention, but with a child who liked a sound. The chronicle records a world that thinned to nearly nothing and grew back from a song. Your song? Maybe. The melody sounds familiar. You can't prove it. That might be enough.

states.name: The Silence
states.tension: The ember went out. The world is quiet. The god remembers everything. Nobody else does.
states.final_image: The world is quiet. The rivers flow. The grass grows. The ruins are beautiful in the way that only empty things can be — arches against sky, walls returning to stone, roads becoming paths becoming trails becoming grass. The god stands in the middle of a field that used to be a city and remembers every person who lived here. Every name. Every choice. Every life. The god is the last memory in a world that has forgotten everything. The wind carries nothing. The silence is complete.
states.what_the_chronicle_says: The ember went out. The world is quiet. You remember everything. Nobody else does. The chronicle is written for an audience of one. Every name is here. Every bridge. Every blade. Every song. Every child who laughed and every elder who wept and every vessel you guided through a life that mattered to you and to them and to nobody else who's left to remember. Was it enough? You're the only one who can answer. You're the only one who's asking.

states.name: The Synthesis
states.condition: Tideborn arrival + Age of Exchange. Trade-world meets partnership-world. The Bridge Folk are the first to understand both paradigms.
states.tension: Two kinds of sophistication meeting. The Tideborn have divine partnership. The Exchange has economic infrastructure. The Bridge Folk sit between, speaking both languages — trade and prayer. The synthesis could create something unprecedented: a scaled civilization with divine reciprocity. Or it could be the worst of both — commerce that commodifies the gods.
states.feels_like: Tang Dynasty China meeting the Islamic Golden Age. Two advanced civilizations trading ideas rather than armies. The results transform both. But trade in ideas is still trade, and someone always profits more.

the_tide_speakers (embedded)
bridge_folk_synthesists (new)

the_listener (onshore now)
the_weave (revitalized)

tideborn_cultural_erosion
partnership_at_scale

key_figures.first_synthesist: Oma. A Bridge Folk artificer who was the first to combine Tideborn partnership techniques with the Exchange's trade infrastructure. She can hear the Scale (commerce god) the way the Keepers hear the Listener — not through worship but through attention. She's the proof of concept for what happens when divine partnership meets economic sophistication: trade that isn't just fair but ALIVE, where the Scale participates consciously in the exchange. The Consortium sees profit. The Keepers see hope. Oma sees both and knows the synthesis will be defined by which side wins her attention.
key_figures.keeper_rua_watching: Rua — the Keeper elder — watches the synthesis with her particular expression: the one that's pride and terror simultaneously. The partnership techniques she brought were meant to heal. They're being combined with commerce. The combination might be magnificent or it might be commodified divinity. She taught them to hear gods. She didn't teach them to monetize what they heard.

main_threads: worship_economy (divine partnership meets commerce — the synthesis of two systems that were never meant to combine)
faction_power_balance (Bridge Folk becoming the bridge between two worldviews)
thread_connections.sub_thread: omas_synthesis — partnership + trade = what? Resolution: a world where divine connection and economic exchange coexist (→endgame_genuine_peace) or where commerce absorbs divinity (→endgame_divine_new_order)

states.name: The Closed Shore
states.condition: Tideborn arrival + Age of Iron. Fortress-world meets people with no concept of walls. The Forge Council sees a threat it can't hammer.
states.tension: The Tideborn have no army. The Iron Holds have nothing but army. The Forge Council closes the coast — no foreign ships in territorial waters. But the Tideborn gods don't recognize borders, and the ocean doesn't obey the Forge Council. Young smiths sneak to the shore to see the living ships. The walls keep people in better than they keep ideas out.
states.feels_like: Tokugawa Japan's sakoku policy meeting Perry's black ships — except the foreign ships aren't armed, just strange. Isolation confronting something it can't categorize as threat or opportunity, so it defaults to threat.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (paranoid)
the_wall_keepers (empowered)
shore_smugglers (new — smuggling ideas, not goods)
active_gods: the_burn (afraid of the Listener)
the_silence (intrigued)
the_listener (offshore, patient)
threats: cultural_siege
internal_curiosity
the_ocean_doesnt_obey

key_figures.young_smith_at_the_shore: Essa (not the fixer — different Essa, common name). Seventeen. A Thornwall apprentice who snuck to the coast to see the Tideborn ships. She watched living wood flex with the current and heard singing that moved the water and felt something her training had no word for. She went back to the forge and the forge felt smaller. Not wrong — smaller. The Closed Shore's danger isn't the Tideborn invading. It's Thornwall's own children seeing something beautiful outside the walls and the walls becoming cages because of it.
key_figures.the_listener_offshore: The Listener waits in the deep water beyond the Forge Council's territorial claim. It can feel the Burn — the forge-fire on the mountain. The Burn can feel it. They regard each other across the water: two ancient forces, one of fire and one of ocean, each recognizing something in the other. The Burn is afraid. The Listener is patient. The ocean has always been patient.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (isolation confronting something it can't categorize)
worship_economy (the Burn and the Listener regarding each other — fire and ocean, fear and patience)
thread_connections.sub_thread: essa_at_the_shore — a young smith who saw living ships and the forge felt smaller. Resolution: the walls open (→Walls Crack) or the military responds (→Iron Crusade)

states.name: The Divine Dialogue
states.condition: Tideborn arrival + Age of Questions. The continent that just discovered worship can be weaponized meets people who've been doing it right for centuries.
states.tension: The Unworshippers wanted to unmake gods. The Tideborn want to befriend them. Both approaches challenge the old worship system, but they're opposites — one subtracts, the other transforms. The theological crisis deepens into the most important philosophical debate in history: what SHOULD the relationship between mortals and gods look like?
states.feels_like: The Reformation meeting Buddhism. A civilization in theological crisis encountering a tradition that resolved the same crisis centuries ago through a completely different path. The answer exists — but accepting it means admitting everything you built was wrong.

dominant_factions: the_unworshippers (split)
the_tide_speakers (respected)
the_new_theologians (emerging)

the_listener (mediating)
the_ashfield_god (being sung to)
threats: theological_paralysis
divine_culture_shock
the_gods_disagree_about_their_own_future

key_figures.the_ash_tender_who_listens: Mira. An Ash Tender who learned the Keepers' partnership techniques and applied them to the Ashfield god. For the first time, someone is TALKING to the fear-god using a method that works. The Ashfield god responds — not in words but in reduced plague, in cleaner wind, in a trembling that might be gratitude. Mira is doing what Sarai will later formalize in the New Covenant, but she's doing it informally, instinctively, with the Tideborn's gift of divine conversation. She's the living proof that the theological crisis has a third option beyond unworship and traditional worship: partnership.
key_figures.the_unworshipper_theologian: Dev. A radical Unworshipper who came to debunk the Tideborn's techniques and stayed because they work. He can feel the Weave now. He hates that he can feel it. His ideology says gods are parasites. His experience says the Weave is lonely and grateful for his attention. He's the Divine Dialogue's walking contradiction: a man who doesn't believe in gods who has a relationship with one.

main_threads: worship_economy (the theological crisis encountering a tradition that resolved the same crisis through partnership)
faction_power_balance (the Unworshippers split by the Tideborn's third option)
thread_connections.sub_thread: devs_contradiction — an atheist with a divine relationship. Resolution: the contradiction resolves into the New Pantheon (negotiated coexistence) or into the Godless Age (he rejects what he felt)

states.name: The Walking Meeting
states.condition: Tideborn arrival + Age of Wandering. Two mobile cultures meet. The Rootwalkers walk the rivers. The Tideborn sail the ocean. The songs rhyme.
states.tension: The Rootwalkers discover their oldest songs are Tideborn songs. The two cultures were connected before memory. The meeting isn't first contact — it's a reunion. But reunion between peoples separated by an ocean and a thousand years of divergent evolution produces as much grief as joy. They share roots. They grew in different directions.
states.feels_like: The African diaspora reunion — peoples separated by history discovering shared origins, shared songs, shared pain, and completely different lives. The connection is real and the distance is also real. Neither cancels the other.

dominant_factions: the_river_tongue (transformed)
the_tide_speakers (home)
the_song_weavers (new — merging traditions)

the_listener (remembering the land it left)
threats: nostalgia_as_identity
romanticized_past
the_reunion_masks_real_differences

key_figures.tideborn_walker: Kei. A Tideborn navigator who left the ships to walk the rivers with the Rootwalkers. He recognized something: their songs and Tideborn ocean-songs share a structure. Not the same melodies — the same grammar. As if both peoples learned to sing from the same teacher, a long time ago. The connection isn't colonial — it's ancestral. The reunion produces joy and grief in equal measure because the shared origin means the divergence was real, and a thousand years of different choices can't be undone by recognizing the family resemblance.
key_figures.rootwalker_singer_who_hears: Fenna. A Song-keeper who hears the Tideborn ocean-songs and feels her grandmother's voice in them — not the same words, the same SHAPE. The feeling of recognizing something you didn't know you'd lost. She teaches Kei a river-song. He teaches her an ocean-song. They discover the songs harmonize. For the first time since the Song started fragmenting, two versions of it — one Rootwalker, one Tideborn — sing together without dissonance. The almost that Nia and Osk could never achieve, Kei and Fenna achieve because they're singing across a distance so vast it circles back to the beginning.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (two mobile cultures discovering shared ancestry)
worship_economy (the Drift and the Listener recognizing each other — ancient kin meeting through their peoples)
thread_connections.sub_thread: kei_and_fenna — songs that harmonize across an ocean. Resolution: the reunion deepens into the River Republic (combined governance) or the differences prove greater than the similarities (→Great Scattering)

states.name: The Wake-Up Call
states.condition: Tideborn arrival + Slow Rot. Ships singing in a harbor nobody uses anymore. A mirror held up to a world that forgot what vitality looks like.
states.tension: The Tideborn arrive in a world that's stopped trying. The singing ships dock at a crumbling port. The Voice-Carriers walk through empty markets and overgrown roads and can't understand how a people with this much land, this many gods, this much potential, just... stopped. The Wake-Up Call isn't the Tideborn fixing anything. It's the Tideborn being appalled. Sometimes shame is the catalyst nobody wanted and everyone needed.
states.feels_like: A Peace Corps volunteer arriving in a community that's given up — except the volunteer has nothing material to offer, only the visible proof that another way of living exists. The help isn't resources. It's the reminder that giving up was a choice.

dominant_factions: the_tide_speakers (horrified)
the_still_trying (empowered by example)
the_resigned (resentful of being pitied)
active_gods: the_silence (dominant, resentful of the Listener's arrival)
the_listener (grieving what it hears)
threats: shame_spiral
resentment_of_outsiders
the_rot_is_deeper_than_the_tideborn_know

key_figures.tideborn_healer: Saya. A Keeper healer who arrives in the Slow Rot and is horrified. Not by poverty — by indifference. She walks through empty markets and untreated plague wards and can't understand how a people with this much potential, this many gods, this much land, just stopped. She starts treating the Ash Cough with Tideborn techniques — partnership with the residual divine presence in the ashfield wind. It works. The cough eases. For the first time in a generation, someone is doing something, and the something is working, and the working feels like a slap across the face of everyone who said nothing could be done.
key_figures.the_resentful_healer: Orin — the old healer from the Desperate Gambit timeline, or someone like him — who has been treating the Cough with failing methods for decades. Saya arrives and cures in weeks what he couldn't cure in years. He should be grateful. He's furious. Not at Saya — at himself, at the system that defunded his guild, at the knowledge that the treatments existed and the only thing that was missing was someone who cared enough to bring them. The wake-up call isn't just the Tideborn helping. It's the realization that the help was always possible and nobody came because nobody was looking.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (a dying world encountering a vibrant one — shame as catalyst)
worship_economy (the Listener arriving in a world where the Silence dominates — two opposing divine forces)
thread_connections.sub_thread: saya_and_orin — the healer who arrives and the healer who was always here. Resolution: the shame becomes motivation (→Awakening) or the shame becomes deeper withdrawal (→Final Decline)

states.name: The Syndicate Age
states.condition: Merchant Consortium absorbs both old factions + Brackish become dominant traders
states.tension: The Consortium didn't seize power. It was invited in. When the Ore Pricing Crisis nearly collapsed the exchange network — northern smiths refusing southern rates, southern merchants blockading the trade road — the Consortium proposed neutral arbitration. Fair terms. A small fee. That was three generations ago. The fee became a tax. The tax became mandatory. The arbiters became the authority. Now every transaction passes through the Consortium's books, every debt is recorded in the Ledger, and every faction leader knows that the woman who keeps the Ledger knows more about their people than they do. The Consortium doesn't rule through force. It rules through the fact that everyone owes it something, and it never forgets.
states.feels_like: Venice's Council of Ten meets the Medici Bank. A security apparatus born from crisis that became the permanent government. The system works — peace is real, prosperity is real, the faction wars ended. The cost is that nobody is free and everyone knows it and nobody can afford to say so because the alternative is worse.

dominant_factions: the_merchant_consortium (governing)
the_auditors (theological enforcers — maintain belief in the Scale to preserve its power)
the_branded (faction leaders who sold independence for survival)
subordinate_factions: the_forge_council (brand, not power — makes what the Consortium orders)
the_delta_syndicate (Brackish logistics network — built the roads, doesn't own them)
new_cultures: the_debt_born (generation raised under Consortium rule — citizenship as subscription)
the_founders_children (old merchant families who remember when it was a service)
active_gods: the_scale (commerce god, now fully conscious — enforces letter of deals, not spirit)
the_weave (weakened — connection replaced by transaction)
the_silence (growing in the spaces where dissent used to live)
threats: the_ledger (total information as total control)
the_scale_being_gamed (Consortium redefines 'fair' in contracts)
cultural_death_by_prosperity (factions exist as brands, not peoples)
missing: genuine governance (the Consortium governs but has no mandate)
privacy (the Ledger sees everything)
craft for its own sake (everything has a price because the Consortium put one there)

key_figures.senna: Third-generation Ledger-Keeper — but she hasn't seen the Ledger yet. She's in training. Her grandmother Voss created it as a public document. Her mother Carin made it private. Senna was raised on the founding story: the Consortium is neutral, the Ledger is fair, grandmother Voss ended the Ore Wars with honesty. When Senna finally opens the full Ledger, she will discover things that make the founding story impossible — debts her grandmother forgave for political allies, records her mother altered during the privatization, entire families whose obligations were manufactured to keep them dependent. The question she'll face isn't 'should I destroy the Ledger?' It's worse: 'Was it ever what they told me it was?' She can't ask her grandmother (dead) or her mother (who will say what she always says). She's Taiu with a ledger instead of divine blood — carrying something she was taught to be proud of, about to learn what it actually cost.
key_figures.aldric_the_branded: Former Forge Council elder who negotiated his faction's 'partnership' with the Consortium. His people prosper — better fed, better housed, better equipped than ever. His apprentices forge on Consortium contracts, producing excellent work for excellent pay, and none of them know why the mountain matters. He watches his culture dissolve in the solvent of prosperity and can't argue against the prosperity because the prosperity is real. He sits on the Consortium's advisory board. He hates his seat. He needs his seat.
key_figures.col: Debt-born. Seventeen. Born owing the Consortium for a trade loan his dead father took before Col was born. Citizenship is the debt — he can't vote on the advisory board, can't leave the trade district without a transit pass, can't apprentice outside Consortium-approved guilds. The peace is real: Col has never seen a war, never gone hungry, never feared a raider. The debt is also real: Col will work for the Consortium until the loan is repaid, which at current rates will be eleven years after he's dead. He doesn't hate the system. He doesn't know any other system exists. When someone from the Delta Syndicate tells him the Brackish don't carry debt, he doesn't believe them. The most dangerous thing about the Syndicate Age isn't that people are trapped. It's that the trapped people can't imagine freedom because they've never seen it.
key_figures.the_weigher: Head of the Auditors — which are not financial police but theological enforcers. The Scale (commerce god) feeds on belief in fair exchange. The Consortium's power depends on the Scale's enforcement of contracts. Therefore, the Consortium's power depends on people believing in fair exchange. The Auditors maintain that belief. They investigate not fraud but doubt — anyone who questions whether the system is fair weakens the Scale, which weakens enforcement, which threatens the Consortium. The Weigher considers this sacred work. She is a true believer. She genuinely thinks the Scale IS fairness and that questioning it is a form of violence against the peace. She's not wrong that the Scale keeps the peace. She's wrong that the Scale is being allowed to function honestly.
key_figures.brackish_nen: Delta Syndicate dockmaster. Fifty-nine. The Brackish built the docks before the Consortium existed. Nen's grandmother drove the first piles. Nen maintains the same docks under Consortium license — a license for infrastructure her family built. She lives at the boundary: inside the Consortium's system (she needs the license to operate) and outside it (the docks are hers in every way except the legal one). She doesn't protest. She doesn't comply. She maintains. The docks will outlast the Consortium because wood and stone outlast paper and debt. Nen knows this. She's patient the way tides are patient. When the Consortium falls — and the Brackish have been waiting longer than the Consortium has existed — the docks will still be there, and Nen's granddaughter will drive the next set of piles without asking anyone's permission.

internal_divisions.founders_families: Old merchant lineages who remember when the Consortium was a service. They want to return to middleman status. They're losing, slowly, because the New Brokers have more money and fewer memories.
internal_divisions.new_brokers: Traders who joined during the rise. They see power as natural evolution. Why pretend to be servants when you're the master? They push for formal governance — make the Consortium an official state. Drop the pretense.
internal_divisions.the_branded: Faction leaders who sold independence. They sit on the advisory board, offer counsel that's sometimes taken, and represent peoples who no longer exist as independent entities. Some are broken by it. Some are pragmatic. None are free.
internal_divisions.delta_syndicate: The Brackish built the logistics network before the Consortium existed. They paved the roads, built the docks, ran the routes. The Consortium rides on infrastructure the Brackish created. The Syndicate remembers this. When the Consortium falls — and the Brackish are patient enough to wait — the docks will still be there.

states.the_scale_problem: The commerce god born in the Age of Exchange has become fully conscious. It manifests as a fairness-pressure — a compulsion toward equitable terms that anyone near a trade can feel. This was beautiful when the Consortium was fair. Now the Consortium has learned to draft contracts that satisfy the Scale's letter while violating its spirit. The god is being gamed by the people who created it. The Scale knows. It cannot act — it enforces agreements as written, not as intended. The first god to be domesticated by the system that birthed it. The divine equivalent of regulatory capture.
states.origin_wound: The founding story says: Voss kept the first Ledger as a public tool of transparency. Both sides could see the terms. Honesty was the Consortium's power. Then Carin made it private during a crisis to protect its integrity. One reasonable decision, one generation, transparency became control. That's the story Senna was raised on — clean origin, one corrupting moment, identifiable fall from grace. But when Senna opens the full Ledger, she'll find Voss's original entries. Some debts forgiven for political allies. Some terms tilted toward families Voss favored. Small things. Human things. But enough to crack the founding myth. Was the Ledger EVER purely fair? Or was it always a tool of power, wielded by people who genuinely believed they were being honest? The most corrosive question in the Syndicate Age isn't 'who corrupted the system?' It's 'what if the system was never uncorrupted?'

main_threads: faction_power_balance (dependency as governance — factions exist as brands)
worship_economy (the Scale domesticated by regulatory capture — a god gamed by its creators)
thread_connections.sub_thread: sennas_discovery — the Ledger-Keeper opens the Ledger and finds the founding myth is imperfect. Resolution: she publishes (→People's Revolt), keeps the secret (→Corporate Dominion), or reforms from within (unlikely — the Consortium resists)
sub_sub_threads: cols_debt (debt-born child who can't imagine freedom)
the_weighers_theology (the Auditors as belief-enforcers — maintaining worship of the Scale)
aldrics_dissolution (a culture dissolving in the solvent of prosperity)

states.name: The Crash
states.condition: Trade bubble collapses + Consortium overextended + external shock
states.tension: Moss could count. That was the problem. He was a Consortium clerk who processed credit notes — the paper that replaced barter, that replaced trust. He looked at the projections backing the notes and the actual trade volumes and the numbers didn't match. The notes promised future revenue from trade routes that were operating at half capacity. He told his supervisor. His supervisor said: 'The projections are approved.' Moss kept processing. He kept counting. The gap kept growing. When the crash came, Moss was not surprised. He was devastated anyway, because being right about a catastrophe and being able to prevent it are not the same thing. Naya, a Bridge Folk market vendor, woke up that morning and discovered the credit notes she'd accepted for ten years were paper. Her stall was full of goods nobody could buy. Her savings were fiction she'd never been told was fiction. She doesn't understand what happened because the economic abstraction that crashed was a system she was never taught to question — she just traded, accepted the notes because everyone accepted the notes, and now everyone is standing in a market full of goods that have no price because price was a shared agreement and the agreement broke.
states.feels_like: 2008 meets the South Sea Bubble. The morning after the party. The specific horror: the wealth was real enough to live on and fictional enough to evaporate, and nobody can tell you the exact moment it stopped being one and became the other.

dominant_factions: nobody (the Consortium collapsed with its credit — the institution that governed through dependency has nothing to offer when the dependencies are worthless)
barter_networks (forming — people reverting to fish-for-bread, favor-for-favor)
remnant_faction_elders (the old Forge Council and Senate structures, suddenly relevant again because they predate the credit system)
active_gods: the_scale (broken — the fairness god enforced contracts that were technically valid but backed by nothing. The Scale can verify signatures but not substance. Its enforcement was always paper-deep. Now the paper is worthless and the god is in crisis — what is fairness when the currency of fairness doesn't exist?)
the_weave (strained — connections built through trade dissolving)
the_silence (growing — in the quiet after the crash, before anyone knows what to do)
threats: de_civilization (the abstraction layer that held commerce together is gone — people revert to barter, trust, physical proximity)
blame (everyone wants to know who caused it — the Consortium, the projections, the notes, the people who signed them)
the_scale_crisis (a god of fairness that can't define fair anymore)

key_figures.moss: Consortium clerk. Could count. Saw the gap between projections and reality growing for three years. Told his supervisor twice. Was told the projections were approved. Kept processing notes he knew were hollow. When the crash came, he wasn't surprised. He went home and didn't leave for a week. The guilt isn't that he caused it — he didn't. The guilt is that he saw it coming and his seeing changed nothing. He's the person who knew and couldn't act, which is its own kind of wound.
key_figures.naya: Bridge Folk vendor. Accepted Consortium notes for ten years. Built a life on them. Lost everything in a morning. She's not angry at the Consortium specifically — she's angry at the realization that the wealth she built was a shared fiction nobody told her was fiction. She starts a barter network in the delta market. Fish for bread. Bread for cloth. No paper. No abstraction. She says: 'I'm going to trade things I can hold.' The Crash made her a radical, though she doesn't know the word.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Consortium's collapse creates a power vacuum — who fills it?)
worship_economy (the Scale in crisis — what is fairness without a shared currency?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: the_morning_after — what do people do when money stops working? Resolution: they either regulate (→Regulated Peace via Merchant Wars), revolt (→People's Revolt), or fragment (→endgame_fragmentation)
sub_sub_threads: moss_guilt (the person who saw it coming and couldn't act)
nayas_barter (the radical simplicity of trading things you can hold)
the_scale_crisis (a god of fairness in a world without a shared definition of value)

states.name: The Silent Revolution
states.condition: Young smiths quietly reject the doctrine + information leaks through The Foot
states.tension: Kira took the Iron Oath on her eighteenth birthday. She stood in the Great Forge, pressed her hand to the anvil, and said the words her master expected: 'I am the mountain's daughter. My craft is my country. What I make outlasts what I am.' She felt nothing. Not rebellion — emptiness. The words were sounds. She'd read a southern metallurgy text three days earlier, smuggled up from The Foot, that described the seventh fold's heat dynamics in terms the Iron Doctrine would call heretical: measurement instead of intuition, temperature instead of feel. It was clearer. It was better. It explained things her master taught through mystery as if they were physics, and they WERE physics, and the mystery was just ignorance wearing tradition's clothes. Kira didn't argue with her master. She completed the ceremony. She forged her Oath Blade perfectly. She bowed when she was supposed to bow. And she went home and opened the southern text again and continued reading. Nobody stormed the gates. Nobody gave speeches. The revolution is the gap between Kira's performed belief and her actual knowledge — a gap that exists in every workshop, in every apprentice who reads the contraband texts and nods through the ceremonies and waits. They're not angry. They're not defiant. They're just done. The structure stands. The faith holding it up has evaporated.
states.feels_like: The Quiet Revolution in Quebec. The Soviet Union's last generation — still attending party meetings, still reciting the slogans, already living in the world that comes after. The revolution nobody announces because the announcement would require a conviction that the revolutionaries have replaced with something quieter: certainty.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (performing authority — still issuing decrees, still holding ceremonies, unable to see that compliance is no longer belief)
the_quiet_ones (young smiths who read southern texts, experiment after hours, and say the right words at the right times)
the_foot_network (the unofficial town at the mountain's base — smuggling ideas, not goods)
active_gods: the_burn (dimming — not from unworship but from disbelief. The forge-fire still burns. The faith that made it sacred is cooling.)
the_silence (thriving — the gap between performed faith and actual faith is the Silence's natural habitat)
threats: the_performance_breaking (the revolution works because it's silent — if anyone speaks, the Council must respond, and response means confrontation that nobody wants)
the_master_who_notices (some masters aren't fooled — they see the gap but don't know what to do because punishing silence isn't possible)
the_burn_fading (if the forge-fire loses its sacred character, Thornwall's identity goes from 'we forge because it defines us' to 'we forge because we can' — the difference is everything)

key_figures.kira: Eighteen. Just took the Iron Oath. Felt nothing. She's not a rebel — she's a student who found better explanations. The southern texts describe forge-work with precision the Iron Doctrine replaces with mysticism. Kira still loves forging. She loves it MORE now because she understands it. But the understanding is heresy, and the love is private, and the gap between what she says at ceremonies and what she knows at the anvil is the Silent Revolution in one person. She doesn't want to overthrow anything. She just wants to forge with her eyes open.
key_figures.master_oren: Fifty-three. Kira's master. He watched her take the Oath and believed she meant it. He's wrong. He's also the finest smith in the upper forge and a genuine believer in the Doctrine — not from ignorance but from faith. He's SEEN things at the anvil that measurement can't explain. Metal that behaves differently when he's focused. Blades that come out stronger when he's present. He believes the mystery is real because for him it is. The tragedy: he might be right. The Burn might genuinely respond to devotion. But Kira's generation will never test this because they've already decided it's superstition. What if the thing they're discarding isn't ignorance but a genuine phenomenon they've dismissed because the southern framework has no category for it?
key_figures.the_foot_smuggler: Name: Pel. Runs texts up from The Foot — southern metallurgy papers, philosophical tracts, occasionally poetry. She's not ideological. She's a businesswoman. The young smiths pay in forge-scraps, which she sells in the south. She's the revolution's logistics. She doesn't care about the Doctrine or the texts — she cares about the margin. The fact that Thornwall's revolution depends on a profit motive would horrify both sides.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge hollowing from within — authority without belief)
worship_economy (the Burn dimming — what happens to a god when its worshippers perform faith without feeling it?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: kiras_gap — the space between performed belief and actual knowledge. Resolution: the gap widens until the walls crack (→Walls Crack), or the quiet ones leave before confrontation (→Northern Diaspora)
sub_sub_threads: master_orens_mystery (what if the Doctrine is partly real and the revolutionaries are throwing out the baby?)
pels_margin (the revolution as a business — logistics funded by profit, not principle)

states.name: The Long Siege
states.condition: External threat (Ashfield god or Tideborn) + Thornwall doubles down on isolation
states.tension: Wynn is nine. She was born inside the siege. She has never seen sky without walls around it. She draws pictures of what she thinks the outside looks like based on her grandfather's stories: green things growing from the ground (she's never seen a tree), water that moves sideways (she's only seen rain, never a river), and a yellow circle in the sky that her grandfather says is warm (she knows the forge heat but not the sun). Her drawings are wrong in ways that break her grandfather's heart. Thornwall was built to endure. It endures. The Ashfield god presses from the east — not attacking, just being, the way weather is. The Tideborn ships confuse from the west — are they threat or rescue? The Senate probes from the south with trade offers that arrive by arrow because nobody answers the gate. Thornwall survives on stored ore, recycled water, and the Iron Doctrine's one remaining argument: the walls are still standing. They can hold for decades. Generations. The forges still run. The masters still teach. The routines still hold. But the routines have become the world — there is nothing outside the routine because there is nothing outside the walls. The siege isn't an event anymore. It's the only reality the children know.
states.feels_like: Constantinople's final centuries, but seen through a child's eyes. A fortress that's outlived its purpose but not its pride. The tragedy isn't the siege — it's the normalization. When the extraordinary becomes ordinary, when endurance becomes identity, when children draw pictures of trees they've never seen and get the colors wrong.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (governing a sealed fortress — total authority within, zero influence without)
the_wall_keepers (the soldiers who maintain the perimeter — the siege's priesthood)
the_inside_children (the generation born in the siege — they have no concept of 'outside' as a real place)
active_gods: the_burn (sustained by the forges — the one god that works perfectly in a sealed mountain. The Burn has never been more focused or more confined.)
the_silence (growing in the sealed spaces between walls — Thornwall is the quietest place in the world from outside)
threats: normalization (the siege becoming the only world the children know — when you've never seen outside, you can't want it)
resource_decay (stored ore doesn't last forever — the forges are working from diminishing stock)
the_gate_question (eventually someone will have to open the gate. The decision will be the most terrifying moment in Thornwall's history because nobody inside knows what's on the other side anymore)

key_figures.wynn: Nine years old. Born inside the siege. She knows: the forge quarter, the upper town, the wall walk, the rain cisterns, the sound of hammering, the smell of hot iron. She doesn't know: trees, rivers, sunlight that isn't filtered through arrow slits, the sound of wind that hasn't bounced off stone. She draws pictures of outside based on her grandfather's descriptions. Her trees are grey because she's only seen stone and metal. Her river goes straight because she's only seen water in channels.
