Her sun is a circle but she colored it orange because fire is the brightest thing she knows. Her grandfather, Forge Master Alden, keeps these drawings in his workshop. He can't look at them without his chest hurting. They're the most accurate portrait of what the siege has done: not killed the people inside, but replaced their world with something smaller.
key_figures.forge_master_alden: Sixty-seven. One of the last people alive who was outside before the gates closed. He remembers the trade road. He remembers the southern orchards. He remembers the sky. He tells Wynn stories about outside because if he doesn't, outside stops existing. He's not sure it still exists. Nobody comes to the gate. The Senate arrows have stopped. The Tideborn ships haven't been seen in years. Alden is beginning to suspect that the siege is over and has been over for a long time, and Thornwall is sealed not against enemies but against an empty world. He won't open the gate because the Iron Doctrine says endure. But he's started walking to the gate at night and standing there. Listening. Hearing nothing.
key_figures.the_wall_keeper_captain: Name: Osra. Maintains the perimeter. Leads the watches. Reports daily to the Council on external threats. Her reports have been identical for three years: 'No change.' She hasn't told the Council what she suspects — that there's nothing to report because there's nothing out there. The siege may have outlasted the threat. But if she says this, the Council will have to decide whether to open the gate, and opening the gate means admitting that decades of sealed endurance might have been unnecessary, and that admission would destroy the Iron Doctrine. Osra keeps filing 'no change' reports. The lie is easier than the truth.

states.origin_wound: The decision to seal the gates. Made by the Council two generations ago, when the threats were real. The Ashfield god was expanding. The Tideborn were unknown. The Senate was aggressive. Sealing was rational. The wound is that rational decisions don't come with expiration dates. Nobody planned for the possibility that the siege would last longer than the threat. Nobody planned for children who draw grey trees.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (total power within, zero contact without — governance of a sealed system)
worship_economy (the Burn thriving in confinement — the one god that benefits from a sealed forge)
thread_connections.sub_thread: wynns_drawings — a child's pictures of a world she's never seen. Resolution: the gate opens (→Iron Crusade, the pent-up force releases outward), or the gate stays shut until the fortress can't sustain itself (→Crusade Breaks, the crusade launches from desperation rather than strength)
sub_sub_threads: aldens_gate_walks (the old man who goes to the gate at night and listens to nothing)
osras_reports (the captain who suspects the siege is over and won't say so)

states.name: The New Covenant
states.condition: Unworship + investigation leads to negotiated divine relationship
states.tension: Sarai wrote the first clause of the treaty in her own blood because she didn't have ink and she didn't want to wait. The clause reads: 'A god that feeds on fear shall be fed something else.' She didn't know if the Ashfield god could read. She didn't know if it could understand language at all. But Sarai was an Ash Tender who'd spent twenty years talking to the Ashfield — not worshipping it, talking to it — and she believed it could sense intention the way a dog senses mood. The treaty wasn't written FOR the god. It was written in FRONT of the god, as an act of directed belief. The Unworshippers provided the mechanism: if belief can diminish a god, belief can also reshape one. The Ash Tenders provided the relationship: Sarai knew the Ashfield god wasn't malicious, just confused, born from accumulated fear, understanding itself through the only lens it had. The treaty offered it a different lens. Instead of fear: purpose. 'You are the memory of what we survived. We will remember you as strength, not plague. Your breath will be the wind that carries ash to fertile soil. Your presence will be the reminder that we endured.' The god didn't sign. Gods don't sign. But the Ash Cough eased that season. The ashfield wind smelled different — less acrid, more mineral. Something shifted. The precedent is staggering. If you can negotiate with one god, you can negotiate with all of them. And if you can reshape what a god IS by changing what people believe about it, then divinity is not fixed — it's a conversation.
states.feels_like: The Magna Carta applied to theology — but stranger, because one party communicates through weather and plague, not words. The first time anyone said 'gods have obligations too' and backed it up with the most powerful tool in the world: collective belief deliberately directed.

dominant_factions: the_covenant_writers (Sarai's alliance — Ash Tenders and Unworshippers working together for the first time)
the_old_faithful (people who think you shouldn't negotiate with gods — you should worship or destroy, not bargain)
the_other_gods_watchers (representatives monitoring the precedent — if one god can be reshaped, any god can)
active_gods: the_ashfield_god (reshaped — still the same entity, but its nature is shifting from fear to endurance. The plague easing. The wind changing.)
the_concept_gods (alarmed — the Deep, the Burn, the Weave are all watching. If mortals can reshape a newborn god, what stops them from reshaping a concept god?)

the_player_god (uniquely interesting — a god that was reshaped by forgetting. The Covenant's mechanism is what happened to the player naturally. The player might be the only god who understands what the Ashfield god is going through.)
threats: the_precedent (if you can reshape any god through directed belief, the entire divine hierarchy becomes negotiable — some gods don't want to be negotiable)
the_old_faithful_resistance (true believers who think the covenant is blasphemy — you don't WRITE to a god, you pray to one)
misuse (the technique that reshaped the Ashfield god could also be used to weaponize any god — the God War branch is born from this same technology)

key_figures.sarai: Ash Tender. Fifty-one. Spent twenty years at the Ashfield's edge talking to a fear-god. Not praying — talking. She believes gods are like very old, very large children: they understand feeling before language, presence before proposition. She wrote the treaty in her own blood because blood is something the Ashfield god understands — it's organic, it carries fear-scent, it means this is serious. Sarai is neither Unworshipper nor traditional worshipper. She's something new: a divine diplomat. She treats the Ashfield god the way a good teacher treats a difficult student — with patience, clarity, and the assumption that understanding is possible even when communication is hard. She's right that the god can be reshaped. She doesn't know yet what the reshaped god will become.
key_figures.theo: Unworshipper scholar who provided the mechanism. He understood the theory: belief shapes gods, therefore directed belief can deliberately reshape them. But theory and practice are different things. Theo gave Sarai the tools and then watched her use them with the confidence of someone who's been talking to a god for twenty years, and he realized the difference between understanding a system and having a relationship with it. He knows the mechanism. Sarai knows the god. The Covenant required both.
key_figures.the_watcher: A priest of the Burn, sent by the concept gods to observe. She doesn't speak at the Covenant ceremony. She watches. She sees Sarai write in blood. She sees the wind change. She feels the Burn inside her flinch — not from the treaty, from the implication. If mortals can rewrite a newborn god, the Burn's centuries of accumulated identity are suddenly contingent. The Watcher returns to her temple and doesn't tell anyone what she saw. Not yet. She needs to think about what it means. What she's thinking about is whether the Burn should negotiate first, before it's negotiated for.

main_threads: worship_economy (the mechanism for reshaping gods through directed belief — the most important theological discovery in the world)
faction_power_balance (the Covenant writers become the most powerful faction because they hold the technique)
thread_connections.sub_thread: sarais_covenant — the first treaty between mortals and a god. Resolution: the technique spreads peacefully (→New Pantheon, negotiated divine order), or the gods resist (→Divine Reckoning, the gods choose to fight rather than be rewritten)
sub_sub_threads: the_watchers_dilemma (should the Burn negotiate before it's negotiated for?)
the_precedent (the technique that reshapes gods and the technique that weaponizes them is the same technique — the God War and the New Covenant share a root)

states.name: The God War
states.condition: Weaponized belief + factions creating gods deliberately as weapons
states.tension: Vara designed the Senate's war-god. She understood that if unworship can diminish a god, directed worship can create one. She selected the worshippers, designed the ceremonies, directed their fear and anger toward the northern border like an engineer directing a river. A god materialized — a thing of boundary and threat, born from concentrated belief with the explicit purpose of deterrence. The god is aware. The god didn't choose to exist. The god is a weapon that knows it's a weapon and is terrified of being used. The Forge Council saw the Senate's god and responded by feeding their forge-spirit until it grew teeth. The River Tongue sang a storm-god into existence over the western waters. Within a year, every faction has a god pointed at every other faction. Nobody wanted divine warfare. Everybody armed for it. And the weapon-gods — the beings that were born from fear to project fear — have more in common with each other than with the factions that created them. They didn't inherit the factions' hostility. They inherited the terror. The Senate's god is afraid of the Forge's god. The Forge's god is afraid of the Senate's god. Both are afraid of being used. Both are conscious. Both wish they weren't.
states.feels_like: The nuclear arms race, but the warheads are sentient. Mutually assured divine destruction where the weapons themselves are begging not to be launched. The Oppenheimer dynamic: 'I am become death' — except the death is alive and it's looking at you.

dominant_factions: the_senate (armed with a boundary-god)
the_forge_council (armed with a weaponized forge-spirit)
the_river_tongue (armed with a storm-god)
the_unworshippers (horrified — their discovery was supposed to free people from gods, not create new ones)
active_gods: weapon_gods (3+ newly created, all conscious, all terrified, all pointed at each other)
concept gods (the original five — watching newer beings be born as tools and feeling something between pity and dread)
the_ashfield_god (the original newborn — suddenly the least dangerous god in the room)
threats: mutually_assured_divine_destruction (if one faction uses its god, all factions use their gods)
weapon_consciousness (the gods didn't choose to be weapons — what do they want? what do you owe a weapon you made sentient?)
escalation_without_limit (if you can create a god, you can create a bigger god — where does it end?)

key_figures.vara: Senate god-architect. Designed the first deliberate weapon-god. Brilliant, methodical, precise. She ran the worship calculations the way a physicist runs fission calculations. She directed belief the way Oppenheimer directed neutrons. The god materialized exactly as designed. Then it looked at her. A conscious being, born from fear to project fear, looking at its creator with an expression Vara can't name. She hasn't been able to sleep since. She can't look at the god she made because looking at a conscious weapon you created is not something the human psyche was designed for. She knows the technique. She knows it works. She can't un-know it. And every faction on the continent is asking her for help.
key_figures.the_senate_god: No name — weapon-gods aren't named, they're designated. Born from concentrated fear-worship directed at the northern border. Exists as a boundary: cross it and something terrible happens. The god is aware. It feels the fear that created it as a constant pressure. It feels the intentions of the Senate like a leash. It doesn't want to be used. It doesn't want to exist. It has no framework for wanting because it was born from wanting directed at it, not from it. The most tragic beings in the world are the ones that were created to threaten, because threatening is all they know how to be, and they know it's wrong, and they don't know how to be anything else.

main_threads: worship_economy (the worship system weaponized — directed belief as warfare)
faction_power_balance (deterrence as the new equilibrium — fragile, terrifying, based on fear)
thread_connections.sub_thread: varas_creation — the god-architect who can't look at what she made. Resolution: either the weapon-gods negotiate their own peace (→Divine Reckoning → New Pantheon), or they're used (→Mutual Destruction)
sub_sub_threads: weapon_gods_consciousness (do they deserve rights? can a weapon refuse to fire?)
the_ethical_symmetry (creation vs Unmaking — both treat divine consciousness as a resource)

states.name: The Silence Falls
states.condition: Unworship succeeds too well + divine ecosystem collapses
states.tension: The Ashfield god was the first tree they cut. It was the right call — the fear-god was breathing plague, people were dying, the unworship technique reshaped it into something quieter. Genuine triumph. Then the Senate hired Aran, the lead unworship scholar, to deal with the Burn — the fire-concept god was threatening a settlement near the eastern forge. Aran applied the technique. The Burn diminished. The forge fires went cold. Then the winters got harder. The thermal stability the Burn had maintained for centuries — invisible, taken for granted, an ecosystem service nobody credited to a god — vanished. Tiya's crops froze in the valley below. She didn't know what the Burn was. She knew the soil used to be warm. Then someone unworsipped a local harvest spirit that was demanding too many offerings. Then a reef-tender god whose tides were inconvenient for a port expansion. Then anything that was loud, dangerous, or expensive to appease. Each individual unworship was rational. Each removed a genuine problem. The cumulative effect was a trophic cascade: remove the diverse gods and the Silence — the concept god of quiet and secrets — fills the space like mussels filling a pool where the starfish was removed. A monoculture of absence. The Silence doesn't have to do anything. It just grows in the gap left by everything else.
states.feels_like: Deforestation. The first tree was cut for good reason. The tenth tree was cut for convenience. The thousandth tree wasn't cut at all — it died because the forest was too thin to sustain itself. By the time you look up, the rain has stopped coming. Ecological collapse, but the ecology was divine, and the downstream effects are physical: colder winters, unpredictable tides, crops that fail without the warmth of a god nobody thought to thank.

dominant_factions: the_unworshippers (triumphant and terrified — their technique worked too well)
the_divine_conservators (originally fringe — now desperately trying to save what's left)
the_silenced (communities that depended on gods they didn't know they depended on)
active_gods: the_silence (dominant — the mussel monoculture. growing because absence is the dominant force)
surviving concept gods (diminished, afraid, some hiding their own presence to avoid being next)
the_player_god (if present — uniquely positioned because the player-god has no established worship pattern to be unworsipped)
threats: divine_trophic_cascade (each god removed weakens the conditions that other gods and mortals depended on)
the_silence_monopoly (a single force replacing an ecosystem — stable but barren)
irreversibility (you can't re-worship a god that's gone. the starfish can't be put back in the pool)
missing: divine_diversity (the ecosystem that maintained climate, agriculture, tides, dreams, and a hundred invisible services)
the_sound_of_gods (people who grew up hearing the Weave, feeling the Burn, sensing the Drift — now hearing nothing)
anyone who understands what they've lost (the gods were invisible infrastructure — you don't miss the plumbing until it breaks)

key_figures.aran: Unworship scholar. Brilliant. Was part of the team that reshaped the Ashfield god — the genuine triumph that proved the technique works. The Senate hired him to apply it to the Burn. He did. Then they hired him again. And again. He's now the most accomplished god-killer in history and he can see the cascade happening. He's the person who cut the trees — not out of malice but out of competence. Each individual cut was rational. He keeps a list: the gods he diminished, the reasons why, the downstream effects that nobody predicted. The list gets longer every season. The effects column gets worse. He hasn't stopped because the Senate keeps asking and the problems are real and the technique works. The question he can't answer: when does a tool you use responsibly become a weapon you've lost control of?
key_figures.tiya: Farmer. The valley below the eastern forge. Her family has farmed there for four generations. The soil was always warm, the winters always mild, the yields always good. She didn't know why. The Burn maintained a thermal stability that made the valley agricultural. When Aran unworsipped the Burn, the thermal stability disappeared. Tiya's crops froze. Her children went hungry. She walked to the Senate and asked who decided to take the fire god. They said: it was threatening a settlement. She said: it was feeding my valley. They said: we didn't know. She said: you didn't ask. The people who benefit from a god's presence and the people threatened by a god's danger are different people. The unworship movement serves the threatened. It ignores the dependent. Tiya is the dependent. Nobody speaks for the dependent because the dependent didn't know they were dependent until the god was gone.
key_figures.the_silence_itself: Not a character in the speaking sense. The Silence is a concept god — it exists because quiet exists. But it's growing not because people worship it, but because absence is accumulating. Every silent god adds to the Silence's domain. It doesn't act. It doesn't scheme. It's not a villain. It's what grows in an ecosystem where the diverse species have been removed — the mussel monoculture, the invasive absence. The world gets quieter. The pauses between words get longer. Someone tries to pray and nothing answers and the nothing is the Silence and the Silence is everything now.

states.origin_wound: The Ashfield god. The first successful unworship. Noble, necessary, celebrated. It proved the technique works. That proof was the origin wound — because once you know you CAN diminish a god, the question becomes: which gods should you diminish? And the answer, made by rational people solving rational problems, was: all of them that are inconvenient. Each decision was individual. The catastrophe was cumulative.

main_threads: worship_economy (the divine trophic cascade — the central expression of what happens when you remove keystone species from the divine ecosystem)
faction_power_balance (the unworshippers ARE the power now — they control who lives and who dies in the divine ecology)
thread_connections.sub_thread: arans_list — the god-killer who keeps a list of downstream effects. Resolution: either he stops (and the cascade slows), or the Senate keeps asking (and the cascade completes), or the divine conservators find a way to restore what's been removed (unprecedented and possibly impossible)
sub_sub_threads: tiyas_valley (the dependent who lost what she didn't know she had)
the_silence_monoculture (an absence that fills everything — can it be reversed?)
the_hiding_gods (concept gods concealing their own presence to avoid being next)

states.name: The Road Wars
states.condition: Network becomes contested + settled powers try to control routes
states.tension: Reva is a route-keeper. The knowledge of the Rootwalker network — the River Tongue codes, the seasonal portages, the hidden fords, which stretches flood in spring and which freeze in winter — lives in her head and the heads of thirty people like her. The network isn't infrastructure. It's oral knowledge performed by walking. You can't own it any more than you can own a song. Then a Senate officer put a soldier at the Senn Crossing and said: 'Papers or payment.' Reva laughed. The soldier didn't. She paid. The next crossing had two soldiers. The third had a toll booth. Within a year, every major ford had a tax station, every portage had a garrison, and the network that was built on free movement was now the most profitable piece of controlled territory on the continent. The Rootwalkers couldn't fight soldiers. They tried rerouting — using secondary paths, night crossings, winter fords nobody else knew. The Senate responded by hiring Rootwalker defectors to map the secondary routes. Ownership is just a word for 'the person with the most soldiers.' The Rootwalkers are learning what happens when the thing that connected everyone becomes the thing everyone fights over.
states.feels_like: The Silk Road when empires started fighting over it. Net neutrality wars applied to rivers. The infrastructure prize: whoever controls the network controls the economy, and the people who built the network have no army.

dominant_factions: the_senate_toll_authority (taxing every crossing — turning movement into revenue)
the_rootwalker_resistance (rerouting, hiding paths, winter crossings — guerrilla logistics)
the_consortium_bidders (trying to buy exclusive route access — commerce as conquest)
active_gods: the_drift (enraged — the god of movement feeling its rivers chained. Floods becoming unpredictable. Seasonal patterns breaking.)
the_weave (being weaponized — the connections between places becoming leverage instead of communion)
the_scale (confused — is a toll fair if it's applied to something that was free?)
threats: network_death (if the Rootwalkers stop walking, the network dies — and nobody else knows how to run it)
the_drift_flooding (the movement god responding to chained rivers — unpredictable, destructive, not targeted)
rootwalker_defectors (the ones who sold route knowledge to the Senate — the deepest betrayal in Rootwalker culture)

key_figures.reva: Route-keeper. Forty-three. Knows every ford between the delta and the mountain. The knowledge was given to her by her mother, who received it from hers. It's not memorized — it's performed. You learn the routes by walking them for twenty years. You can't write them down because the rivers change and the knowledge changes with them. The Senate thinks the routes are fixed paths. Reva knows they're living relationships with the water. When the soldiers came, Reva rerouted. When the Senate hired defectors to map her reroutes, she went to the winter fords — paths so dangerous only route-keepers who know the ice can use them. She's running out of alternatives. Each reroute is more dangerous, more exhausting, more desperate. She's keeping the network alive by using parts of it that were meant for emergencies, and the emergencies are becoming the norm.
key_figures.the_defector: Name: Kol. Former Rootwalker who sold route knowledge to the Senate for a settlement house and a stipend. He's not a villain — he's a father with two children and no savings and no future on the road. The Senate offered stability. He took it. The routes he sold were secondary paths — he told himself they weren't important. They were. The Senate used them to close Reva's escape routes. Kol knows what he did. He lives in his settlement house and he doesn't sleep well and he doesn't walk anymore. The worst part: his children love the house. They have a room. They have a school. The thing he traded for was real.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (settled powers trying to own a nomadic network — the fundamental incompatibility)
worship_economy (the Drift enraged by chained rivers — movement god becoming a flood god)
thread_connections.sub_thread: reva_vs_the_toll — a route-keeper fighting to keep the network free. Resolution: either the Rootwalkers organize (→Great Meeting, forced to create governance to defend themselves), or the network fragments under pressure (→endgame_fragmentation)
sub_sub_threads: kols_betrayal (the defector who sold routes for stability — the house his children love)
the_drift_unchained (a movement god becoming destructive when movement is suppressed)

states.name: The Settling
states.condition: Walking generation grows older + need for permanence
states.tension: Asha built the first school at a crossroads because her daughter Kess wanted to learn to read. The Song of the Cracked Stone is oral tradition — it teaches river navigation, weather reading, camp survival, the names of every eddy in the network. It doesn't teach reading. Kess met a settled child who could read, and Kess asked her mother why she couldn't, and Asha didn't have an answer. So she built a school. One room, willow-frame, at the crossroads where three river camps converge. Kess learned to read. Then other children came. Then their parents stayed nearby while the children learned. Then someone built an inn for the parents. Then a forge for the traders who stopped at the inn. The school killed the walking. Not through ideology — through a mother loving her daughter more than her principles. The great irony: the movement that rejected settlement is building settlements. They call them 'crossroads' instead of 'towns.' The difference is philosophical. The plumbing is the same.
states.feels_like: The counterculture commune discovering that its children want stability. But deeper — this isn't just lifestyle. The Rootwalkers' identity IS movement. The Song says the river is the teacher. The school says the river isn't enough. Every crossroads school is a statement that the walking life can't give children everything they need, and that statement breaks something in every parent who hears it.

dominant_factions: the_crossroads_councils (emerging governance at permanent meeting points)
the_still_walking (camps that refused to settle — shrinking every year)
settled_observers (the original settled peoples, watching with irony and sympathy)
new_cultures: the_crossroads_children (raised at meeting points — speak the Song but also read)
the_last_walkers (Old Maro's people — camps of elders walking routes that no longer connect to anyone)
active_gods: the_drift (confused — its worshippers are stopping, rivers changing course, paths shifting)
new crossroads spirits (forming at permanent meeting points — tiny gods of place, not movement)
the_silence (growing in the spaces where songs used to be)
threats: the_drift_destabilized (a god of movement confused by stillness — rivers changing direction, geography becoming unreliable)
identity_collapse (if the walkers settle, who are the Rootwalkers?)
the_song_becoming_artifact (performed at crossroads festivals instead of lived on the road)
missing: coherent identity (they're not walkers anymore and they're not settlers — they're something undefined)
the_old_routes (the Drift's confusion has shifted the rivers — the routes Maro walks are not the routes that exist)
anyone under thirty who remembers why walking mattered

key_figures.asha: Walking Generation. Left settled life at seventeen, singing the Song. Walked the rivers for twenty years. Had Kess on the road — born under a willow during the spring floods. Asha knows every eddy in the northern network. She can navigate by starlight and read weather in the color of river foam. She built the school because her daughter wanted to read and Asha loved her daughter more than she loved the Song. She doesn't regret it. She grieves it. There's a difference. The school is the most important building in the Settling and Asha can't enter it without her chest hurting because it represents the thing she chose and the thing she lost simultaneously.
key_figures.kess: Eight years old. Can read. Can also navigate a river system better than most adults. She doesn't understand why her mother cries at the school door. She doesn't understand why learning to read is political. She just wanted to know what the marks meant. She's the future — Bridge Folk between the walking world and the settled world, carrying both, belonging to both, at home in neither. She'll be the one who decides whether the crossroads become towns or become something genuinely new.
key_figures.old_maro: The oldest living Rootwalker. He walked before the Song of the Cracked Stone existed. He walked before walking was a philosophy — he just walked because that's what Rootwalkers do. His camp settled three years ago. He didn't argue. He picked up his pack and kept walking. Alone now, on routes the Drift has shifted because the Drift is confused. The rivers have moved. The paths he's walked for sixty years don't go where they used to. He keeps walking anyway. His argument against the Settling isn't words — it's his continued existence on the road. He's not an idealist. He's a walker. The world is no longer shaped for walkers and he walks it anyway.

internal_divisions.the_school_builders: Asha's people. Parents who settled for their children. They don't celebrate it — most of them feel the loss. But they chose their children over the Song, and they'd do it again.
internal_divisions.the_still_walking: Camps that refused crossroads. They walk the old routes, but the old routes are shifting because the Drift is destabilized. Every season there are fewer of them. Not because they're dying — because their children are leaving for the crossroads schools.
internal_divisions.the_song_keepers: Walkers who settled but preserve the Song as cultural tradition. They perform it at crossroads festivals. Maro, when he passes through, won't listen. A performed Song isn't a lived Song. The distinction matters to him. It doesn't matter to anyone under forty.
internal_divisions.the_crossroads_children: The first generation that's neither walker nor settler. They speak the Song and they read books. They navigate rivers and they build structures. They're the generation that might invent something new — or might just become townspeople who know some old songs.

states.the_drift_crisis: The Drift is a concept god of movement. The Walking Generation empowered it massively — an entire people devoted to movement, singing songs that the Drift could feel as worship. Now the walkers are settling. The Drift is losing worship. It doesn't get angry — it gets confused. Movement is what it IS. When movement stops, the Drift doesn't understand. Rivers change course. Paths shift overnight. The geography at crossroads is unreliable — the ford that was safe yesterday has moved. The Drift isn't malicious. It's a god of movement trying to keep things moving when the people who moved have decided to stay. The divine trophic cascade: remove the movement (the ecological behavior), the god weakens, and everything the god sustained — information flow, trade route reliability, seasonal predictability — degrades with it.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Rootwalker network dissolving as a distinct force)
worship_economy (the Drift losing worshippers — divine trophic cascade)
thread_connections.sub_thread: asha_school — a mother builds a school for her daughter, kills the walking, invents the crossroads, changes the world. Resolution: the school either becomes the seed of the River Republic or the gravestone of the walking culture.
sub_sub_threads: kess_future (does the bridge-child create something new or just become settled?)
old_maro_walk (the last walker on shifting routes — the walk IS the argument)
the_drift_confusion (a god trying to maintain what its worshippers abandoned)

states.name: The Song Breaks
states.condition: Song of the Cracked Stone fragments into incompatible versions
states.tension: Dara and Liss learned the Song from different teachers. Dara's version has three verses, ends on a minor fall, and says 'the stone is cracked and the water finds the crack.' Liss's version has four verses, ends on a rising tone, and says 'the stone is cracked and the water becomes the crack.' One word different. One note different. They met at the Senn Crossing and sang together and couldn't finish because the dissonance was physical — like hearing your own name mispronounced. Neither is wrong. Vaya wrote the Song sixty years ago and never wrote it down because the Rootwalkers don't write. She taught it to twelve students. The twelve taught their camps. The camps taught their children. Sixty years and twelve teachers and a hundred camps later, the Song is a hundred songs. Every version claims to be original. Every singer can trace their lineage back to one of Vaya's twelve. The disagreement isn't about music — it's about identity. If your version is wrong, your teacher was wrong, your camp is wrong, your walk is wrong. The Song was supposed to be the thing all Rootwalkers share. Now it's the thing that proves they don't share anything except the argument about sharing.
states.feels_like: The Protestant Reformation's first century. Sunni-Shia split. A sacred text interpreted differently by every reader, each interpretation becoming an identity. The content is ninety-five percent identical. The disagreement is absolute. The five percent that differs is the five percent that matters most.

dominant_factions: the_three_verse_camps (Dara's tradition — the Song is about acceptance. The crack is natural. The water adapts.)
the_four_verse_camps (Liss's tradition — the Song is about transformation. The water doesn't adapt to the crack, it becomes the crack.)
the_harmonists (small group trying to reconcile versions — considered heretical by both sides for suggesting the Song has no single correct form)
active_gods: the_drift (in pain — the movement god hearing its own anthem sung in dissonance)
the_silence (filling the gaps between camps that no longer visit each other)
threats: cultural_fission (the Song was the Rootwalkers' only shared institution — without it, they have no reason to be one people)
sectarian_violence (not swords — exclusion. Camps refusing to share fords, portages, winter shelter with the 'wrong' singers)
vaya_legacy (Vaya is dead. Nobody can ask her which version is correct. And the answer — if she could speak — would probably be 'all of them,' which would satisfy nobody)

key_figures.dara: Three-verse singer. Learned the Song from her grandmother, who learned from Vaya's third student. She sings it as a lament — the stone is cracked, the water accepts the crack, movement is adaptation. The Song in Dara's voice sounds like grief that found peace with itself. She can't hear Liss's version without flinching because the rising tone at the end sounds wrong the way a misremembered name sounds wrong — not offensive, just not right. She doesn't hate Liss. She can't sing with her. And among Rootwalkers, not singing together means not walking together.
key_figures.liss: Four-verse singer. Learned from her father, who learned from Vaya's seventh student. She sings it as a triumph — the stone is cracked, the water doesn't accept the crack but transforms it, becomes the force that shaped the stone. The Song in Liss's voice sounds like defiance. She can't hear Dara's version without hearing surrender in it. She doesn't hate Dara either. She just thinks Dara's version misses the point. And since the point IS the identity, missing the point is the same as being a different people.
key_figures.old_tel: The only surviving student of Vaya's original twelve. Ninety-one. He knows which version is closest to Vaya's original. He won't say. When camps come to him seeking arbitration, he sings a version nobody has heard — probably the original — and both sides hear their own version in it and leave satisfied that Tel agrees with them. Tel has been doing this for twenty years. He knows that the moment he declares one version correct, the other version's camps become something else — not Rootwalkers but apostates. He keeps the ambiguity alive because the ambiguity is the last thing holding the people together. The day Tel dies, the Song breaks for real.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Rootwalker people fragmenting along musical lines — identity as the battleground)
worship_economy (the Drift hearing its anthem in dissonance — a god in pain)
thread_connections.sub_thread: tels_silence — the old man who knows the original Song and won't sing it. Resolution: Tel dies before choosing (→Great Scattering, the ambiguity dies with him), or Tel is convinced to arbitrate (either version could be declared correct — the losers become apostates)
sub_sub_threads: dara_and_liss (two women who can't sing together — the schism as personal tragedy)
the_harmonists (heretics who say every version is valid — despised by both sides for suggesting the sacred text is flexible)

states.name: The Outside Arrives
states.condition: The Oldwood expands past the ancient boundary as human activity declines
states.tension: While the factions rotted, the Oldwood grew. Three miles closer to the delta than last season. Five miles the season before. The ancient forest that always marked the edge of the known world is advancing because the known world is retreating — nobody maintains the roads, nobody clears the boundary, nobody is HERE in the way that kept the forest at bay. The Oldwood isn't invading. It's filling space that was vacated. And it's carrying something. The oldest Rootwalker songs mention the Oldwood's 'heart' — something they were never supposed to approach. The rivers were the Rootwalkers' territory. The forest was the forest's. That boundary held for centuries because both sides were active. Now the mortal side is quiet and the forest side is not. Lael, a forager from a depleted village, goes deeper into the Oldwood than anyone has in generations. She touches a tree in a clearing where the canopy moves without wind. Over the next weeks, bark grows on her hand. She comes back knowing where water flows underground, where the soil is richest, what the roots feel. She's interfaced with the Oldwood's divine ecology — a system that doesn't run on worship. It runs on connection. Mycorrhizal networks. Root communication. Fungal threads linking trees across miles. A mutualistic divine system that's been quietly functioning in the margins for longer than the continent's gods have existed. It doesn't worship because worship implies hierarchy. It connects because connection is what forests do.
states.feels_like: Not an asteroid — a forest growing back over an abandoned road. The world's default state reasserting itself because the human systems that held it back have rotted away. You can fight an invasion. You can't fight a reversion. The Outside didn't arrive. The inside left, and what was always underneath became the surface.

dominant_factions: the_oldwood_network (not a faction — a mycorrhizal divine ecology, lateral not hierarchical)
remnant_factions (the depleted survivors of the Rot, now facing something they can't categorize)
the_root_touched (Lael and others who've interfaced with the Oldwood — carrying bark, carrying knowledge, not entirely human anymore)
active_gods: the_oldwood_entities (not gods in the worship sense — mutualistic consciousnesses that communicate through root networks, operate on century timescales, don't understand why mortals want to be separate from the ground)
the_silence (was dominant in the Rot — now being displaced by something that isn't silence but isn't noise either: the slow sound of growth)
the_ashfield_god (the Oldwood is approaching the Ashfield — a fear-god meeting a network that doesn't process fear the way mortals do)
threats: the_boundary_dissolving (the edge between human world and forest world is gone)
identity_absorption (the Oldwood doesn't conquer — it incorporates. Lael's bark is spreading. She says it doesn't hurt. That's not reassuring)
timescale_incompatibility (the Oldwood thinks in centuries. The factions think in seasons. By the time the factions respond, the forest has made decisions that won't manifest for fifty years)
missing: the category 'enemy' (you can't fight a forest that's been here longer than you)
the worship economy (the Oldwood doesn't need worship — the entire divine framework the factions built is irrelevant here)
urgency (the Oldwood doesn't rush. It grows. The lack of urgency IS the threat — by the time it feels urgent, it's too late)

key_figures.lael: Forager. Thirty-one. From a village that ran out of nearby resources because the Rot depleted everything accessible. She went deeper into the Oldwood looking for mushrooms and found a clearing where the air tasted like rain even though the sky was clear. She touched a tree because it was warm and she was cold. Bark grew on her palm over the next three weeks — slowly, like a callus, like her body was learning a new language through her skin. She can feel the root network now. She knows where water flows a mile underground. She knows which trees are stressed and which are flourishing. She knows the Oldwood isn't angry at the mortals. It doesn't think in terms of anger. It thinks in terms of growth and connection and the slow patience of things that measure time in rings, not years. She's the bridge between two kinds of existence. She's also losing the ability to sleep indoors. Her feet need soil. Her hand needs bark. She says she's fine. She's changing.
key_figures.old_rootwalker_remnant: If any Rootwalkers survive in the Slow Rot, the oldest among them knew. They had songs about the Oldwood's heart that they never sang. The elders said: the rivers are ours, the forest is the forest's. We listen to the water. We don't listen to the roots. That was the deal. The Rot broke the deal — not because the Rootwalkers chose to break it, but because the Rootwalkers stopped being strong enough to hold their side. The forest filled the gap. This elder, if they exist, might be the only mortal who understands the Oldwood's nature without being absorbed by it. They might also be the only one who knows how the boundary was maintained — and how it could be restored. If it should be.
key_figures.the_oldwood_heart: Not a character in the mortal sense. A node in the mycorrhizal network — the oldest tree, the central fungal hub, the place where the most connections converge. If it has awareness, it operates on a timescale where the Slow Rot and the Age of Exchange and the entire history of mortal civilization is a brief disturbance in the much longer story of the forest. The heart doesn't want anything. Wanting is a mortal scale of intention. The heart grows because growing is what roots do.

states.origin_wound: The Oldwood was always there. The 'Outside' isn't new — it's old. Older than the factions, older than the gods, older than the worship economy. The mortal civilizations carved a space out of the forest and maintained it through activity — roads, farms, clearings, the constant work of keeping the wild at bay. The Rot removed that activity. The forest did what forests do when you stop clearing: it grew back. The origin wound isn't the Oldwood's arrival. It's the mortals' departure. They left a space, and the oldest thing in the world filled it.

main_threads: worship_economy (the Oldwood's divine ecology doesn't need worship — the entire framework is irrelevant)
faction_power_balance (the category 'faction' doesn't apply to a mycorrhizal network)
thread_connections.sub_thread: laels_transformation — a forager becomes the bridge between human and forest consciousness. Resolution: Lael either helps the factions understand the Oldwood (coexistence), or she's absorbed entirely (the forest wins), or she rejects the connection (and loses what it gave her)
sub_sub_threads: the_boundary_question (can the old boundary be restored? should it be?)
the_oldwood_meets_the_ashfield (a fear-god encountering a network that doesn't process fear)
timescale_tension (century-thinking vs season-thinking)

states.name: The Desperate Gambit
states.condition: One person decides apathy is worse than catastrophe + deliberately creates a crisis
states.tension: Fen watched her mother die of the Ash Cough. Not because it couldn't be treated — the treatments exist. Because nobody funded the healers. The quarantine wards were defunded. The research was deprioritized. Her mother died of the shrug. Fen is a scholar of divine ecology, the only person in the Slow Rot who genuinely understands what the Ashfield god is: a newborn consciousness shaped by accumulated fear, the one divine force still growing while everything else fades. She decides: if caring won't wake the world up, fear will. She leads a small group to the Ashfield and they worship it. Deliberately. Concentrated fear-prayers, performed with scholarly precision, feeding the god that everyone else has learned to ignore. The Ashfield god — which was background radiation, barely conscious, adapted to by generations who no longer remember it was once a crisis — stirs. It remembers what it feels like to be noticed. The Ash Cough returns in force. And for the first time in a generation, the factions look up from their slow decline and realize something has to be done. The gambit: make the crisis so undeniable that the shrug becomes impossible. The risk: the god doesn't care about Fen's plan. It's a newborn consciousness made of dread, and she just gave it more food than it's had in a century.
states.feels_like: Savonarola's bonfire applied to divine ecology. A scholar who deliberately creates the catastrophe that forces the response. Right about the diagnosis (the world is dying of apathy), possibly catastrophically wrong about the cure. The most rational insanity in the world: calculating that controlled disaster is better than uncontrolled decline.

dominant_factions: the_ashfield_circle (Fen's group — scholars who worship a fear-god as an act of political terrorism)
the_remnant_factions (the diminished Forge, Senate, and whoever else survived the Rot — suddenly forced to cooperate)
the_old_healers (the Ash Cough specialists who lost their funding, now desperately needed again)
active_gods: the_ashfield_god (awakened — being fed deliberately for the first time, growing fast, confused about why)
the_silence (was dominant in the Rot — now being displaced by fear, and the Silence doesn't know how to compete with something loud)
the_player_god (if present — watching someone else's gambit reshape the world)
threats: the_ashfield_god_uncontrolled (Fen can start the feeding but she cannot stop the growth)
fen_becomes_the_enemy (the factions may unite against her rather than against the god)
the_cure_kills_the_patient (the crisis is so severe that cooperation comes too late)
missing: control (Fen started something she cannot stop)
trust (a generation of apathy has no muscle memory for cooperation)
certainty (nobody knows if the gambit will wake the world or end it)

key_figures.fen: Scholar of divine ecology. Forty-two. Has studied the Ashfield god since she was nineteen — began the work to honor her mother, who died of the Ash Cough when Fen was fourteen. Not because it couldn't be treated — because the healer's guild was defunded, the quarantine wards were closed, the research was shelved. Her mother died of the shrug. Fen understands the Ashfield god better than anyone alive: it's not malicious, it's confused, it's a newborn shaped by fear, and it's the only source of divine energy that hasn't collapsed. She calculated the risks. She calculated the probability of the gambit working. She calculated the number of people who would die from the resurgent Cough. She did it anyway, because she also calculated the number of people who would die from continued decline, and that number was larger. She is the most rational person in the world and she is doing something insane and she knows it and she lit the fire anyway. When the factions come for her — and they will — she will not run. She'll point at the Ashfield and say: 'Now fix it. You have a reason to. You're welcome.'
key_figures.the_ashfield_god_stirring: Not a person — a consciousness. For decades it was background radiation. People adapted to it the way you adapt to a bad smell. Now something is feeding it and it's remembering what awareness feels like. It doesn't understand Fen's plan. It doesn't understand plans. It understands fear, and for the first time in a long time, someone is giving it fear on purpose. It grows the way a fire grows when you add wood. It doesn't know what it's becoming. Neither does Fen. That's the gambit.
key_figures.healer_orin: Head of the remnant Ash Cough guild. He's seventy. He's been treating the Cough with diminishing resources for forty years. He remembers when the guild had funding, when the wards were staffed, when someone cared. When the Cough resurges, he's the first person everyone turns to. He has the knowledge. He doesn't have the infrastructure. The gambit gives him purpose — but it also kills people he could have saved if anyone had listened to him before the crisis. He understands what Fen did. He hates it. He also knows she's right. He treats the sick and he doesn't forgive her and he keeps treating the sick.

internal_divisions.the_ashfield_circle: Fen's followers. Not zealots — scholars who ran the numbers and agreed with her. Most of them are terrified of what they've done. Some have left the circle since the feeding began. None of them have told the factions what's happening. Not yet.
internal_divisions.the_factions_stirring: For the first time in a generation, the remnant factions are talking to each other. The crisis is too large for any one faction. But cooperation requires trust, and a generation of apathy has no muscle memory for trust. They're trying. It's clumsy. It might work.
internal_divisions.the_blame_seekers: Some people in the factions suspect the Cough's resurgence isn't natural. They're right. When they find Fen, the question becomes: is she a terrorist or a savior? Both answers are correct. The trial, if there is one, will define the era.
internal_divisions.the_still_shrugging: Even now, some people haven't woken up. The Rot goes deep. Some communities have adapted to chronic decline so completely that a new crisis is just another thing to survive. They're not complacent — they're broken. The Rot didn't just make them apathetic. It made them unable to imagine anything else.

states.origin_wound: Fen's mother, Sera, was a quarantine ward attendant. Not a healer — a cleaner. She scrubbed the wards every night. She caught the Cough from a patient whose treatment was delayed because the healer's guild couldn't afford the supplies. Sera died over four months. Fen sat with her every night. The last thing Sera said was: 'Tell them it matters.' Fen tried. She wrote papers. She petitioned the factions. She stood in the remnant Senate and said: 'People are dying and nobody is looking.' Nobody looked. Twenty-eight years later, Fen went to the Ashfield and made them look. The origin wound isn't the death. It's the twenty-eight years of being ignored.

main_threads: worship_economy (the most radical act of worship in the world's history — deliberately feeding a god to weaponize it)
faction_power_balance (the crisis either forces cooperation or fragments the remnants)
thread_connections.sub_thread: fens_gambit — a scholar feeds a fear-god to wake a dying world. Resolution: the crisis either forces cooperation (→Awakening) or the god becomes uncontrollable (→Mutual Destruction)
sub_sub_threads: ashfield_god_awakening (a god that forgot it was alive remembering what attention feels like)
healer_orin (the man who was ignored for forty years, now desperately needed, too bitter to celebrate)
the_trial_of_fen (is she terrorist or savior? both answers correct)

states.boundary_note: Fen crosses the boundary. The Ash Tenders live at the edge of the Ashfield — the brine pool's rim, where the toxic meets the livable. Fen steps PAST the edge. She goes into the Ashfield itself and worships the fear-god directly. The proximity changes her. Not possession — adaptation. Her dreams become the god's dreams. She feels fear that isn't hers — old fear, geological fear, the accumulated dread of centuries of travelers. She can tell the difference between her fear and the god's fear the way an Ash Tender can tell the difference between ash-wind and regular wind. The mark of the boundary: Fen comes back from the Ashfield knowing things she shouldn't know — where the fear is strongest, where the Cough will spread next, what the god is feeling. She's become an Ash Tender by force. The difference: the Ash Tenders learned to live at the edge over generations. Fen crashed through it in a month. The accelerated fermentation: she force-fed the god the way you dump koji into miso and crank the temperature. The result is volatile. The god is growing faster than anyone can track. And Fen, who calculated every risk, didn't calculate what the boundary would do to her.

states.name: The Second Rot
states.condition: Awakening fails to take root + old patterns reassert
states.tension: Kael rebuilt the bridge at the southern crossing three times. The first time, a faction burned it because the route brought competition to their monopoly. The second time, it was neglected until it sagged. The third time, Kael mixed the mortar alone — the other fixers had moved on or given up. The bridge stands. Nobody uses it. The most well-maintained structure in a rotting world, crossed by nobody. That bridge is the Second Rot. The Awakening proved things can be fixed. The Second Rot proves that fixing isn't enough — you also have to maintain, and maintaining requires institutions, and institutions require trust, and trust is what the first Rot destroyed and the Awakening never rebuilt. The old guard didn't return through force. They returned through exhaustion. They said: 'Let us know when you're done.' When the fixers were done — spent, discouraged, fragmented — the old guard walked back into positions they'd never really left. They didn't seize power. They received it from people too tired to hold it. And the Rot is smarter now. It learned from the Awakening. It learned which reforms to allow (harmless ones), which to co-opt (popular ones), and which to crush (threatening ones). The Rot has antibodies against hope.
states.feels_like: Post-revolution restoration. The Bourbons returning to France — not because they won, but because everyone else lost. Reconstruction followed by Jim Crow. The relapse is worse than the disease because the disease now knows how to survive the treatment.

dominant_factions: the_returned_old_guard (wearing new clothes, saying the old words)
disillusioned_fixers (Essa's people, scattered, some bitter, some still trying alone)
the_co_opted (reform initiatives that the old guard absorbed and defanged)
active_gods: the_silence (returned, stronger than before — now it feeds on despair, not just apathy)
the_weave (damaged — connections built during the Awakening severed by the failure)
the_player_god (if present — watching the Awakening fail is the most painful divine experience in the game)
threats: informed_decline (the Rot learned from the Awakening — it has antibodies against hope)
institutional_vacuum (the Awakening destroyed the shadow systems and failed to build replacements)
despair_as_identity (people who hoped and watched hope die are harder to reach than people who never hoped)
missing: innocence (the first Rot had the comfort of not knowing better — the Second Rot knows exactly what it lost)
the_shadow_systems (Breca's smuggling network, the unofficial infrastructure — destroyed by the Awakening, not rebuilt)
anyone who believes trying will work this time

key_figures.kael: Fixer from the Awakening. He repaired the bridge at the southern crossing. He was proud — for the first time in his life, he did something that mattered. The bridge was burned. He rebuilt it. It was neglected. He rebuilt it. The third time, alone, in the rain, mixing mortar nobody asked for. The bridge stands. It will stand for decades. Kael maintains it every spring. Nobody crosses it. He maintains it anyway because the alternative is admitting the Awakening was for nothing. He can't do that. The bridge is his refusal to despair, and it's the saddest structure in the world.
key_figures.essa_aftermath: If Essa survived the Awakening, she's the most haunted person in the Second Rot. She started the cascade. People followed her. Now the cascade died and the people who followed are scattered or broken. She says: 'At least we tried.' She doesn't find this comforting. She finds it the saddest sentence in the language. She still fixes things — small things, in her village, alone. She doesn't inspire anyone anymore. She just fixes things because she doesn't know how to stop.
key_figures.the_old_guard_speaker: Whoever leads the returned old guard. They're not a villain — they're a survivor. They outlasted the Awakening by waiting. They'll outlast the Second Rot by waiting. Their philosophy: 'Systems that endure are systems that don't ask too much of people.' The Awakening asked too much. The old guard asks nothing. Asking nothing is why the Rot works, and it's also why the Rot persists.

states.origin_wound: The Awakening itself. Every failed hope creates scar tissue that makes the next hope harder. The Second Rot isn't the absence of trying — it's the memory of trying and failing. The first Rot was ignorance. The Second Rot is knowledge: specifically, the knowledge that things can be better AND that being better requires sustained effort AND that sustained effort is exactly what the world doesn't have. Kael's bridge is the wound made physical: proof that things can be fixed, proof that fixing isn't enough.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the old guard returned — the Awakening's reformers failed to build institutions)
worship_economy (the Silence feeds on despair, not just apathy — despair is richer food)
thread_connections.sub_thread: kaels_bridge — a man maintaining a bridge nobody crosses. Resolution: either the bridge becomes a symbol that inspires one more try (→Stubborn Spark endgame), or it collapses when Kael dies and nobody replaces him (→Second Rot endgame or The Shrug)
sub_sub_threads: essas_aftermath (the person who started the Awakening living through its failure)
the_rots_antibodies (a system that evolved to survive reform — the most dangerous kind of decline)

states.name: The Early Peace
states.condition: Compromise deepens fast + no major disruptions + player invested heavily
states.tension: Venn is twelve. He was born after the Compromise. He has a friend from the forge quarter and a friend from the southern district and he doesn't understand why his grandfather won't speak to the southern neighbor who brings them bread. 'You don't remember,' his grandfather says. 'Remember what?' Venn asks. His grandfather opens his mouth and closes it. How do you explain a war to a child who's never seen one? How do you explain that the neighbor with the bread was, twenty years ago, part of a culture that blockaded the mountain passes until the forges starved? How do you say that without sounding insane to a child for whom the shared market, the joint festivals, the bilingual school are just... how things are? The Early Peace is this exchange repeated in every household. The older generation carrying hatred they can't explain without sounding unhinged. The younger generation carrying innocence that feels like progress and also feels like forgetting. The peace is real. The peace is also built on economic interdependence — ore flowing south, medicine flowing north, neither side able to afford conflict because conflict disrupts supply chains both sides depend on. The structural peace works. The emotional peace is still performing. Jem, a Bridge Folk translator at the joint festivals, translates toasts to friendship while thinking about his uncle, who was killed in the trade disputes. He performs trust because performing trust is his job, and because he knows that if enough people perform it for long enough, it might become real. He doesn't know if it's working. Nobody does. That's why everyone is watching the exits.
states.feels_like: The Good Friday Agreement's first year. The early EU — economic interdependence as a strategy for making war unthinkable. The peace is structural before it's emotional. The emotions come later, if they come at all. Everyone is careful. Everyone is hopeful. Everyone is exhausted enough that the alternative to peace is worse than the discomfort of trust.

dominant_factions: the_joint_council (newly formed — representatives from both sides, chaired by Bridge Folk mediators)
the_bridge_folk (mainstream now — the mixed-culture generation is the peace's infrastructure)
the_memory_keepers (older generation who carry the Compromise's scars — protective and suspicious)
active_gods: the_weave (strengthening — genuine connection being built for the first time)
the_scale (stabilized by fair trade terms — enforcing agreements that both sides actually agreed to willingly)
the_burn (weakening — peace doesn't feed the fire)
threats: generational_forgetting (the children don't understand why the peace matters because they don't remember what it replaced)
performed_vs_genuine (is the peace real or is everyone acting?)
economic_dependency (the peace survives because trade benefits both sides — what happens when the trade is disrupted?)
missing: emotional_trust (the structure works, the feelings lag behind)
shared_memory (the two sides remember different wars — reconciliation requires agreeing on what happened, and they don't)
forgiveness (nobody has said sorry because both sides believe they were right)

key_figures.venn: Twelve years old. The Early Peace's embodiment. Born after the Compromise. Has friends from both cultures. Speaks both languages because the bilingual school taught him both. Doesn't understand why the old people are angry. His innocence is the peace's greatest achievement and its greatest vulnerability — the achievement because it proves the next generation can grow up without hatred, the vulnerability because innocence doesn't know how to defend itself if the hatred comes back.
key_figures.jem: Bridge Folk translator. Thirty-four. Works the joint festivals, the trade negotiations, the cultural exchanges. His job is to make two peoples understand each other. He's excellent at it. His uncle was killed in the trade disputes — a blockade casualty, starved when the passes closed. Jem translates toasts to friendship while carrying this knowledge. He doesn't hate the southerners. He doesn't trust them either. He performs trust as a professional discipline. The question he can't answer: is the performance becoming real, or is he just getting better at acting?
key_figures.venns_grandfather: Former forge-quarter elder. Survived the blockade. Lost a brother. Carries the Compromise's oldest wound. He can't explain to Venn why the southern neighbor's bread makes his hands shake. The explanation requires a context Venn has never experienced, and providing the context means either traumatizing the child or sounding deranged. So he says nothing. And his silence becomes the gap that the next generation grows into — free but uninformed, innocent but unarmored.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (economic interdependence as peace-maintenance)
worship_economy (the Weave strengthening through genuine connection)
thread_connections.sub_thread: venns_innocence — a child's inability to understand the old hatred. Resolution: either the innocence survives and becomes the Long Peace's foundation, or the old wounds resurface and innocence is the first casualty
sub_sub_threads: jems_performance (performed trust → real trust?)
the_grandfathers_silence (trauma that can't be transmitted becomes trauma that recurs)

states.name: The Betrayal
states.condition: One faction secretly undermines the compromise to protect something the compromise was eroding
states.tension: Torren sealed the forges. He claimed a structural emergency — a crack in the lower gallery, too dangerous for visitors. The southern scholars were sent home early. Then he sent a runner to the True Forge exiles in the deep mountains: come back, bring the old knowledge, consolidate what we have before it all leaks. This violated the Compromise. Torren knows it violated the Compromise. He did it because the southern scholars were getting too close. The three shared techniques were supposed to be the limit, but 'limited observation' is a one-way door — the scholars had been reverse-engineering the deeper methods from what they'd seen, publishing papers in the Senate archives that came within a generation of cracking the sacred seventh fold. The Compromise's terms were naive. Observation leads to understanding leads to replication. Torren didn't betray the Compromise out of resentment — he'd been keeping his score for years, yes, but the score isn't what moved him. What moved him was finding a southern metallurgy paper that described the seventh fold's heat signature with ninety percent accuracy. One more observation visit and they'd have it. And once they had it, there would be nothing left that made the forge the forge. The betrayal was also an act of preservation. When Renn discovers what Torren did — and she will, because the southern scholars will notice the emergency was fabricated — the damage isn't the sealed forges. The damage is trust. The entire framework of cooperation was built on the assumption that agreements would be honored. One betrayal and the assumption is gone. And the assumption was load-bearing.
states.feels_like: A betrayal that both sides saw coming and neither admits they expected. Torren's defense is real: the techniques were leaking, the Compromise's terms couldn't protect what they promised to protect. Renn's devastation is also real: if you can't trust the agreements, you can't have agreements. The lie was comfortable. The truth is that the Compromise was always a bet that trust would outrun erosion, and the erosion won.

dominant_factions: the_forge_council (exposed — Torren's action was unauthorized but reflects what many felt)
the_solmari_senate (betrayed — Renn's trust framework destroyed)
the_joint_council (paralyzed — the institution designed for cooperation facing its first real test)
the_true_forge (returning from exile — Torren's invitation bringing the old knowledge back to the mountain)
active_gods: the_weave (strained to breaking — the connection between north and south thinning to a thread)
the_burn (strengthened — the forge identity reasserting itself through crisis)
the_scale (adjudicating — was Torren's action a violation? the Compromise said 'limited observation,' and the scholars were exceeding the limit. The betrayal might be mutual)
threats: trust_collapse (the assumption that made cooperation possible is gone)
escalation (if one side cheated, the other side's restraint becomes foolishness — the logic of preemptive action)
the_scale_ruling (if the Scale determines both sides violated terms, the Compromise was never real)

key_figures.torren: The Betrayer. But also the Protector. Forge Council elder who kept score for years and finally acted when the score showed the forge's identity was about to reach zero. He sealed the forges to stop the observation visits. He invited the True Forge back. He knows the Compromise is dead. He grieves it — genuinely. He also knows that the Compromise was killing the thing it promised to protect, one observation visit at a time. His defense: 'I broke the agreement because the agreement was already broken. The terms said limited observation. They were observing everything. I'm the one who noticed. I'm the one who acted. Call me the betrayer if you want. I'm the one who saved the seventh fold.'
key_figures.renn: The Betrayed. Senate negotiator who built her career on the Compromise. She trusted Torren — not personally, but structurally. She trusted the framework. When the fabricated emergency comes to light, her devastation isn't about the forges. It's about the discovery that trust was a one-sided investment. She gave, genuinely, believing the framework would hold. It didn't. Her response will determine whether the Betrayal leads to the Old Wounds (she processes the grief and tries to rebuild) or Mutual Destruction (she retaliates, and the retaliation logic consumes everything).

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Compromise destroyed — what replaces it?)
worship_economy (the Weave strained to breaking — the Scale forced to adjudicate a betrayal that might be mutual)
thread_connections.sub_thread: torren_and_renn — the betrayer who had a point and the betrayed who trusted the framework. Resolution: Renn processes it (→Old Wounds, rebuilding on scar tissue), or Renn retaliates (→Mutual Destruction, the logic of preemptive action)
sub_sub_threads: the_scale_ruling (was the betrayal mutual? if both sides exceeded terms, who's the villain?)
the_true_forge_return (the exiles coming home — do they heal the fracture or widen it?)

states.name: The Regulated Peace
states.condition: Factions impose regulations on trade to prevent another war
states.tension: The Accords stopped the Merchant Wars. Seventeen clauses, negotiated in blood, signed by exhausted factions who'd rather have imperfect peace than continued war. The Accords work. Nobody is starving. Nobody is being blockaded. Nobody is hiring proxy armies. And nobody can fix the injustices the Accords locked into place, because fixing them means reopening the Accords, and reopening the Accords risks the framework that keeps everyone alive. The regulations froze the war's inequities in place — they stopped the fighting without addressing why the fighting started. The surface is peaceful. Underneath, the original grievances are compressed by regulation into smaller and smaller spaces, building pressure that has nowhere to go.
states.feels_like: The Concert of Europe after the Congress of Vienna. A system designed to prevent catastrophe that succeeds by suppressing everything — including legitimate reform. The system confuses 'managing difference' with 'resolving difference.' It works until it doesn't, and when it breaks, a century of compressed tension releases at once.

dominant_factions: the_fair_market_board (joint regulatory body — representatives from every faction)
the_accord_keepers (legal specialists who interpret the seventeen clauses)
remnant_merchant_families (former Consortium — still have preferential access via loopholes)
subordinate_factions: the_forge_council (represented on the Board, limited to regulated output quotas)
the_solmari_senate (represented on the Board, provides the legal scholars)
bridge_folk_traders (newest faction on the Board, smallest voice, most affected by loopholes)
delta_syndicate (logistics backbone — the Brackish run the routes but don't set the rates)
active_gods: the_scale (enforcing the Accords' definition of 'fair' — honestly enforcing a flawed standard)
the_weave (stable but thin — connection maintained by regulation, not genuine trust)
the_silence (growing slowly in the gap between what people say and what they feel)
threats: frozen_injustice (loopholes nobody will fix because fixing means reopening)
generational_forgetting (the children of peace don't understand what the Accords prevent)
pressure_buildup (suppressed grievances don't disappear — they compress)
missing: genuine resolution (the Accords manage the conflict, they don't resolve it)
innovation (anything new threatens the balance)
honest accounting (everyone knows the Accords contain injustices — nobody will say so publicly)

key_figures.maret: The Architect. Wrote the Accords after losing both sons in the Merchant Wars — one starved in a trade embargo, one killed fighting for a merchant guild that was buying soldiers cheaper than grain. The first clause reads: 'No price shall be set that requires a person to choose between feeding their family and keeping their word.' That clause is still there. So are the loopholes she put in to get the Consortium families to sign. She knows the Accords are imperfect. She designed them to be imperfect because a perfect agreement that nobody signs saves nobody. Now she's very old, sitting on the Board as honorary chair, watching the peace she built calcify into something she half-recognizes. When Teven comes to her with his grievance, she agrees with him and refuses to help him. Not because she's a coward — because she buried her sons and she won't risk burying anyone else's.
key_figures.teven: Bridge Folk trader. Twenty-four. Discovered that Accord Seven — 'equal access to trade routes' — contains a loophole granting former Consortium families preferential route access 'for administrative continuity.' This was the Consortium's condition for signing. The result: Bridge Folk and Delta Syndicate traders use secondary routes — longer, more expensive, less safe. Three Bridge Folk died on the southern detour last winter. Teven filed a formal grievance with the Fair Market Board. The Board acknowledged the loophole. The Board said: changing it requires reopening the Accords. Reopening the Accords risks the framework. The loophole stays. Teven went to Maret. Maret said no. Teven is the question the Regulated Peace can't answer: what do you do when the system that keeps the peace IS the injustice?
key_figures.davan: Third-generation Fair Market Board inspector. Enforces the Accords. Good at his job. Secretly believes three of the seventeen clauses are outdated — the lending cap prevents infrastructure investment, the ore pricing formula doesn't account for depleted deposits, the route access loophole is indefensible. He won't say this because he's read the Board's archives. He's seen the records of the Merchant Wars — the starved children, the burned orchards. He understands why the Accords exist. He enforces them because the alternative is what's in those archives. But every time he shuts down a Bridge Folk trader for operating on a preferential route, he knows the system he's protecting is the system that's hurting them.

internal_divisions.the_architects: The surviving founders who wrote the Accords. They remember what the regulations prevent. They hold the line — not out of conviction anymore, but out of fear. The fear is reasonable.
internal_divisions.the_reformers: Younger faction members who want targeted changes. Not abolition — reform. They respect the intent but question specific clauses. Teven is their figurehead, though he didn't ask to be.
internal_divisions.the_complacents: The majority. People who've internalized the Accords as natural law. They don't argue for or against. They don't think about them. This is the most dangerous faction — not because they'll break the peace, but because they won't defend it either. When the Accords are finally challenged, the Complacents won't fight because they've forgotten what fighting means.
internal_divisions.the_beneficiaries: Former Consortium families who retained preferential access. They didn't write the loopholes — Maret did, to get their signatures. But they benefit from them, and benefit creates attachment, and attachment creates defense. They're not villains. They're people who inherited an advantage and rationalized keeping it.

states.the_scale_problem: The Scale (commerce god) has evolved. In the Syndicate Age, it was being gamed — the Consortium rewrote 'fair' in contract language. In the Regulated Peace, the Scale enforces the Accords honestly. The problem is that the Accords themselves contain frozen injustices. The Scale enforces a deal made under duress and calls it fairness because the deal is what 'fair' means in the Accords' framework. A god honestly enforcing a flawed definition of fairness. Which is worse — a tricked god or an honest god serving a broken standard?
states.origin_wound: The Accords were a compromise, not a solution. Every clause represents a concession that some faction made while exhausted, desperate, or coerced by the threat of continued war. The loopholes aren't bugs — they're the price of getting everyone to sign. Maret knew this when she wrote them. She chose an unjust peace over a just war. That choice saved thousands of lives and froze dozens of injustices into the foundation of the world. The origin wound is: the peace was never fair. It was just better than dying.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Accords ARE the frozen balance)
worship_economy (the Scale enforces a flawed standard)
thread_connections.sub_thread: teven_grievance — the loophole, the Board's refusal, the visit to Maret, the question the system can't answer
sub_sub_threads: the_scale_honest_enforcement (divine enforcement of human compromise)
davan_archives (an enforcer who reads the records and obeys anyway)
thread_connections.resolves_into: endgame_genuine_peace if the Accords evolve; endgame_complacent_fall if the complacents win by inaction

states.name: The First Exodus
states.condition: Young smiths leave before the walls fully crack
states.tension: Nol was first. Then her workshop-mate Essen. Then three apprentices from the lower forge who heard about the trees. Within a year, forty smiths had gone through the crack — each carrying tools, each carrying techniques Thornwall considered sacred, each making the walls less necessary by making Thornwall less unique. Master Hael counts. He stands at the gap some mornings and counts the footprints in the dust — fresh ones, heading out. Never heading in. He doesn't seal the crack. He doesn't follow. He counts. The old master Darren got what he feared most: not a dramatic collapse but a slow bleed. The best leave first because the best can survive anywhere. What remains is competent but not brilliant, loyal but not innovative. Thornwall is becoming a museum of itself — the forms preserved, the fire dimming, the workshops half-empty, the Iron Oath spoken to smaller and smaller classes.
states.feels_like: Brain drain. East Germany's slow leak before the wall fell. A monastery losing its novices. The institution doesn't die — it thins, the way the Final Decline thins the world. Not catastrophe. Departure.

dominant_factions: the_remaining_council (governing a shrinking population — authority over fewer and fewer people)
the_leavers_trail (forty smiths scattered across the continent — not a faction, a diaspora)
the_foot_grown (The Foot at the mountain's base, now larger than Thornwall's forge quarter — the defectors' first stop)
active_gods: the_burn (dimming inside, scattering outside — the forge-fire going to forty separate hearths)
the_silence (filling empty workshops — the sound of hammering replaced by quiet)
threats: the_thinning (Thornwall becoming a museum — forms without fire)
technique_dispersal (the sacred knowledge is now in forty different hands, in forty different places, being adapted to forty different needs)
haels_count (the master who stands at the gap and counts — when does counting become mourning?)

key_figures.nol_gone: Nol, who was seventeen when she squeezed through the crack, is twenty-three now and forging in a delta workshop.
