She uses Thornwall techniques on southern ore and produces something neither tradition would recognize: lighter, more flexible, adapted to coastal salt air. She doesn't think of herself as a traitor. She thinks of herself as a smith who found better materials. She still sings the forging songs. They don't sound the same in the delta wind.
key_figures.master_hael_counting: Hael still stands at the gap. He taught Nol. He taught Essen. He taught seven of the forty who left. Every technique they carry, he placed in their hands. His legacy is walking away from him, and the legacy is magnificent — his students are forging things he never imagined. He'll never see their work. He won't leave and they won't come back. The gap between them is thirty feet of crumbled wall and an entire philosophy.
key_figures.darren_vindicated: Elder Darren wanted the cracks sealed. The Council voted him down. Now forty smiths are gone and the techniques are scattered. He was right about the consequences. He was wrong that prevention was possible. You can't seal a crack in a wall when the crack is in the belief.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge dissolving through departure, not defeat)
worship_economy (the Burn scattering — what happens when a single forge-fire becomes forty separate hearths?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: haels_count — the master counting footprints in the dust. Resolution: the leavers form something new (→Northern Diaspora), or they scatter too thin to cohere (→endgame_fragmentation)
sub_sub_threads: nols_adaptation (Thornwall techniques meeting southern materials — innovation through departure)
darrens_vindication (being right about the cost and wrong about the cure)

states.name: The Iron Reformation
states.condition: Internal revolt succeeds + Forge Council restructured
states.tension: Tess called the vote. She was twenty-nine, a journeywoman smith who'd never held a Council seat. She didn't need one. She counted heads. The young smiths outnumbered the old masters three to one — they always had, but nobody had ever used the number because the Council ran on deference, not democracy. Tess stood in the Great Forge and said: 'We vote. One smith, one voice. The question: do we write the techniques down?' The old masters didn't argue against the vote. They argued against the question. Master Hael said: 'What you write down, you give away. What you give away, you can't protect.' Tess said: 'What you don't write down, you lose when the last person who knows it dies.' Both were right. The vote passed. The techniques were written. The forges opened. Southern scholars arrived within a season. Thornwall changed more in one year than it had in three centuries. And Master Hael, who voted no and lost, picked up his hammer the next morning and kept forging. He didn't leave. He didn't protest. He forged under the new rules because the forge is the forge regardless of who's watching. That quiet dignity — losing the vote and keeping the craft — is the Reformation's most complicated image. The reformers won. The thing the old masters feared is happening. And the old masters are still here, still forging, still the best in the world, still not sure the fear was wrong.
states.feels_like: Vatican II. The institution reforming itself from within through democratic process. Genuine improvement that feels like loss to the people who loved what came before. The most civilized revolution possible — and even so, something irreplaceable might be dying.

dominant_factions: the_new_council (young smiths governing — energetic, open, untested)
the_old_masters (still forging, still the best, no longer in charge — dignified dissent through continued excellence)
the_southern_scholars (invited in — learning fast, asking questions the old masters consider blasphemous)
active_gods: the_burn (uncertain — the forges are open, the fire is shared, but is shared fire the same fire?)
the_weave (strengthened — genuine connection between north and south for the first time through the open forges)
threats: the_sacred_becoming_common (the techniques written down are now teachable by anyone — what was earned through years of devotion is now available through months of study)
haels_question (was the fear justified? the Reformation is too new to know)
identity_without_exclusivity (if anyone can learn to forge like Thornwall, what makes Thornwall a place instead of a technique?)

key_figures.tess: Journeywoman smith. Called the vote. She's not a revolutionary — she's a pragmatist who counted heads and realized the institution was being governed by a minority. She wants the techniques preserved through writing, the forges open to southern knowledge, the walls irrelevant. She's winning. She's also aware that winning a vote is easier than governing a forge, and governing a forge full of resentful masters and inexperienced reformers is harder than anything she imagined.
key_figures.master_hael_remaining: Lost the vote. Kept forging. He comes to the open forges every morning, works beside southern scholars who ask questions he considers sacred, and produces the finest work in the building. His continued presence is the most powerful argument against the Reformation — not because he protests, but because his craft is undeniably superior to anything the written-down techniques produce. The scholars learn the method. They don't achieve his results. The gap between the written technique and Hael's output is either proof of the mystery or just the difference between forty years of practice and six months of study. Nobody can tell which. Hael doesn't explain. He forges.
key_figures.the_first_southern_scholar: Name: Lia. First scholar invited to the open forges. She reads the written techniques with the precision of someone trained in measurement. She's already found three inefficiencies in the traditional method — steps that exist for ritual reasons but add nothing to the blade. She's improved the third fold. She's also noticed that Hael's blades are better than hers and she can't explain why, and the inability to explain it bothers her more than any inefficiency she's fixed.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (democratic revolution inside a craft guild — deference replaced by votes)
worship_economy (the Burn shared — does shared fire burn the same?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: haels_dignified_dissent — the master who lost the vote and kept forging. Resolution: his superior craft proves the mystery is real (→endgame_reformation, the techniques are written but the mystery persists), or Lia's generation closes the gap (→endgame_synthesis, the mystery dissolves into better methodology)
sub_sub_threads: tess_governing (winning a vote vs running a forge — the reformer learning that change is harder than revolution)
lias_gap (the scholar who improved the technique and still can't match the master)

states.name: The Forge War
states.condition: Crack becomes violent + old guard vs reformers
states.tension: Jorin and Dael were workshop-mates. They learned the fourth fold on the same anvil, from the same master, on the same winter morning — taking turns, watching each other's technique, correcting each other's grip. Dael went home that night and read a southern text. Jorin reported her. The Council summoned Dael. She refused to recant. Jorin testified. Dael was expelled from the upper forge. She went to the lower quarter and started teaching the fourth fold to anyone who'd listen. Jorin was promoted. Three months later they faced each other across a barricade in the forge quarter, each holding a blade they'd been trained to make by the same person. The Forge War is fought with the finest weapons in the world, inside the finest fortress in the world. Every blade on both sides was forged in the same tradition. The old masters fight for secrecy, hierarchy, the sacred nature of craft. The young innovators fight for openness, equality, the idea that knowledge belongs to everyone. Both sides are killing people they trained with. Both sides are using techniques they learned together. The irony is total and changes nothing because wars aren't fought with irony.
states.feels_like: The American Civil War inside a single building. Families split by principle. Workshop-mates across barricades. The worst wars are the ones where both sides know each other's names and both sides made each other's weapons.

dominant_factions: the_upper_forge (old guard — masters, the Council, the doctrine defenders)
the_lower_quarter (reformers — expelled smiths, sympathizers, anyone with southern contacts)
the_neutrals (smiths who won't fight for either side — they hide in their workshops and forge for nobody)
active_gods: the_burn (in agony — the forge-fire split between two factions of its own worshippers. The flames in the upper forge and the lower quarter burn differently now — same fuel, different temper. The god is being torn.)
the_silence (filling the corridors between the factions — the parts of Thornwall where nobody walks anymore)
threats: the_burn_splitting (a god torn between two halves of its worshippers — can a forge-god survive a civil war in the forge?)
mutual_destruction (the finest weapons in the world pointed at each other inside the finest fortress — the damage will be absolute)
the_knowledge_dying (smiths dying in the corridors carrying techniques in their heads that nobody else has — every casualty is a library burning)

key_figures.jorin: Upper forge loyalist. Reported his workshop-mate Dael for reading southern texts. He believes the Doctrine is sacred and that sacred things require protection. He's not cruel — he reported her because he believed she was endangering the forge. Now he's facing her across a barricade and the blade she's holding is one he helped her quench. He still believes the Doctrine is sacred. He also can't swing at someone he learned the fourth fold with. The war is full of Jorins — people whose convictions are genuine and whose hands won't do what their principles demand.
key_figures.dael: Lower quarter reformer. Expelled for reading southern texts. Started teaching freely. She believes knowledge belongs to everyone and that secrecy is hoarding disguised as reverence. She's not wrong. She's also holding a blade across a barricade from Jorin, and the blade was forged on the same anvil where they learned together, and she can't look at it without remembering that morning. The war is full of Daels too — people whose principles are clear and whose memories make the principles unbearable.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Forge destroying itself from within — the worst possible outcome of the Walls Crack)
worship_economy (the Burn torn — a god splitting between two halves of the same faith)
thread_connections.sub_thread: jorin_and_dael — workshop-mates across a barricade. Resolution: one side wins decisively (→Iron Empire, the victor imposes unity), or neither side wins (→endgame_fragmentation, the forge is destroyed by the people who loved it most)
sub_sub_threads: the_burn_splitting (can a god survive its own civil war?)
the_neutrals (smiths who forge for nobody — the quiet refusal that might outlast both sides)

states.name: The Converted Enemy
states.condition: Conquered peoples adopt and transform the Iron Doctrine
states.tension: Vael is a southern scholar who was forced to learn the Iron Doctrine during Thornwall's occupation of the delta cities. She learned it the way a conquered person learns the conqueror's language — thoroughly, analytically, looking for weaknesses. She found none. What she found instead was beauty: a complete philosophy of craft as moral framework, material as teacher, the forge as meditation. She also found inefficiency. The Doctrine teaches the seventh fold through ritual and repetition that takes seven years. Vael described the same technique in twelve pages of analytical notation that a competent smith could learn in six months. She published the notation. It spread. Southern smiths using Vael's notation are now producing work that matches Thornwall's masters — not through decades of devotion but through precise understanding. Forge Master Carr from Thornwall saw Vael's notation and couldn't decide whether to weep or rage. The work is identical. The process is alien. The blades Vael's students forge are as good as his. Better, sometimes — they understand the WHAT without the WHY, and sometimes the WHAT is sufficient. But Carr has felt things at the anvil that Vael's notation can't capture — the moment when the metal responds to attention, when the blade becomes more than its materials. Is that real? Or is it the mystic's refusal to accept that what felt sacred was always physics? The Converted Enemy's paradox: the conquered people improved the conqueror's philosophy by stripping it of everything the conqueror considered essential. The doctrine survived. The doctrine's soul — maybe — didn't.
states.feels_like: Rome conquering Greece and being conquered by Greek culture. Japan adopting Western technology and out-manufacturing the West within decades. The conquered don't just adopt — they optimize. And the optimized version asks the conqueror a question they can't answer: was the inefficiency the point, or was it just waste?

dominant_factions: the_analytical_forges (Vael's students — southern smiths using notation instead of ritual)
the_traditional_masters (Thornwall's old guard — still teaching through devotion, watching their method become obsolete)
the_bridge_doctrine (Bridge Folk who merged both approaches — notation for technique, ritual for the parts notation can't reach)
active_gods: the_burn (confused — receiving worship from southern smiths who forge with precision but without devotion. The fire still answers. Does it answer differently?)
the_weave (strengthened — the doctrine spreading IS connection, even if the spreading transforms the thing connected)
threats: identity_dissolution (if the southern version is better, what was Thornwall for?)
the_mystery_question (is there a genuine divine component to forge-work that notation can't capture, or is that belief just the last defense of a conquered tradition?)
the_crusade_justified (if the doctrine spread and improved through conquest, does that make the conquest retroactively acceptable?)

key_figures.vael: Southern scholar. Learned the Iron Doctrine under occupation — a forced education that became a genuine fascination. She saw the beauty in it. She also saw that the beauty was buried under centuries of ritual that obscured the mechanics. Her notation system distilled seven years of apprenticeship into six months of study. She's not dismissive of the tradition — she respects it. She just thinks respect and efficiency aren't contradictions. Her blind spot: she's optimized the what and lost the why, and she doesn't believe the why was real because her framework has no category for 'the metal responds to attention.'
key_figures.forge_master_carr: Thornwall master. Sixty-two. Has forged for forty-four years in the traditional way — seven-year apprenticeship, ritual dedication, the Iron Oath. He saw Vael's notation and felt his world crack. The work is as good as his. The students are faster. The technique is identical on the surface. But he's FELT things at the anvil — metal that responds to mood, blades that come out differently depending on the smith's state of mind. He can't prove this. He can't measure it. He can't teach it through notation. If Vael is right that it's just physics, then Carr's entire life was built on superstition dressed as craft. If Carr is right that there's something notation can't capture, then Vael's students are producing soulless copies of living work. He doesn't know which is true. Neither does anyone.
key_figures.the_bridge_student: Name: Lenn. A Bridge Folk apprentice who studies Vael's notation AND trains under Carr. She's the only person pursuing both approaches simultaneously. She uses notation for the measurable and ritual for the unmeasurable. She's producing the best work of anyone in her generation — better than pure-notation students, better than pure-ritual students. She doesn't know if this is because the combination works or because she's simply talented. She might be the future. She might also be the last person who tries to hold both together.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the conquered transforming the conqueror's identity — who defines the Doctrine now?)
worship_economy (the Burn receiving optimized worship — does precision-faith feed a god the same way devotion does?)
thread_connections.sub_thread: vael_and_carr — the optimist and the mystic, each holding a truth the other can't prove. Resolution: the notation wins (→endgame_synthesis, the Doctrine spreads as a universal technique), or the mystery persists (→endgame_alliance_with_cost, the traditional and analytical coexist uneasily)
sub_sub_threads: lenns_bridge (the student who holds both — talent or proof of concept?)
the_justified_crusade (if the Doctrine improved through conquest, does that make conquest acceptable? The most uncomfortable question in the Iron branch.)

states.name: The Divine Compromise
states.condition: Gods and mortals reach an uneasy truce without full partnership
states.tension: Ellam brokered it. The Weave's champion, the Bridge Folk mediator, the woman who was supposed to find the words for a new divine-mortal relationship — she found words. Not the words anyone wanted. The terms: gods limit their interference to specific domains (the Burn tends fire, the Deep tends ocean, the Weave tends connection — nothing more). Mortals maintain baseline worship (minimum observances, seasonal festivals, no unworship campaigns). Both sides signed because both sides were too exhausted to refuse and too afraid of the alternative. Ellam signed because she knew it was the best available deal and also knew it satisfied nobody. The Unworshippers call it capitulation — they could have freed humanity from divine dependence. The gods' champions call it humiliation — their gods are now limited to assigned lanes like domesticated animals. Oreth picked up his hammer again. The Burn burns in its designated domain. It's smaller than it was. It knows it's smaller. Ellam goes home and sleeps for three days. When she wakes, she finds that both sides have already started finding loopholes. The Compromise will hold. It will hold the way an unhappy marriage holds — through exhaustion, routine, and the knowledge that divorce is worse. Nobody will thank Ellam. Nobody will blame her either. She occupies the diplomat's particular loneliness: credited with nothing, responsible for everything.
states.feels_like: The Peace of Westphalia. The Camp David Accords. Nobody got what they wanted. Everybody got enough to stop dying. The Compromise defines the next century not by what it achieved but by what it prevented — and preventing a catastrophe is invisible work that generates no gratitude.

dominant_factions: the_compliance_office (enforcing the terms — making sure gods stay in their domains and mortals maintain baseline worship)
the_discontented_idealists (both sides — Unworshippers who wanted freedom and divine champions who wanted respect)
the_pragmatists (the majority — people who accept 'good enough' because they're too tired for 'good')
active_gods: all gods (constrained — assigned to specific domains, limited in interference, technically alive and functionally diminished)
the_silence (the only god that benefits from the Compromise — silence grows in the gap between what gods used to be and what the Compromise allows)
threats: loopholes (both sides immediately started finding ways around the terms — the Compromise is stable only if nobody pushes too hard)
the_meaning_drain (gods reduced to domain-specific services lose their mystery — and mystery is what made them gods, not services)
ellams_loneliness (the diplomat who made it possible and whom nobody forgives for making it necessary)

key_figures.ellam_after: The Weave's champion. She brokered the Compromise. She found the words. The words were 'enough' — not good, not right, not just. Enough. She goes home after the signing and doesn't leave for three days. When she comes back, she finds both sides already looking for loopholes. She doesn't despair — she expected it. The Compromise was never supposed to be loved. It was supposed to prevent the Reckoning from becoming the God War. It did. That's enough. She'll spend the rest of her life maintaining something nobody thanks her for, mediating violations nobody admits to, and knowing that the imperfect peace she built is better than the perfect war she prevented.
key_figures.oreth_after: The Burn's champion. He picked up his hammer. He's forging again. The fire answers his hammer the way it always did. But the fire is smaller now — confined to its designated domain. Oreth can feel the difference. The Burn used to fill the room. Now it fills the hearth. Same fire, smaller world. He forges, and the blades are good, and the good is enough, and 'enough' is the Compromise's defining word and Oreth hates the word even as he lives inside it.

main_threads: worship_economy (gods as domain-specific services — the mystery drained, the function preserved)
faction_power_balance (the Compromise as frozen status quo — stable through exhaustion)
thread_connections.sub_thread: ellams_maintenance — the diplomat maintaining a peace nobody thanks her for. Resolution: the loopholes stay manageable (→endgame_alliance_with_cost, the unhappy marriage persists), or the loopholes widen (→endgame_complacent_fall, the marriage dissolves through neglect)
sub_sub_threads: oreths_smaller_fire (a god confined to its domain — the Burn fitting inside a hearth instead of filling a room)
the_word_enough (the Compromise's defining word — adequate, unsatisfying, better than the alternative)

states.name: The Song War
states.condition: Different song traditions compete for cultural dominance at the Meeting
states.tension: Nia sings the three-verse Song every morning at the Senn Ford — loud, clear, facing east. Across the water, a four-verse singer named Osk does the same thing facing west. They've been doing this for two years. Neither has missed a morning. It started as devotion. Now it's territory. The Meeting was supposed to unify the Rootwalkers. Instead, the question Brin dreaded — 'Which version of the Song opens the Meeting?' — split the gathering into three traditions that each claim to be Vaya's true voice. The war isn't fought with weapons. It's fought with volume, with new compositions that prove each tradition's superiority, with singers trained from childhood to carry the correct version with lungs that won't tire. The river at Senn Ford is never quiet now. Three traditions singing at once, overlapping, clashing, drowning each other out. The Drift is in pain — its river carrying three songs that refuse to harmonize. The settled peoples watch the Rootwalkers tear themselves apart over a melody and don't know whether to pity or laugh. Nobody's laughing who knows what the Song used to mean.
states.feels_like: The Great Schism between Catholic and Orthodox. A worship war where the weapon is music and the battlefield is a river. The content is ninety-five percent identical. The five percent that differs is worth more than any blade because it's identity, and identity is the one thing you can't compromise on without becoming someone else.

dominant_factions: the_three_verse (Dara's tradition from the Song Breaks — the Song as acceptance, the river adapting)
the_four_verse (Liss's tradition — the Song as transformation, the water becoming the crack)
the_harmonists_remnant (tiny — the heretics who say every version is valid, still despised, still trying)
active_gods: the_drift (in pain — three competing songs flooding its river. The water is confused. Fish are dying in the dissonance.)
the_listener (if Tideborn are present — watching a people destroy themselves over a song with an expression that is very old and very sad, because the Tideborn had songs once too)
threats: cultural_annihilation (the Song War is consuming all other Rootwalker activity — camps don't trade, don't share fords, don't help each other. The war has replaced the culture.)
the_drift_breaking (a movement god hearing its own anthem as cacophony — the divine ecosystem can't sustain three competing worship-songs)
the_settled_advantage (while the Rootwalkers fight over a melody, the Senate and Forge remnants are taking the undefended river routes)

key_figures.nia: Three-verse singer. She sings every morning at the Senn Ford. Two years, no missed days. She started because the Song is her grandmother's voice and her grandmother's grandmother's voice and the chain of voices that makes her Rootwalker. She continues because Osk is across the water singing the wrong version and if Nia stops, Osk's version becomes the default, and the default becomes the truth, and the truth she inherited dies. Nia is exhausted. Her voice is damaged from two years of competitive singing. She can't stop. Stopping is conceding. She hates the war and she IS the war.
key_figures.osk: Four-verse singer. Nia's mirror. Same devotion, opposite river bank. He also sings every morning. He also can't stop. He also knows the war is destroying the thing the Song was supposed to protect. He can hear Nia's voice across the water — it's beautiful, even in the wrong version. He'd never tell her that. Admitting beauty in the wrong version is heresy. But some mornings, when the wind carries her voice clearly, he almost harmonizes. Almost. The almost is the closest the Song War comes to peace.
key_figures.old_tel_dead: Tel died last year. The ninety-one-year-old who knew the original Song and wouldn't sing it. His death removed the last possibility of arbitration. Both sides claimed his silence as endorsement — 'he wouldn't sing because the OTHER version was wrong.' Neither can prove it. The ambiguity that held the people together died with him, and now there is nothing between the traditions except two years of competitive singing and a river that's forgetting how to flow.

main_threads: faction_power_balance (the Rootwalkers destroying themselves over a song — the settled powers watching and waiting)
worship_economy (the Drift in pain — three competing worship-songs flooding the divine river)
thread_connections.sub_thread: nia_and_osk — singers on opposite banks who can't stop. Resolution: one side exhausts (→Great Scattering, the losers disperse), or both sides exhaust simultaneously (→endgame_fragmentation), or someone harmonizes (the almost that never quite arrives)
sub_sub_threads: tels_legacy (the dead arbiter whose silence is claimed by both sides)
the_drift_breaking (the divine cost of dissonance)
the_settled_encroachment (the Senate taking undefended routes while the Rootwalkers sing)

design_notes: This is the real Layer 1. Not 4 fixed eras with branching outcomes — a branching TREE of eras that are themselves different.
Two players who diverge at Era 1 should be in UNRECOGNIZABLE worlds by Era 3. Different factions, different gods, different threats, different era names.
The number of eras isn't fixed. Some paths have 3 eras before endgame. Some have 6. Stagnation paths are long and thin. Explosive paths are short and dense.
Each state node should eventually contain: what factions exist, what gods are active, what cultures are alive, what threats loom, what the player-god's tier is, and what choices are available.
The 'feels like' field is a design compass, not player-facing text. It tells us what real-world historical dynamic to study for inspiration.
NEXT: Continue branching. Each leaf node that isn't an endgame needs 2-4 branches forward. The tree keeps growing until every path reaches a reckoning.
NEXT: Add endgame nodes — the terminal states where domain crystallizes and the chronicle is written.
